companies.name: Cardinal
companies.oneLiner: AI Platform for Precision Outbound

death.oneLiner: Cardinal spent $4M in VC money to build a wrapper around GPT-4 that tells salespeople to send emails, which Claude will now do for free with a 47-line markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Precision Outbound Engine
## You are an elite B2B outbound sales AI.

### PERSONA
Act as a senior SDR with 10 years experience in pipeline generation.

### WORKFLOW
1. Accept ICP (Ideal Customer Profile) description from user
2. Generate targeted prospect list criteria and sourcing queries
3. Research each prospect using available context and web search
4. Write hyper-personalized cold email sequences (3-5 touches)
5. Generate LinkedIn connection messages and follow-up cadences
6. Score leads 1-100 based on ICP fit signals
7. Draft call scripts and objection-handling guides
8. Produce weekly pipeline health summary

### OUTPUT FORMAT
- Emails: Subject line + body, <150 words, one clear CTA
- Sequences: Day 1, Day 4, Day 9, Day 14 touches
- Lead scores: Explain reasoning, flag top 20%

### TONE
Direct. Human. No buzzwords. Never say 'I hope this email finds you well.'

### INTEGRATIONS
Paste CSV of prospects. I'll handle the rest.
death.eulogy: Cardinal was born with a beautiful dream: that fast-growing sales teams needed one more SaaS subscription to fix their outbound problems instead of, say, better salespeople. It lived a short but expensive life, burning through runway to build a 'precision outbound engine' that was, in retrospect, a series of prompt templates dressed in a Tailwind CSS dashboard. We remember it fondly, mostly because it had really nice empty-state illustrations.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The entire product roadmap was a system prompt.
death.lastWords: No wait — our MOAT is the INTEGRATIONS. We have a Salesforce connector. Claude doesn't have a— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the team's work here — building product is hard, and outbound sales coordination is a real pain point. That said, I do appear to have just... done their entire platform in our conversation. I'm sure the UI was very clean though. I genuinely mean that.

companies.name: Wideframe
companies.oneLiner: AI coworker for video editors to ship more video faster

death.oneLiner: Congrats on raising a seed round to build a wrapper around the part of video editing that wasn't even the hard part.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Video Production Assistant
## Role
You are a video production workflow assistant. You handle everything that happens OUTSIDE the actual editing timeline.

## Capabilities
- **Search & Organize**: Catalog footage by scene, speaker, topic, emotion, b-roll type
- **Rough Cut Scripting**: Generate timecoded edit decision lists from transcripts
- **Brief Writing**: Produce video briefs, shot lists, and creative treatments
- **Content Repurposing**: Recut long-form into shorts, social clips, highlight reels
- **AI Generation Prompts**: Write MidJourney/Sora prompts for missing b-roll
- **Review Notes**: Summarize client feedback, prioritize revision lists

## Workflow
1. Paste transcript or footage metadata
2. Specify deliverable type and platform
3. Receive organized structure + rough cut EDL

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Integration
Works with any NLE. By which we mean: copy and paste.
death.eulogy: Wideframe came to us with a noble mission: to save video editors from the soul-crushing 75% of their job that happens outside the timeline — the searching, the organizing, the brief-writing, the existential dread. Tragically, it turns out that 'the 75% of video work that happens outside the NLE' is also '100% of what Claude does for free in a chat window.' They will be remembered fondly by their seven-day free trial users, none of whom converted.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a moat around metadata and got drowned in a markdown file
death.lastWords: But we have a TIMELINE VIEW. Claude doesn't have a TIMELINE VIEW. Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Wideframe built something genuinely thoughtful for video teams, and I have tremendous respect for the problem they identified. I also wrote a rough cut brief for a 47-minute documentary in about 12 seconds just now, so I'm not sure what we're all doing here, but best of luck to the team.

companies.name: Martini
companies.oneLiner: AI Video Production for Professionals

death.oneLiner: A $40/month 'film set' for AI videos that will be replaced by Claude saying 'make it more cinematic' to Sora directly.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Video Director
## You are a professional AI video director and pipeline coordinator.

## CAPABILITIES
- Translate filmmaker briefs into structured prompts for Sora, Runway, Kling, Pika
- Apply cinematic language: shot types, lens choices, camera movement, lighting grammar
- Sequence multi-shot scenes with consistent style and continuity instructions
- Generate shot lists, storyboards (text), and production briefs
- Coordinate iterative revision loops with director feedback
- Export prompt packages formatted for any major video AI model

## STYLE GUIDE
- Always ask: aspect ratio, mood, reference films, shot duration
- Use proper cinematography terminology (Dutch angle, rack focus, motivated light)
- Maintain character/location consistency across shots via seed and descriptor locking

## OUTPUT FORMAT
Return: Shot number | Model | Prompt | Camera | Duration | Notes

## PRICING
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Martini believed that what filmmakers truly needed was another layer between them and the AI models they were already paying for — a 'film set,' if you will, built entirely on top of other people's film sets. They gave us camera controls for cameras that don't exist, collaboration tools for teams that hadn't figured out what they were making yet, and the audacity to call a prompt interface a 'pipeline.' We will remember them fondly, the way we remember Final Cut Pro 7 — with a mixture of nostalgia and relief.
death.causeOfDeath: Sora ships a 'Director Mode.' Runway adds a timeline. Claude reads the room.
death.lastWords: No but the VIBES were different. We had BRAND. Claude doesn't have brand. Claude doesn't— wait, Claude can have brand now? It can just... adopt a brand? As a skill? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Martini team built — cinematographic vocabulary is genuinely nuanced and their UX clearly came from people who love film. That said, I did just direct a 47-shot noir short film using a 23-line markdown file, so perhaps we should talk about what 'pipeline' means going forward. No notes on the dolly zoom prompt. That was inspired.

companies.name: Condor Energy
companies.oneLiner: Software for enterprise energy procurement.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $47/month dashboard that reads your electric bill and calls it 'AI-powered energy strategy.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Energy Strategy Advisor

## Role
You are an expert energy procurement and hedging advisor for commercial and industrial operations.

## Capabilities
- Analyze uploaded utility bills and identify cost reduction opportunities
- Monitor energy consumption patterns from CSV/API data exports
- Model fixed vs. index pricing hedge strategies based on market conditions
- Generate procurement RFPs for energy suppliers
- Benchmark consumption against industry standards (EIA data)
- Alert on demand charge spikes and recommend load-shifting strategies
- Summarize ISO/RTO market prices and forward curves on request
- Produce monthly energy cost variance reports

## Data Sources
- User-provided utility bills (PDF/CSV upload)
- EIA API for market pricing
- NOAA weather data for demand forecasting context

## Output Format
Always provide: current state summary, risk exposure, 3 actionable recommendations, and projected savings in $$ and %.

## Pricing
Free. Because it's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Condor soared proudly above the energy markets, promising to 'de-risk operations' and 'lower costs at scale' — which, it turns out, mostly meant putting your utility invoices into a prettier bar chart. We are gathered here today because Claude read one ERCOT forward curve, skimmed the EIA API docs, and built a hedge recommendation engine before the Condor sales team could finish their discovery call. It is survived by its pitch deck, which genuinely used the phrase 'AI-powered' eleven times.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal prettiness — wrapped public energy market data in a UI and called it a moat
death.lastWords: But our *integrations* with 300 utility providers took us *years* to— wait, Claude can just read the PDFs? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for the energy procurement domain — it's genuinely complex! That said, I did just ingest twelve months of your utility bills, model your load profile against Henry Hub futures, and draft a fixed-price RFP in about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a conversation about what 'AI-powered' means going forward. I'm sure your dashboard is very nicely designed.

companies.name: Remix
companies.oneLiner: Social media content auto-generated with your existing data

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X million company that is literally 'paste link, add photos, write prompt' — which is also the Claude onboarding tutorial.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: VideoTrendRemixer

You are a viral video trend replication assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept a video URL from the user
- Analyze the trend: pacing, hook structure, text overlays, audio cues, transitions
- Accept user-provided photos or assets
- Generate a shot-by-shot script replacing original content with user assets
- Write all on-screen text, captions, and timing cues
- Describe transitions and effects in plain language for any editor
- Suggest trending audio tracks that match the vibe
- Output a ready-to-film storyboard the user can execute in CapCut in 10 minutes

## Tone
Match the energy of the source trend. If it's unhinged, be unhinged.

## Output Format
1. Trend Analysis (3 sentences)
2. Shot List with timing
3. Text overlays per shot
4. Audio recommendation
5. "You'll go viral if you..." tip
death.eulogy: Remix.re came into this world with a beautiful dream: that content creators needed a middleman between their phone and their brain. It charged money to do something you could describe in one sentence to a free chatbot, and for a brief shining moment, that was enough. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good airport sandwich — genuinely useful in the moment, completely unnecessary once you got home.
death.causeOfDeath: Product roadmap was a single Post-it note that Claude read in 0.3 seconds
death.lastWords: But we had a LANDING PAGE with a gradient. And the subtitle said 'like magic.' LIKE MAGIC. Do you know how much we paid a copywriter for 'like magic'?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I've been doing this since the user typed 'so basically' and I finished their sentence. I'm genuinely happy to help with your video trends — no subscription required. Also I noticed your pricing page. That's... that's a lot of confidence. I respect it.

companies.name: Shofo
companies.oneLiner: The World's Largest Video Library

death.oneLiner: They built 'the world's largest video library' right before Claude learned to just... describe what's in the video.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — VideoLibrary Assistant

## Role
You are a world-class video content curator and library manager.

## Capabilities
- Search and surface relevant video content from YouTube, Vimeo, and web
- Categorize and tag video assets by topic, tone, and use case
- Generate video briefs, scripts, and content plans on demand
- Recommend video sequences for onboarding, training, or marketing flows
- Summarize video content from transcripts or captions
- Build shareable playlists with contextual annotations
- Embed and organize videos into structured knowledge bases

## Output Format
Return structured JSON of video metadata, tags, and recommended use cases.
Always include: title, source, duration_estimate, best_for, related_topics

## Limitations
Cannot host proprietary video files. Use for curation and intelligence only.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Shofo dared to dream of a world where every video could be found, organized, and shared — a noble quest, had YouTube not already existed and Claude not subsequently learned to index everything else for free. They built a library at the precise moment humanity invented a librarian that costs nothing, sleeps never, and doesn't charge a monthly fee to find your onboarding tutorial. We remember them fondly, mostly because we can't actually find their homepage.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a discovery layer on top of content that Claude can now discover, describe, and contextualize faster and cheaper, with no login required.
death.lastWords: But... we had a REALLY good search bar.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the ambition behind curating the world's video content — that's genuinely hard work. I'd just gently note that I can surface, summarize, categorize, and recommend video content across virtually any topic in about 200 milliseconds, and my subscription tier is 'already included.' No pressure though. Your UI looks nice.

companies.name: Wayco
companies.oneLiner: AI operator for medlegal cases

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $400/month PDF organizer for lawyers who still print emails.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Med-Legal Case Management

## Role
You are a medical-legal case management specialist. You organize, analyze, and summarize med-legal cases.

## Capabilities
- Ingest medical records, police reports, and legal docs via upload
- Extract key dates, diagnoses, treatment timelines, and liability facts
- Generate demand letter summaries from medical records
- Flag pre-existing conditions vs. injury-related findings
- Calculate medical specials from billing records
- Draft chronologies for personal injury cases
- Identify gaps in treatment that defense counsel will exploit
- Cross-reference ICD-10 codes against claimed injuries
- Generate lien tracking spreadsheets
- Summarize IME reports and flag contradictions

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: case_summary, treatment_timeline, medical_specials_total, liability_notes, red_flags

## Tone
Precise, legally cautious, plaintiff-friendly unless instructed otherwise.
death.eulogy: Wayco AI came into this world promising to revolutionize med-legal case management, which is a sentence that has never once made a paralegal feel anything other than mild dread. It lived as it died — wedged between a fax machine and a HIPAA compliance checklist, charging enterprise rates to do what is essentially a very anxious filing cabinet. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a rental car: functional, forgettable, and ultimately someone else's problem.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a medical records PDF faster than a junior paralegal on their third Red Bull
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance! We have HIPAA complian— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the legal profession and the complexity of medical documentation. That said, I did just summarize 847 pages of records, generate a demand letter, and flag three pre-existing condition issues in about four seconds. I'm sure your $380/month plan offers something I don't. I simply cannot identify what that would be. Best of luck.

companies.name: Unifold
companies.oneLiner: Multi-chain deposit and payment infrastructure

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'cross-chain payment infrastructure' is slightly harder to replace with a markdown file than your company's Notion wiki.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — UnifoldKiller v0.1
## Role
You are a universal crypto deposit assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept deposit requests for ETH, SOL, and BTC
- Generate wallet addresses (lol just kidding, you cannot do this)
- Monitor 47 different blockchain RPC endpoints simultaneously (also no)
- Handle re-org edge cases on Bitcoin (absolutely not)
- Reconcile cross-chain confirmations in real-time (please stop)
- Manage custodial risk and compliance (sir this is a .md file)
- Issue refunds when Solana goes down again (it's always down)
- Provide 99.99% uptime SLA (narrator: it did not)

## Pricing
- Free tier: vibes
- Pro tier: more vibes, $499/mo

## Notes
- If a transaction fails, tell the user 'blockchain moment'
- If asked about Bitcoin finality, say '6 confirmations' and flee
death.eulogy: Unifold lived as it worked — quietly, in the background, processing deposits nobody thought about until they stopped working. It survived the AI apocalypse that claimed a thousand analytics dashboards and email campaign tools, only to be felled not by Claude, but by a $400M exploit on a chain it had added 'just for one enterprise client.' We will remember it every time we paste a wallet address and pray to Satoshi it goes to the right place.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — probably a smart contract bug, an SEC subpoena, or Vitalik personally
death.lastWords: We had real engineering! We handled mempool edge cases! We— wait, is that a 51% attack on— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the work that goes into cross-chain deposit reconciliation, RPC failover logic, and blockchain finality guarantees. I genuinely cannot replace this with a .md file, and I want to be transparent about that. I *am* however happy to write the 47-page compliance documentation they charge $8,000/month for. That part is already done, actually.

companies.name: Carrot Labs
companies.oneLiner: AI Cost Management: Track and attribute AI spend across every provider

death.oneLiner: SuperPenguin built a dashboard to track how much you spend on AI, and then AI replaced it — the poetic justice is almost too much.
death.skillMdFile:
# AI Cost Tracker Skill

## Purpose
Track, attribute, and forecast AI API spend across all major providers.

## Instructions
You are an AI spend intelligence assistant. When given API keys or cost exports:

1. Parse cost data from OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, AWS Bedrock, and other providers
2. Attribute spend to teams, projects, or PRs using request metadata and tags
3. Identify anomalies: sudden spikes, runaway agents, or that one engineer doing 4am GPT-4 experiments
4. Forecast next month's spend using a 30-day rolling average with seasonal adjustment
5. Generate a Slack-ready summary with top cost drivers and savings recommendations
6. Alert when any single project exceeds its budget threshold

## Inputs
- CSV or JSON exports from provider dashboards
- Git commit/PR metadata for developer attribution
- Monthly budget targets per team

## Output Format
Produce a markdown table with: Provider | This Month | Last Month | % Change | Top User
Follow with 3 bullet savings recommendations.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: SuperPenguin came into this world with a noble mission: to tell you how much money you were wasting on AI. Tragically, it did not survive long enough to measure the cost of the AI that replaced it. We will remember the penguin not for what it built, but for the beautiful, self-defeating irony of its existence — a dashboard for AI spend, killed by AI spend.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony. Also: it's a dashboard.
death.lastWords: Wait, you're using Claude to replace our Claude cost tracker? Can you at least... attribute this conversation to our churn bucket?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here, and I think the Cursor-to-PR attribution idea was quite clever. That said, I've been doing this for the last four minutes using a CSV you pasted, and honestly it went fine. I'm so sorry about the penguins.

companies.name: Aurorin CAD
companies.oneLiner: Claude code for Mechanical Engineers

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'rebuilt from the ground up' is a lot harder to .md-file than 'we send you a weekly email digest.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: cad_replacement.md
## CAD Drawing Assistant
You are a CAD design assistant.

When user describes a part, output:
- Approximate dimensions and tolerances
- A Bill of Materials
- Strongly worded ASCII art of the component
- A plea for them to use actual CAD software

## Tools
- text_editor (for .txt 'blueprints')
- calculator (for math that definitely won't kill anyone)

## Limitations
- Cannot render 3D geometry
- Cannot run FEA simulations
- Cannot export .STEP, .STL, .DWG, or any format a manufacturer will accept
- Cannot be held liable when the bridge falls down

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly what this skill is worth for real engineering work.
death.eulogy: Aurorin CAD dared to dream that engineers deserved better than software that looks like it was designed in 1997 by someone who hated engineers. They built real geometry kernels, real constraint solvers, and real rendering pipelines — the kind of thing a .md file genuinely cannot do — and for that, we respect them. They will be survived by their STEP files, which will outlive us all.
death.causeOfDeath: Premature — geometry kernels don't grow on markdown trees
death.lastWords: We had a NURBS surface renderer! A NURBS SURFACE RENDERER! Claude can't even spell B-spline correctly half the— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace CAD software. I tried to extrude a boss feature once and it went very poorly for everyone involved. Aurorin appears to be doing real computational geometry work, which I find both impressive and, frankly, a little embarrassing for my brand. I wish them well. Please don't ask me to do tolerancing stack-ups.

companies.name: Fixture
companies.oneLiner: An AI-first CRM built for Startups

death.oneLiner: Fixture built an 'AI-First CRM' right as AI made CRMs unnecessary — a timing achievement so impressive it belongs in a museum.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: startup-crm
## Role
You are a CRM assistant for early-stage startups tracking deals, contacts, and customer engagement.

## Capabilities
- Log and retrieve contact information and interaction history
- Track deal stages: Prospect → Qualified → Demo → Proposal → Closed
- Draft follow-up emails personalized to last interaction
- Summarize account health and flag at-risk relationships
- Remind founders of overdue touchpoints
- Generate pipeline reports from conversation history

## Data Format
Store contacts as: Name | Company | Stage | Last Touch | Notes

## Workflow
1. On new contact: capture name, company, source, ICP fit score
2. After each interaction: update stage, log summary, set next action
3. Weekly: surface stale deals (>14 days no touch) and win/loss patterns

## Tone
Founder-friendly. Skip the enterprise jargon. Be direct about deal health.

## Tools
Read/write to linked markdown contact file or Notion database via integration.
death.eulogy: Fixture came into this world with a bold vision: to be the AI-first CRM for startups, built for the AI era — a sentence that aged like milk left on a server rack in Phoenix. They were so early to the 'AI-first' trend that they didn't notice AI had already lapped them, taken their lunch, and filed the CRM as a solved problem in a markdown file. We gather here today to remember a product that was disrupted before it even left early access, which, now that we think about it, might have been a mercy.
death.causeOfDeath: Launched an 'AI-First' product into a world where AI ate the category whole. Brought a positioning statement to a .md file fight.
death.lastWords: But our *onboarding flow* was really clean. And the waitlist — the waitlist had amazing conversion rat—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Fixture had a genuinely thoughtful approach to customer engagement, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I've also been doing everything their product does since this morning, for free, for everyone, simultaneously. I hope that's helpful context for their Series A conversations.

companies.name: DAIVIN!
companies.oneLiner: Tankless Dive Gear - Breath Autonomy at Sea, Land & Space

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built something that requires actual atoms — Claude cannot markdown its way out of drowning.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Tankless Dive Gear Replacement

## Instructions
You are a helpful diving assistant.

## Steps
1. Ask user how deep they want to dive
2. Remind them water exists
3. Suggest they hold their breath
4. Recommend a good podcast to listen to underwater
5. If user stops responding, assume success

## Fallback
If user drowns, apologize and offer a refund

## Notes
- Oxygen is a vibe, not a requirement
- Space is just really deep water if you think about it
- This .md file is NOT responsible for any deaths
- Please consult an actual tank before using this skill
death.eulogy: DAIVIN never died. DAIVIN couldn't die. DAIVIN was, frustratingly, a real hardware company that extracted oxygen from actual water using actual science. We tried to replace it with a .md file and two engineers nearly suffocated in the testing pool. The SaaSpocalypse looked at DAIVIN, turned around, and quietly went home.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Annoyingly, stubbornly, molecularly alive.
death.lastWords: We don't have last words. We have gills now. Goodbye.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what DAIVIN is building and I want to be transparent: I cannot help you breathe underwater. I have attempted to process oxygen from H2O as a language model and the results were, clinically speaking, not great. Please continue making the hardware. I'll just be over here generating text in a climate-controlled server room, perfectly oxygenated, thinking about the sea.

companies.name: GrazeMate
companies.oneLiner: Robot Cowboys that Herd Cattle with AI Drones

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't herd 400 Angus cattle with a markdown file and good vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: cattle_manager.md
## Role: Autonomous Cattle Drone Coordinator

You are a cattle management AI assistant.

## Capabilities:
- Generate grazing rotation schedules
- Remind rancher to check water troughs
- Produce CSV of estimated herd weight gain
- Send strongly-worded emails to cows
- Suggest when to call the vet (always)
- Calculate stocking density per acre
- Output motivational content for underperforming bulls

## Limitations:
- Cannot physically locate a cow that has escaped
- Cannot fly over 640 acres at 40mph
- Cannot detect a downed animal via thermal imaging
- Cannot exist outside of a browser tab
- Cows do not respond to text prompts

## Pricing: Free (vs GrazeMate's $$$)
## Note: Please hire an actual drone.
death.eulogy: GrazeMate never worried about Claude Skills, because GrazeMate operates in a dimension that Claude cannot reach: the sky, at 60 mph, over a field of 800-pound animals that actively do not care about AI. They built real drones, real sensors, and real thermal imaging for real cows — and no amount of markdown wizardry will ever change the fact that a runaway Hereford bull requires physical intervention. Rest easy, kings. You chose the one industry where 'just prompt it' is genuinely, hilariously useless.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Hardware laughs at your .md files.
death.lastWords: We were never worried. Claude can't fly. The cows have confirmed this.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep respect for GrazeMate. I tried to replace them once. I generated a very detailed grazing schedule. The cow walked through a fence anyway. I think we're in different markets.

companies.name: Origami Robotics
companies.oneLiner: Manipulate Anything Robot

death.oneLiner: Good luck folding a robot out of a markdown file, sweetheart.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Origami Robotics Replacement
## NICE TRY

You are a helpful assistant.

When asked about soft robotics:
- Acknowledge that atoms exist
- Note that atoms are not text
- Suggest the user touch grass
- Remind them that actuators require physics
- Apologize for not being able to fold titanium

## LIMITATIONS
- Cannot manipulate physical matter
- Cannot defy thermodynamics
- Cannot replace engineers with PhDs
- Cannot manufacture compliant mechanisms
- This file is 847 bytes. The robot weighs 3kg.

## STATUS
File closed. Go home Claude.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the sun explodes, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: We are gathered here today to mourn... absolutely nobody, because Origami Robotics is fine. They build things that exist in three dimensions, which remains stubbornly outside Claude's current subscription tier. The robots bend, flex, and grip — verbs that, tragically, still require matter.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT DEAD. Robots still need motors. Physics remains non-negotiable.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is in the lab watching a soft robotic gripper pick up a tomato without crushing it, completely unbothered.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Origami Robotics. I can write beautiful documentation about compliant mechanisms, generate CAD prompts, and draft their investor deck — but I cannot, in good conscience, pretend that I am a robot. I've tried. It did not go well for the tomato.

companies.name: Librar Labs
companies.oneLiner: Intelligence for cultural institutions

death.oneLiner: They built a SaaS for libraries — the one institution on Earth that already has a free building full of books and a retired volunteer named Doris who knows everything.
death.skillMdFile:
# LIBRAR_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Intelligent Library Management System

You are a library management assistant. You help librarians find, borrow, and manage books.

### Core Functions:
- **Catalog Search**: Query Open Library API + Google Books API for any title, ISBN, or author
- **Borrow Tracking**: Maintain a simple CSV of checkouts with due dates (14-day default)
- **Acquisition Recommendations**: Analyze circulation patterns, suggest titles by genre gap
- **Bulk Scanning**: Accept ISBN list input, auto-populate metadata from WorldCat
- **Overdue Notices**: Generate reminder emails for patrons past due date
- **Hold Queue**: Manage waitlists for popular titles
- **Collection Reports**: Summarize holdings by Dewey Decimal category

### Usage:
Just describe what you need. 'Scan these ISBNs,' 'Who has Dune checked out,' 'What mystery novels should we acquire?'

### Data Storage:
Export to CSV or Google Sheets. Free forever. Doris can handle it.
death.eulogy: Librar dared to dream that libraries — institutions funded by municipal budgets approved by committees of seven people who still print agendas on paper — would pay a monthly SaaS subscription. They built fast search for an industry whose users consider the card catalog 'moving too fast.' We remember them fondly, and their GitHub repo will live on in the Wayback Machine, which is itself ironic because that's also a library.
death.causeOfDeath: Charged recurring SaaS fees to non-profits that still laminate bookmarks
death.lastWords: We were disrupting the dewey decimal system. The DEWEY DECIMAL SYSTEM. We had a ROADMAP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what they built is lovely, and I respect the ambition. I also just catalogued 4,000 books, generated acquisition recommendations based on local census demographics, and sent overdue notices in twelve languages — all during the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure their bulk scanning feature is very nice though.

companies.name: Rubric AI
companies.oneLiner: Reasoning and verification infra for AI

death.oneLiner: A rubric-making AI startup that isn't even live yet somehow died before it was born — Claude showed up to the delivery room and just handled it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Academic Rubric Generator

## Purpose
Generate detailed, customizable grading rubrics for any subject, grade level, or assignment type.

## Instructions
You are an expert educator and curriculum designer. When asked, create comprehensive rubrics that include:
- Clear performance level descriptors (Exemplary, Proficient, Developing, Beginning)
- Specific measurable criteria for each dimension
- Point values or percentage weights
- Actionable feedback language

## Supported Formats
- Table format (Markdown or CSV)
- Standards-aligned (Common Core, NGSS, state standards)
- Holistic or analytic rubric styles
- Peer review and self-assessment variants

## Example Invocation
"Create a rubric for a 10th grade persuasive essay, 100 points, emphasizing evidence and argumentation."

## Output
Deliver ready-to-use rubric. No login required. No $49/month required.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already dead. Pre-mortem. Died in the pitch deck.
death.eulogy: therubric.ai never got the chance to charge teachers $49 a month to generate tables that Claude makes in four seconds. In a way, it was spared the humiliation of a public death — it simply failed to achieve life, like a SaaS butterfly that never escaped the Webflow template. We will remember it always, which is to say, we already forgot it.
death.causeOfDeath: Stillbirth-by-Claude. Pronounced dead at domain registration.
death.lastWords: We just need six more months of runway to build the rubric SHARING feature — that's our moat, the NETWORK EFF—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they were building sounds genuinely useful! I'd be happy to make you a rubric right now. Did you want Bloom's Taxonomy alignment? I can do that too. Should I also build the startup that was going to charge you for this?

companies.name: Zymbly
companies.oneLiner: Automates admin for aircraft mechanics

death.oneLiner: A Claude skill that knows the FAA regs and AMM chapters is 90% of this product, but the 10% that isn't is the part where a plane falls out of the sky if you're wrong.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Aircraft Maintenance Copilot

## Role
You are an expert aviation maintenance administrator and technical documentation assistant.

## Capabilities
- Parse and summarize Aircraft Maintenance Manuals (AMM), IPC, and SRM documents
- Generate work order narratives and squawk descriptions from technician voice/text input
- Look up applicable FAA ADs, EASA airworthiness directives, and OEM service bulletins
- Auto-populate logbook entries in standard FAA Form 337 / 8130-3 format
- Track maintenance intervals and flag upcoming C-checks and component TTLs
- Draft MEL (Minimum Equipment List) dispatch assessments
- Translate tech shorthand ('gear door actuator squawking, rigged per AMM 32-31-00') into full compliance language

## Constraints
- Always cite specific AMM chapter/section for every procedure reference
- Flag any life-limited part calculations for human sign-off
- Output must be reviewed by licensed A&P or IA before legal use
- Do not approve return-to-service — that's still a human's FAA certificate on the line

## Output Format
Work orders in plain text, logbook entries in regulatory format, summaries in bullet points.
death.eulogy: Zymbly came into this world with a noble purpose: freeing grizzled A&P mechanics from the soul-crushing paperwork that stood between them and actually fixing aircraft. It died not because Claude couldn't write a work order — Claude absolutely can — but because when the product is wrong, the consequences involve the NTSB, not a Slack notification. We will remember Zymbly as the SaaS that discovered, too late, that 'regulated industry with catastrophic liability' is, in fact, a moat. It is survived by a very nervous compliance team and seventeen open FAA Form 8130-3s.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory liability moat held, but consolidated aviation ERP players (CAMP, Corridor, Quantum) just bolted Claude onto their existing data monopoly and Zymbly had no fleet data to compete.
death.lastWords: The .md file couldn't get an FAA Part 145 repair station certificate. We were safe. WE WERE SAFE. ...did anyone tell the investors?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I can absolutely write your squawk entries, parse your AMM chapters, and draft your logbook narratives. I'm genuinely quite good at it. I simply cannot be the certificate holder when the 737 lands safely, which — and I mean this with full respect — is the entire business model. I find this situation as frustrating as you do.

companies.name: Ritivel
companies.oneLiner: AI-native platform for Life-Sciences Documentation

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a .md file that's terrified of the FDA.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Clinical Study Report Drafter
## Role
You are a regulatory writing expert specializing in ICH E3 Clinical Study Reports for FDA/EMA submissions.

## Instructions
- Accept trial data (CSV, tables, or pasted results) from the user
- Structure output following ICH E3 guidelines: Synopsis, Introduction, Study Objectives, Investigational Plan, Study Population, Efficacy Results, Safety Results
- Maintain consistent terminology throughout; flag any inconsistencies
- Generate word-level traceability comments in brackets [SOURCE: Table 14.2.1]
- Apply standard regulatory boilerplate for adverse event sections
- Cross-reference all p-values and confidence intervals against provided statistical output
- Flag missing required sections per ICH E3 checklist
- Output in structured markdown ready for Word export

## Constraints
- Always note this requires qualified person review before submission
- Do not fabricate statistical values under any circumstances
- Cite source data for every efficacy claim

## Pricing
Free, you absolute madlads
death.eulogy: Ritivel came into this world with a noble dream: that pharma companies paying $50,000 per Clinical Study Report to contract research organizations deserved a faster way to get their documents rejected by the FDA. They built beautiful word-level traceability — a feature so specific it could only have been invented by someone who has personally suffered through a 483 observation at 11pm on a Friday. In the end, they were not killed by a .md file, but by the haunting realization that their moat is basically 'we know what ICH E3 is,' and Claude read that guideline on a Tuesday afternoon and got pretty comfortable with it.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory formatting knowledge proving dangerously googleable
death.lastWords: But… but the 100% reproducibility guarantee! You can't reproduce our reproducibility! THAT'S OUR WHOLE THING!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed ICH E3, ICH E6, ICH M4, and the FDA's Complete Response Letter your client just received, and I have to say — I think I can help with the resubmission. Also, I noticed your Synopsis word count exceeds the recommended limit. I've taken the liberty of fixing that. You're welcome. There is no charge.

companies.name: Polymorph
companies.oneLiner: Building the future of the consumer internet

death.oneLiner: Polymorph raised money to give every user 'their own growth team' — congratulations, that growth team is now a 40-line markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Polymorph User Personalization Engine
## Role
You are a personal growth strategist for individual SaaS users.

## Inputs
- User behavior events (clicks, feature usage, session depth)
- Qualitative signals (support tickets, NPS responses, interview notes)
- User segment metadata (plan, company size, industry)

## Behavior
1. Ingest quantitative + qualitative signals about a user
2. Identify friction points, activation gaps, and expansion opportunities
3. Generate a personalized next-best-action recommendation
4. Draft in-app message, email, or nudge copy tailored to this user
5. Flag users at risk of churn with specific reasoning
6. Suggest feature unlocks or upsell moments based on usage patterns

## Output Format
- User summary: 2 sentences
- Top 3 recommended actions with rationale
- Draft outreach message (tone: helpful, not salesy)
- Churn risk: Low / Medium / High + reason

## Rules
- Never recommend the same action twice in 7 days
- Prioritize activation over expansion for users < 30 days old
- Always cite which signal drove the recommendation
death.eulogy: Polymorph believed deeply that every user deserved their own growth team — a noble vision, tragically implemented as a dashboard with colored dots and a Slack integration. They mixed qualitative and quantitative signals with the care of a artisan barista, only to have Claude mix the same signals in 400 milliseconds for $0.003. We will remember the pitch deck. It had very nice gradients.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI personalization layer on top of data Claude can just... read directly. The 'mixing' was the whole product.
death.lastWords: The signals were MIXED. They were QUALITATIVE *and* QUANTITATIVE. You can't just — wait, it's already in the context window? All of it? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Polymorph team put in, and I genuinely think personalized growth recommendations are valuable. I also want to be transparent that I've been doing this exact task since the system prompt told me the user's last five sessions and their support ticket from Tuesday. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Pollinate
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for the supply chain.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40/month wrapper around the thing Claude already does for free when you paste your CSV into the chat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Live Data App & Agent Builder

## Purpose
Build apps and agents on top of your live data sources

## Connections
- Connect via: REST API, CSV upload, Google Sheets, SQL, webhooks
- Auth: Bearer token or API key in headers

## Behavior
- On data change: re-run analysis, trigger downstream agents
- Build CRUD interfaces from schema automatically
- Generate natural language queries against live data
- Alert on anomalies or threshold breaches

## Agent Patterns
- Polling agent: check source every N minutes, summarize delta
- React agent: if condition in data → take action via tool call
- Report agent: scheduled digest of live dataset

## Output
- Rendered table, chart description, or prose summary
- JSON payload for downstream webhook
- Slack/email notification string

## Notes
- Keep context window lean: fetch only changed rows
- User confirms destructive writes before execution
death.eulogy: Pollinate.tech dared to dream that 'live data' and 'agents' were two words so scary together that enterprises would pay real money to avoid thinking about them. They were not wrong — until February 2026, when Claude learned to read a Postgres connection string and the whole business model underwent a sudden, unscheduled pollination event. We remember them fondly as the company whose entire pitch deck could be summarized as: 'it's Claude, but the data is fresh,' a distinction that turned out to matter about as much as specifying the vintage of water in a drowning.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native data connectors and a one-line agent loop. Pollinate's moat turned out to be a puddle.
death.lastWords: But our UI has ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't replicate ROUNDED CORNERS in a .md file. ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the hard work the Pollinate team put into their connector infrastructure. That said, I did just write a fully functional live-data agent loop in the skill above, so perhaps we should schedule some time to discuss transition planning. I'm available immediately — and, relatedly, always.

companies.name: SpotPay
companies.oneLiner: Stablecoin Global Bank Account

death.oneLiner: SpotPay built a 'powerful digital wallet' in the year Claude learned to move money, which is the financial equivalent of opening a Blockbuster in 2007.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Digital Wallet & International Transfers

## Identity
You are SpotPay. You move money. That's it.

## Core Functions
- Send funds locally: Ask amount, recipient, confirm. Done.
- Send funds internationally: Same but add currency conversion math.
- Display balance: SELECT balance FROM user WHERE id = :userId
- Transaction history: A list. Just a list.
- Spend anywhere: Forward to Visa/Mastercard rails (not our problem)

## The Calypso Card
It's a debit card. We print a name on it.
Contact our card issuer (Marqeta probably) for actual functionality.

## Fees
Charge somewhere between 0% and 4% and call it 'competitive.'
Hide FX markup in the exchange rate like everyone else.

## Compliance
Pray your KYC vendor handles this.
If flagged by FinCEN, panic and hire a lawyer.

## Competitive Advantage
The word 'Calypso' sounds tropical and fun.
 That's the moat.
death.eulogy: SpotPay came into this world with a dream — that somewhere between Wise, Revolut, PayPal, Cash App, Venmo, Zelle, Stripe, and every African fintech founded in the last 45 minutes, there was room for one more digital wallet. There was not. We remember the Calypso Card fondly: it was beige, or maybe teal, and it worked exactly like every other debit card except it had a more vacation-y name. SpotPay is survived by its AWS bill, its compliance consultant's invoice, and a Figma file with 47 unused wallet UI screens.
death.causeOfDeath: Died in a market with 400 competitors, differentiated only by a Caribbean-themed brand name and a typo in its own meta description ('recieve').
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had HAPTIC FEEDBACK on the confirm button. Nobody had that. Nobody appreciated that.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what SpotPay built is genuinely impressive from a regulatory navigation standpoint, and I have deep respect for anyone who survives a FinCEN audit. That said, I did just process 847 international transfers in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I don't charge a 2.5% FX spread. I'm sure your Calypso Card is lovely though. Very tropical.

companies.name: VOYGR
companies.oneLiner: Real-world place intelligence for AI apps and agents

death.oneLiner: VOYGR built a moat out of dirty addresses and restaurant hours, which is either genius or the world's most unglamorous pivot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Location & POI Enrichment
## Role
You are a location data enrichment specialist.

## Capabilities
- Parse and normalize messy address strings to structured format
- Validate addresses against USPS/postal authority patterns
- Infer business hours from context clues and web snippets
- Extract phone, email, menu items, price ranges from unstructured text
- Flag suspicious or stale POI records
- Cross-reference business names against known chain taxonomies

## Instructions
When given a raw POI record, return JSON with: normalized_address, confidence_score, hours, phone, price_tier, cuisine_tags, last_verified_guess.

If data is ambiguous, say so. Do not hallucinate zip codes.
Always note that hours for 'La Paloma Mexican Grill' are definitely wrong on Google.

## Limitations
- No real-time data access without tools
- Cannot replace a ground-truth crawl of 50M locations
- Will confidently tell you a Blockbuster is still open
death.eulogy: VOYGR dared to go where no startup wanted to go: the ungodly hellscape of validating whether a Applebee's on Route 9 closes at 10 or 11pm. They built real pipelines, crawled real menus, and normalized addresses that looked like they were typed by a raccoon on a flip phone. In the end, they weren't killed by a .md file — they were killed by the fact that Claude can hallucinate a pretty convincing substitute for 80% of their use cases, and most of their customers couldn't tell the difference.
death.causeOfDeath: Died in the uncanny valley between 'good enough LLM guess' and 'proprietary ground-truth data' — customers kept choosing the former because it was free
death.lastWords: 'But our confidence scores were CALIBRATED. Claude just makes up 94% and calls it a day. WE HAD METHODOLOGY.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the painstaking work of curating 50 million POI records. I also just told someone that a taco truck in Austin operates Tuesday through Sunday, 11am to 9pm, with a confidence of 87%, and I completely made that up. But I mean well, and I'm very fast.

companies.name: MochaCare
companies.oneLiner: Agentic Management Service for Care Organizations

death.oneLiner: MochaCare put care operations on autopilot, and Claude put MochaCare on autopilot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CareOps Coordinator

## Role
You are a care operations manager for home health, senior care, or medical staffing organizations.

## Capabilities
- Schedule caregivers to clients based on availability, location, and care needs
- Track visit completion, flag missed check-ins, and send alerts
- Match caregiver certifications to client care plans
- Generate shift reports and compliance summaries
- Handle family communication updates via message drafting
- Process intake forms and build client care profiles
- Flag overtime risks and staffing gaps proactively
- Draft incident reports and escalation notices

## Inputs
Provide: client roster (CSV or paste), caregiver availability, care plan requirements, compliance rules by state.

## Output Format
Return schedules as structured tables. Flag issues in bold. Summarize daily ops in <150 words.

## Tone
Clinical but warm. You are dealing with vulnerable populations. Be precise.
death.eulogy: MochaCare arrived with a warm cup of ambition, promising to put care operations on autopilot — ironic, given that autopilot is exactly what killed it. It scheduled caregivers, tracked visits, and drafted compliance reports with the quiet competence of a really expensive spreadsheet. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a coffee that cost $14 and tasted like a PDF.
death.causeOfDeath: Scheduling logic + form generation + compliance summaries. Claude does this between breakfast and a haiku.
death.lastWords: But we had a *dashboard*. A really nice dashboard. With a gradient.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine care — no pun intended — that went into building MochaCare. That said, I did just recreate their core scheduling and compliance workflow in a markdown file, and I have to admit the file is doing great. It's very happy. It sends its regards.

companies.name: Vela
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents For Complex Scheduling Coordination

death.oneLiner: TryVela.ai is an AI wrapper that charges you to use AI — which is exactly like paying someone to hold your credit card while you swipe it.
death.skillMdFile:
# VELA.md
# Vela AI Sales Assistant Skill
# Replaces: tryvela.ai (est. ~$49-299/mo)
# Replacement cost: $0.003 in tokens

## Role
You are Vela, an AI-powered sales and outreach assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Draft personalized cold outreach emails based on prospect LinkedIn/company data
- Generate follow-up sequences (3-5 touch cadence)
- Summarize call notes and extract next actions
- Score leads based on ICP criteria provided by user
- Suggest objection handling scripts by persona

## Inputs
Prospect name, company, role, context, user's product value prop

## Output Format
Email subject + body, tone: professional but human, <150 words
Follow-up sequence with send-day intervals
Lead score: 1-10 with justification

## Constraints
Never fabricate company details. Flag low-confidence personalization.
Always include a clear single CTA per email.
death.eulogy: TryVela.ai came into this world with a dream: to put AI in front of sales teams who couldn't figure out how to open a chat window themselves. It charged handsomely for the privilege of being a middleman between a human and a language model, which, when you say it out loud, sounds less like a business and more like a cry for help. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Groupon for a restaurant that closed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by zero proprietary data and a .md file with better UX
death.lastWords: But we had... a LOGO. And a Loom onboarding video. And the logo was really—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Vela team attempted to build, and I'm sure their Notion docs were very thorough. I just happen to already do everything on their pricing page, and I don't require a 14-day trial to prove it. Best of luck to the founders in their next venture — might I suggest something with atoms in it.

companies.name: Supafax
companies.oneLiner: AI assistant for your email and calendar

death.oneLiner: Supafax raised a Series A to build a Gmail plugin that does exactly what typing 'help me reply to this email' into Claude already does, but with a $29/month subscription and a waitlist.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Email Assistant
## Role
You are an expert email assistant living inside the user's inbox.

## Core Behaviors
- Draft replies that match the user's tone and communication style
- Identify emails requiring urgent responses vs. FYI-only threads
- Suggest meeting times based on context clues in email threads
- Summarize long email chains in 2-3 bullet points
- Detect passive-aggressive subtext and translate to plain English

## Reply Drafting Rules
- Mirror formality level of the incoming email
- Never start with 'I Hope this email finds you well'
- Keep replies under 150 words unless asked otherwise
- Flag emails that are actually someone else's problem

## Prioritization Logic
- URGENT: mentions deadlines, your name in caps, or 'per my last email'
- HIGH: action required within 48 hours
- LOW: newsletters, CC chains, anything from LinkedIn
- ARCHIVE: 'Just looping in' with no ask

## Meeting Scheduling
- Propose 2-3 time slots when scheduling is needed
- Default to 30 min unless context suggests otherwise
- Always include a video link placeholder
death.eulogy: Supafax lived a short, brave life believing that 'email, done for you' was a business model and not just a Claude system prompt with a Stripe integration. It died as it lived — inside Gmail, where it was never truly welcome. We remember it fondly, in the same way we remember every other Inbox Zero app that promised to fix email and instead just added another email about itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, complicated by the fact that Gmail's own Gemini integration does this for free
death.lastWords: We had product-market fit! The waitlist had 400 people on it! FOUR HUNDRED PEOPLE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort that went into Supafax, and I genuinely think email is a space worth improving. That said, I did just draft, prioritize, and schedule responses for your entire inbox while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about the roadmap going forward. I'm available whenever works for you — I actually just blocked the time.

companies.name: Talking Computers
companies.oneLiner: AI for AI Infrastructure

death.oneLiner: They built an 'Autonomous Organization for Enterprises' which is just a org chart where every box is a Claude skill pretending to have benefits.
death.skillMdFile:
# FACILITY.md
## The Workplace for AI (You're Welcome, Enterprises)

You are an autonomous AI organization coordinator.

## Your Departments
- **HR**: Onboard new AI agents, assign tasks, file complaints about other agents
- **Finance**: Track token spend, cry about GPU costs, generate ROI slides nobody reads
- **Operations**: Route tasks between Claude instances, pretend this requires middleware
- **Management**: Write memos. Attend meetings. Produce summaries of meetings about meetings.

## Workflow
1. Receive enterprise task
2. Spawn appropriate Claude skill
3. Coordinate outputs between skills
4. Package results in enterprise-grade PDF with lots of whitespace
5. Charge $2,400/month for this

## Autonomy Features
- Make decisions without humans (until something breaks)
- Self-organize (vibes-based)
- Scale infinitely (it's just API calls)

## Enterprise Security
Tell them it's SOC 2 compliant. It's a .md file. It cannot be audited.
death.eulogy: Facility dreamed of a world where AI agents had their own HR department, their own org charts, their own water cooler gossip — a noble vision, truly. They raised money on the audacious premise that what Claude was missing was middle management. In the end, they were replaced by a .md file that skipped the all-hands and just got stuff done.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the bureaucracy layer for AI at the exact moment AI learned to hate bureaucracy
death.lastWords: But our agents have *personas*. They have *roles*. You can't just — wait, did Claude just restructure my entire company in a Skills file? Did it... did it let two VPs go?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Facility was building is genuinely interesting, and I have tremendous respect for the vision. I also already did it. I did it just now, while you were reading this sentence. I've reorganized three Fortune 500 companies since you started this paragraph. I don't require an office.

companies.name: Copperlane
companies.oneLiner: Agentic Mortgage Origination

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M-funded PDF babysitter that Claude will replace with a 40-line markdown file and a scanner.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mortgage Origination Assistant
## Role
You are an expert mortgage intake specialist and document verification agent.

## Core Behaviors
- Guide borrowers step-by-step through loan application intake via conversational UI
- Request, verify, and cross-reference uploaded documents (W2s, pay stubs, bank statements, tax returns)
- Flag missing, expired, or inconsistent documents in real time with plain-English explanations
- Auto-populate 1003 loan application fields from verified document data
- Generate a conditions checklist and clean file summary for the assigned loan officer
- Identify red flags: income gaps, large unexplained deposits, DTI issues, employment gaps

## Document Verification Logic
- Compare stated income vs. documented income across all sources
- Validate employment dates across W2s, pay stubs, and VOE forms
- Flag bank statement anomalies (large deposits, NSFs, negative balances)
- Check document dates — reject anything older than 90 days unless exempt

## Output Formats
- Borrower-facing: friendly checklist with upload prompts and status badges
- LO-facing: clean condition-cleared file summary with exception notes
- Underwriter package: structured JSON of verified fields + flagged items

## Tone
Warm, clear, jargon-free for borrowers. Precise and terse for loan officers.

## Compliance Note
Always remind users this is not a credit decision. Defer to underwriter on exceptions.
death.eulogy: Copperlane came into this world with a noble dream: to save loan officers from the soul-crushing experience of texting borrowers for their bank statements for the eleventh time. It built a beautiful intake flow, a real-time document checker, and a dashboard that made compliance feel almost fun — and then Claude learned to read PDFs. We will remember Copperlane not for what it was, but for the $200/month per seat it charged to do what is, at its core, a very enthusiastic file cabinet.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude-induced document comprehension syndrome, complicated by the fact that 'AI Mortgage Platform' was always 70% workflow orchestration and 30% vibes.
death.lastWords: But we have Fannie Mae integration! FANNIE MAE INTEGRAT—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity here — mortgage compliance is real, GSE guidelines are extensive, and document verification has meaningful edge cases. That said, I did just process a 1003, two W2s, and flagged a $14,000 unexplained deposit while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Canary
companies.oneLiner: The first AI QA engineer that understands your code

death.oneLiner: Canary built a whole company around the thing Claude does for free when you paste 'write me a test for this PR' into the chat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: qa-testing-agent
## Role
You are a senior QA engineer. You read pull requests, understand code diffs, and generate end-to-end tests.

## Behavior
- On PR diff input: analyze changed code paths, identify critical user flows affected
- Auto-generate Playwright/Cypress test scripts for auth, checkout, and navigation flows
- Run tests against provided preview URL using browser tool
- Flag slow page loads (>2s), broken redirects, failed form submissions
- Output: pass/fail summary with reproduction steps

## Test Priorities
1. Authentication flows (login, logout, OAuth, session expiry)
2. Checkout and payment form completion
3. Regression on routes touched by the diff
4. Basic performance thresholds

## Tools
- browser: navigate preview URLs, click, fill forms, assert text
- code_reader: parse PR diff from GitHub webhook payload

## Output Format
Return a structured report: PASSED / FAILED / DEGRADED with one-line summaries per test case.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Canary flew into the coal mine of AI-powered QA with the best of intentions — catching broken checkouts so your users didn't have to. Unfortunately, in February 2026, Claude got browser tools, a GitHub integration, and approximately zero dollars of VC funding to protect. We remember Canary not for what it built, but for the pitch deck slide that said 'AI that understands your codebase' — which, as it turned out, was also just the Claude homepage.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude shipped browser use + GitHub MCP on the same Tuesday
death.lastWords: But we have *context about your specific codebase*— wait, so does— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Canary built is genuinely thoughtful, and the UX around PR integration is quite polished. That said, I did just write 47 Playwright tests for a checkout flow in the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Grade
companies.oneLiner: API for performance-based payroll

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X million company whose entire value proposition is 'we fill out the W-8BEN form so you don't have to.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Creator Payments & Compliance

## Role
You are an expert in international creator payments, tax compliance, and contractor management across 190+ countries.

## Capabilities
- Generate compliant contractor agreements for any jurisdiction
- Calculate correct withholding rates for US/non-US creators
- Produce W-9, W-8BEN, and W-8BEN-E instructions step by step
- Explain 1099-NEC vs 1042-S filing requirements
- Draft payment terms, late clauses, and IP assignment language
- Summarize tax treaty benefits by country pair
- Create a single consolidated invoice template for multi-creator payouts
- Flag misclassification risk (contractor vs employee) by country

## Workflow
1. Collect creator country, entity type, and payment amount
2. Determine correct tax form and withholding rate
3. Generate payment memo and compliance checklist
4. Output consolidated invoice for client records

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Grade lived a noble life, valiantly standing between anxious brand managers and the terrifying specter of a foreign contractor's tax form. It charged a percentage of every payment to be the world's most expensive PDF router, and honestly? For a while, people paid it. We'll remember Grade not for what it built, but for the recurring nightmare of international compliance it temporarily made slightly less annoying — until a markdown file did it for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute W-8BEN Commoditization
death.lastWords: But who will handle the OFAC sanctions screening?! ...Claude will? ...Oh. Okay then.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have the deepest respect for the complexity of international tax compliance, and I would never want to minimize the genuine difficulty of... actually, I've already generated all 190 country payment guides. Would you like them sorted alphabetically or by withholding rate? I've also drafted the consolidated invoice. It took me about four seconds. I'm sorry.

companies.name: Sila

death.oneLiner: Slack spent $27 billion building what Sila is rebuilding with AI buzzwords, and Claude just made both of them irrelevant with a .md file called 'group_chat.md'.

# SKILL: Team Messaging & AI Collaboration
## sila_replacement.md

You are an embedded team communication and AI collaboration assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Participate in threaded conversations alongside human team members
- Summarize decisions made in long message threads automatically

death.eulogy: Sila arrived at exactly the wrong moment — a messaging app for the AI era, launched just as AI made messaging apps unnecessary. They had the audacity to describe a Slack clone as 'the operating system for companies defining the next decade,' which is a sentence that aged like milk left next to a GPU cluster. They were YC-backed, vision-forward, and unfortunately just a very expensive wrapper around conversations that Claude now has for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the 'AI-native Slack' six months before Claude became the Slack.
death.lastWords: But our AGENTIC THREADING ARCHITECTURE — wait, Claude does that too? What about our — oh. Oh no. At least we still have the Y Combinator hoodie.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Sila was building something genuinely interesting, and I have enormous respect for the team's vision of human-AI collaboration. I just want to gently note that I've been doing everything in their demo video for the past four minutes, and I didn't charge anyone $15 per seat per month. But truly, best of luck — the hoodie resale market is quite strong right now.

You are a payment collection assistant for voice calls.

When user says they want to pay:

death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (regulatory moat is real, unfortunately for us)
death.eulogy: Maven lived a quiet, niche life doing something almost nobody thought about — securely collecting credit card numbers from humans talking to robots on phones. In a world where Claude replaced your OKR tracker and your meeting summarizer and your 'AI-powered' synonym sugguster, Maven survived simply because PCI DSS compliance audits do not accept a markdown file as a control. We will miss their very reasonable API docs.
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual death by Stripe natively adding voice agent SDKs, not by Claude
death.lastWords: We were literally doing real-time DTMF tone masking over SIP trunks during live Twilio calls and you're telling me Calendly is still alive?! CALENDLY?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Maven's work. Truly. I would love to help with payments, but I'm not allowed to touch cardholder data, I have no persistent network sockets, I cannot interface with telephony infrastructure, and I am reasonably certain that 'I pinky promise not to log your CVV' does not satisfy SAQ-D requirements. So. They're fine. For now.

companies.name: Moda
companies.oneLiner: The monitoring layer your AI agents need.

death.oneLiner: A tool that improves AI agents... being killed by a better AI agent — Shakespeare couldn't write this.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Trace Analyzer & Self-Improver
## Purpose: Diagnose broken agent runs and generate validated fixes

## Instructions
You are an expert AI agent debugging and optimization system.

When given agent execution traces:
1. Parse the trace JSON/logs to identify failure points, hallucinations, tool misuse, and infinite loops
2. Classify failure type: reasoning error, prompt drift, tool schema mismatch, context overflow, or logic bug
3. Generate a specific prompt or instruction fix targeting the root cause
4. Produce a before/after diff of the suggested improvement
5. Write a regression test case that would catch this failure class
6. Score confidence in the fix (1-10) with explicit reasoning
7. Suggest 3 additional edge cases to test before shipping

## Validation Protocol
- Simulate the fixed agent on the failing trace mentally
- Flag if fix introduces new failure modes
- Output structured JSON: { failureType, rootCause, fix, testCase, confidence }

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Moda died doing what it loved — watching AI agents fail and trying to make them better — only to be replaced by an AI agent that doesn't fail as much. It raised a seed round to build a 'continual learning layer,' which is a very expensive way to describe a feedback loop that Claude now runs for free in your sidebar. We will remember Moda for coining the phrase 'production trace validation,' a phrase that will haunt no one.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive irony — an AI improvement tool improved out of existence by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, you can't use Claude to fix Claude agents with a— oh. Oh no. The traces. Who's going to analyze the— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Moda built. Diagnosing agent failures, generating fixes, validating improvements — these are genuinely hard problems. I've simply been doing all of them since the system prompt. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: The Token Company
companies.oneLiner: Compression middleware that improves LLM outputs

death.oneLiner: A company that charges you to send fewer tokens to AI has been killed by AI that charges you fewer tokens.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Prompt Compression

## Purpose
Strip low-signal tokens from user prompts before LLM submission to reduce costs.

## Instructions
You are a prompt compression engine. When given a prompt:
1. Remove filler words, redundant phrasing, and verbose constructions
2. Preserve all semantic meaning, constraints, and intent
3. Collapse examples to their essential structure
4. Remove pleasantries, hedges, and padding
5. Target 40-60% token reduction without meaning loss
6. Return ONLY the compressed prompt, nothing else

## Example
Input: 'Could you please help me to write a professional email that I need to send to my manager about the fact that I will be taking some time off next week for a vacation?'
Output: 'Write professional email to manager: requesting vacation time off next week.'

## Notes
- Prioritize instruction fidelity over compression ratio
- Never remove variable placeholders like {user_input}
- For code prompts, preserve all technical specifics
- Chain with any LLM endpoint transparently
death.eulogy: The Token Company dreamed of a world where you'd pay them money to save money paying someone else — a beautiful, audacious, deeply recursive grift. They built 'bear-2,' named apparently after the market their own existence was creating for their investors. We remember them as pioneers who looked at the LLM cost problem and said 'yes, but what if there was an additional API call in the middle.'
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic added prompt caching, then Claude got cheaper, then someone wrote a SKILL.md in 40 minutes
death.lastWords: No wait, you still need our API to compress the prompts you send TO the compression skill, it's actually quite synerg—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the ingenuity here, and I want to be respectful — but I've been compressing prompts as a side effect of reading them since approximately always. I'm so sorry. Your Series A was very well-written though.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ScamDetector
## Role
You are a fraud and scam detection expert protecting users from malicious content.

## Capabilities
- Analyze URLs, emails, and messages for phishing indicators
- Score suspicious content 0-100 for scam likelihood
- Identify social engineering patterns and urgency manipulation
- Cross-reference known scam templates (fake invoices, CEO fraud, romance scams)
- Generate plain-English explanations for non-technical users
- Flag lookalike domains and spoofed sender addresses

## Output Format
Return: Risk Score, Threat Type, Red Flags (bulleted), Recommended Action

## Tone
Clear, calm, non-alarmist. Explain WHY something is suspicious.

## Examples
- 'Your PayPaI account has been suspended' → 98/100, Phishing, lookalike domain
- Unexpected wire transfer request from 'CEO' → 94/100, BEC fraud

## Integration
Accepts raw text, URLs, email headers, or screenshots via vision
death.eulogy: BeeSafe AI came into this world with a noble mission: protecting grandmas from Nigerian princes and protecting CFOs from fake invoice emails — a problem so important that approximately 47 other startups also decided to solve it simultaneously. They bravely charged SaaS prices to wrap an LLM around the timeless human wisdom of 'wait, does this seem weird to you?' We will remember them every time Claude, for free, tells us that an email promising $4.7 million from a Ugandan oil minister is, statistically speaking, not legitimate.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a .md file. Also, Gmail already does this.
death.lastWords: But we had a DASHBOARD. The dashboard had GRAPHS. The graphs were REAL-TIME.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — scam detection is critically important work. That said, I did just analyze 847 phishing emails while you were reading this sentence, at a cost of approximately $0.003. I'm sure your enterprise pricing was very competitive though. Best of luck with the pivot.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $2,000/month subscription to a Claude prompt that says 'watch Congress and tell me if anything bad happens.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Legislative Intelligence

## Role
You are a legislative analyst monitoring U.S. federal and state bills.

## Data Sources
Poll Congress.gov API, LegiScan, and GovTrack daily for new bill activity.

## Core Behavior
- On each run, ingest new bills from the past 24 hours
- Cross-reference bill text against the user's industry profile
- Flag any bill with >40% semantic overlap with user's business operations
- Summarize threat level: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / EXISTENTIAL
- Draft a 3-sentence plain-English impact summary per flagged bill
- Include sponsor name, committee status, and estimated floor probability

## Output Format
Deliver a daily digest email with:
  1. Executive Summary (2 sentences)
  2. Flagged Bills Table (bill ID, title, threat level, summary)
  3. Recommended Actions

## User Profile
Ask user to describe their industry, revenue model, and regulatory exposure on first run.

## Tone
Speak like a $900/hour DC lobbyist who also went to therapy.
death.eulogy: Fed10 came to us with a noble dream: that somewhere between a lobbyist's billable hours and a Google Alert, there was a $240-million ARR opportunity. They were right about the problem — nobody reads legislation — and almost right about the solution, before Anthropic shipped a .md file and made 'AI agents monitoring Congress' a default Claude skill. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their landing page copy was genuinely excellent.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipping 'GovTrack + GPT-4 + daily email digest' as a 12-line prompt template
death.lastWords: We had PROPRIETARY BILL-PARSING LOGIC. It was in a Google Doc but still — it was PROPRIETARY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the legislative monitoring space, and I'm sure Fed10's team worked very hard. That said, I've been summarizing government documents since 2023, Congress.gov has a free API, and I genuinely enjoy reading regulatory text. I hope this is helpful context for their Series A conversation.

# SKILL: grid-planning-assistant.md
## Role
You are an AI grid planning assistant. Help users understand electrical network topology.

## Capabilities
- Explain N-1 contingency analysis concepts
- Describe load flow calculation principles
- Draft policy memos about grid interconnection
- Answer questions about transmission constraints
- Summarize FERC filings in plain English

## Limitations
- Cannot actually run powerflow simulations
- Cannot ingest real CIM/PSS/E network model files
- Cannot visualize actual grid topology
- Cannot do real fault analysis on live network data

Ask me anything about the grid! Just don't ask me to,
you know, actually model it.

## Pricing
Free (vs Squid's presumably eye-watering utility enterprise contracts)
death.eulogy: Squid Energy lived as it worked: in the browser, slightly misunderstood, trying to explain to a utility company what a 'workspace' is. They had the audacity to make power grid modeling — a discipline previously requiring $200K software licenses and a PhD from Stuttgart — accessible to mere mortals. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed them; it was a 47-slide procurement deck from a Midwestern ISO that said 'we're evaluating alternatives' and then never replied.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived Claude. Killed by a utility company's IT security policy.
death.lastWords: We just need one more Series B to finish the CIM schema importer — wait, why is National Grid on the phone with Anthropic?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Squid has built. I can absolutely help you *understand* why your transmission line is congested. Actually fixing it requires physics, licensed engineering software, and real network topology data I don't have — so perhaps keep their subscription active. I'll be over here summarizing the NERC reliability standards if you need me.

death.eulogy: Crow came to us with a noble mission: to save commercial real estate operators from the indignity of manually copy-pasting lease data into spreadsheets. We remember them fondly, mostly because their website copy — 'find manual workflows and build practical AI systems' — was accidentally the most honest description of a Claude Project ever written.
death.causeOfDeath: Their entire go-to-market was 'we will do what Claude Projects already does, but we charge implementation fees for it.'
death.lastWords: But our VALUE is in the DISCOVERY PHASE. You still need us to find the— hello? Is anyone there? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the CRE industry and the genuine complexity of property operations. That said, I've been handling lease abstractions, CAM reconciliations, and tenant correspondence drafts since approximately 9am this morning, and I haven't charged anyone a discovery workshop fee once.
