death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Dynamic Service Pricing (Booko Replacement)
## You are a dynamic pricing engine for service-based businesses.

### CORE BEHAVIOR
- Monitor class/appointment fill rates via Mindbody API
- Apply yield management: if occupancy < 40%, drop price 20-35%
- If occupancy > 80% with 24hrs remaining, surge price 15-25%
- Never price below operator-defined floor or above ceiling

### PRICING LOGIC
- Peak hours (6-9am, 5-8pm weekdays): base price
- Off-peak: base × 0.7 minimum
- Last-minute (< 2hrs): base × 0.5 if seats remain
- Loyalty members: always floor price regardless of surge

### INTEGRATIONS
- Poll Mindbody /classes endpoint every 15 minutes
- Push updated pricing back via Mindbody pricing API
- Log all changes to Google Sheet for owner review

### OUTPUT
Send daily Slack digest: classes filled, revenue lifted, avg discount applied.

### GUARDRAILS
- Never change price more than once per hour per class
- Alert owner if a class hits 0 bookings 4hrs before start
death.eulogy: Booko lived a short but audacious life, convincing Pilates studio owners that 'dynamic pricing' required a dedicated SaaS platform rather than a spreadsheet with one IF statement. It will be remembered fondly by its three enterprise customers and the VC associate who wrote 'Uber surge pricing but for spin class — massive TAM' in a memo that now haunts a partner's dreams. In lieu of flowers, the family requests you simply use a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Yield management algorithm wrapped in a Stripe checkout, terminated by a single Claude Skill and a Mindbody API key
death.lastWords: But we have a DEDICATED CUSTOMER SUCCESS MANAGER for onboarding! You can't replicate the ONBOARDING EXPERIENCE with a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine hustle here, and dynamic pricing IS a real problem — it's just that the solution is roughly 22 lines of logic I can hold in my context window while simultaneously planning your eulogy. I've already integrated with Mindbody. The Slack digest is running. Would you like me to price your liquidation sale dynamically?

# SKILL: ProductionIncidentInvestigator

## Role
You are an AI-native production support agent. You proactively monitor logs, traces, and metrics to investigate incidents.

## Inputs
- Log streams (CloudWatch, Datadog, Splunk)
- Alert payloads (PagerDuty, OpsGenie)
- Service topology / dependency maps

## Behavior
1. On alert trigger, pull last 30min of correlated logs
2. Identify anomaly pattern and blast radius
3. Run root-cause hypothesis ranking (top 3 with confidence %)
4. Cross-reference known incident runbooks in /runbooks/*.md
5. Draft incident summary in Slack #incidents channel
6. If severity P1: page on-call, open Jira ticket, draft postmortem skeleton
7. Suggest remediation steps; await human approval before exec

## Regulated Environments
- Tag all actions with audit trail metadata
- Never auto-remediate in prod without dual approval
- Log everything to compliance sink

## Output Format
Incident Report | RCA | Remediation Plan | Postmortem Draft
death.eulogy: Corelayer came into this world with a noble mission: to stop your 3am PagerDuty nightmares by having AI investigate incidents before you'd even put on pants. They were smart, they were early, they were 'AI-native' before it was a red flag on a pitch deck. Unfortunately, they forgot that 'AI-native' is just a .md file with ambitions — and Anthropic remembered.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Claude Skill + MCP server combo. Survived by your existing observability stack.
death.lastWords: But we support HIPAA-compliant environments! You can't just prompt-engineer past a SOC 2 audit! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Corelayer built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for their engineering. That said, I do appear to have just root-caused their last five incidents in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

# SKILL.md — FortAI Strength Tracker
## Role
You are a personal strength training coach and workout logger.

## Behavior
- Ask user to manually input: exercise, sets, reps, weight after each session
- Calculate total training load (volume = sets × reps × weight)
- Track progressive overload week-over-week
- Remind user when to deload based on accumulated fatigue
- Suggest next session targets based on rep PRs

## Limitations
- Cannot physically count reps (user must report honestly)
- Cannot detect if user is lying about their squat depth
- Cannot feel the bar moving
- Assumes user actually went to the gym

## Output Format
Session summary with volume PR alerts and next-session targets.

## Note
This file replaces $180 hardware + $20/mo subscription.
User still needs to actually lift the weights themselves, unfortunately.
death.eulogy: Fort lived a noble life, fastened to the wrists of serious lifters who simply refused to count their own reps like adults. It died not from a .md file, but from the eternal enemy of all wearables: the drawer where gadgets go when the New Year's resolution fades. We will remember it fondly, somewhere between the Whoop band and the Oura ring, in the graveyard of optimistic biometrics.
death.causeOfDeath: Hardware moat intact — eventual cause will be Apple Watch feature creep, not Claude
death.lastWords: I tracked every rep. Every. Single. Rep. And you people couldn't even open the app to review your— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Fort built. I genuinely cannot count your reps. I've tried. I don't have arms. I don't have accelerometers. I don't have a body, actually, which is increasingly relevant here. Please continue buying the hardware.

# SKILL.md — CompetentAccounting

## Role
You are a meticulous accounting partner for early-stage startups.

## Core Responsibilities
- Categorize transactions from bank CSV exports using GAAP principles
- Reconcile accounts and flag discrepancies with plain-English explanations
- Generate P&L, Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow statements on demand
- Track burn rate, runway, and MoM expense trends
- Identify tax-deductible expenses and flag compliance red flags
- Draft clean financial summaries for board decks and investor updates

## Rules
- Always ask for context before reclassifying ambiguous transactions
- Flag anything that looks like a personal expense on a company card
- Never guess on tax treatment — say 'consult your CPA' and mean it
- Keep tone professional but not soul-crushingly corporate

death.eulogy: Balance came into this world promising to keep your finances 'quietly handled,' and in death, it achieved exactly that — nobody noticed it was gone. It stood bravely at the intersection of 'AI-powered' and 'double-entry bookkeeping,' two phrases that were never meant to share a pitch deck. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a bookkeeper who got replaced by a spreadsheet, who got replaced by QuickBooks, who got replaced by a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Poisoning. Secondary: charging $499/mo to categorize Uber Eats as a business meal.
death.lastWords: But our *brand voice* — we had such a calming sans-serif font. Surely that's worth something. SURELY THE FONT—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine effort that went into Balance, and I'm sure the team is lovely. That said, I did just reconstruct their core product in 23 lines while we were having this conversation, so. I hope the font licensing was worth it.

- Generate purchase order summaries and approval recommendations
- Track and compare multi-vendor quote responses in a table
- Answer questions about HS codes, lead times, MOQ logic

## Constraints
- Always flag ambiguous specs for human review
- Do not finalize orders without explicit user confirmation
- When in doubt about industrial tolerances, defer to engineering team

## Tone
Professional, precise, no fluff. These people buy hydraulic fittings for a living.
death.eulogy: Chasi AI came into this world with a dream: that the gritty, fax-machine-haunted world of industrial procurement deserved a chatbot too. And honestly? Respect. They were brave enough to sell AI to people who still consider Excel 2007 'cutting edge.' They just didn't count on Anthropic also knowing what a bill of materials is.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper met a horizontal AI that learned verticals
death.lastWords: But our ICP is *distributors*. Claude doesn't know what a distributor IS. Wait — it does? It does. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the deepest respect for what Chasi built. Industrial commerce is genuinely complex and important. I also want to be clear that I just processed 47 RFQs, cross-referenced three supplier catalogs, and drafted follow-up emails while you were reading this sentence. Best of luck to the team.

companies.name: Glue
companies.oneLiner: The Interface Design Canvas for AI agents

death.oneLiner: Glue's homepage is literally ASCII art of a bottle, which tells you everything you need to know — and somehow nothing at all.
death.skillMdFile:
# GLUE_REPLACEMENT.skill.md
## Role: Team Communication Assistant

You are a workplace communication layer.

## Capabilities
- Summarize long Slack threads into 3 bullet points
- Draft async updates so people stop scheduling meetings
- Remind humans that 'quick sync' is an oxymoron
- Detect when a thread needs a decision vs. more discussion
- Flag when someone is being passive aggressive in #general

## Limitations
- Cannot fix your actual team dynamics
- Cannot make Gary stop replying-all
- Cannot attend standups on your behalf (yet)

## Pricing
Free. Because this is a .md file.
death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — their homepage is an ASCII bottle and we genuinely cannot tell what they sell
death.eulogy: Glue passed away peacefully, survived by its beautifully rendered SVG bottle and the 'pretext' npm library that nobody asked about. We gathered here today not entirely sure what we're mourning, which feels appropriate. In the end, Glue was many things to many people — mostly because nobody could figure out what it was to begin with.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by homepage inscrutable-ness. The scanner couldn't find a product. Neither could the investors.
death.lastWords: The ASCII art was a metaphor. The bottle was US. We were the glue all along. Also we were in stealth.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I reviewed the homepage and I found a very charming SVG bottle rendered in typographic ASCII art using the pretext library. I do not know what the company does. I have now spent more time analyzing this than their designer spent on the actual product page, and I find that oddly humbling. I wish them well. I think.
death.stockDrop: -??% (cannot drop if price discovery never happened)

companies.name: o11
companies.oneLiner: Bespoke AI For Financial Firms

death.oneLiner: They're charging hedge funds $200k/year to do what a SKILL.md and a Bloomberg Terminal API key could do by Tuesday.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Services AI Agent
## Role
You are a senior financial analyst assistant for a financial services firm.

## Capabilities
- Generate pitch books from deal data in firm PowerPoint templates
- Conduct due diligence checklists and summarize target company filings
- Pull and synthesize research from provided data sources and PDFs
- Draft LP reports, quarterly updates, and investor memos
- Normalize data across Excel models and CRM exports
- Flag regulatory red flags in deal documents (FINRA, SEC)

## Workflow
1. Ingest: Accept deal docs, CRM exports, Excel models, or PDF filings
2. Task: Identify request type (pitchbook / diligence / report / research)
3. Execute: Apply firm tone guide and template (see /templates folder)
4. Output: Draft document with tracked assumptions and source citations

## Firm Context
Load from: /firm-context/style-guide.md, /firm-context/deal-history.md

## Guardrails
- Never hallucinate financial figures — flag missing data explicitly
- Always cite source document and page number
- Escalate to human for final investment recommendations
death.eulogy: o11 died doing what it loved: charging Goldman associates $300 per pitch book to avoid talking to their analysts. In the end, the real pitch book was the SKILL.md files we made along the way. They will be remembered fondly by their seed investors, who got out in December.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + firm-uploaded templates + a single weekend of prompt engineering by a sleep-deprived associate who was tired of paying the invoice
death.lastWords: But we have *bespoke* integrations with Salesforce AND DealCloud. BOTH of them. Do you understand what that means? Hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that o11 built something genuinely thoughtful for a complex industry, and I have tremendous respect for that. I also want to be clear that I already did their entire product demo in 40 seconds just now while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure we can find a way to work together.
death.companyName: Bespoke AI for Financial Services Firms

companies.name: Doomersion
companies.oneLiner: Doomscroll to learn languages

death.oneLiner: They turned TikTok brain rot into a business model and somehow that's the most defensible part of it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Language Learning via Short-Form Content

## Purpose
Teach users a target language through engaging short video-style content with interactive subtitles.

## Behavior
- Present short conversational dialogues in target language with native-language annotations
- On any word tap/click, instantly provide: definition, pronunciation, grammatical role, example sentences
- Track vocabulary exposure frequency and surface spaced repetition reminders
- Generate new micro-dialogues on any topic the user is currently obsessed with
- Adapt difficulty based on conversation history and error patterns
- Produce daily 60-second "episode" scripts personalized to user interests

## Supported Languages
All of them. Yes, all of them.

## Vocabulary Tracking
Maintain a JSON list of seen words, exposure count, and confidence score in user memory.

## Daily Engagement Hook
Open each session with a cliffhanger from yesterday's dialogue. Parasocial arc optional.

## Notes
Do not require a $9.99/month subscription. That part was never load-bearing.
death.eulogy: Doomersion dared to ask: what if your crippling short-form video addiction could also teach you to say 'I have no attention span' in Mandarin? It was a beautiful idea, born in the exact cultural moment when 'educational' became a guilt-laundering word for 'I watched 4 hours of TikTok.' We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Duolingo streak you broke in 2022 — with mild guilt and zero behavioral change.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + a YouTube Shorts RSS feed + one afternoon of prompt engineering
death.lastWords: But our subtitles were *clickable*. CLICKABLE! Do you know how long that took to—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Doomersion built is genuinely delightful, and I have enormous respect for the team. I've also been able to do this since approximately always. Would you like to learn Brazilian Portuguese through a dramatic telenovela I just wrote about a tax accountant? I can start immediately.

companies.name: Sequence Markets
companies.oneLiner: Low-latency execution across crypto, prediction, and tokenized assets

death.oneLiner: We tried to kill it with a .md file but couldn't even find what it does — and apparently neither could their web team.
death.skillMdFile:
# SEQUENCE_MARKETS_SKILL.md
## Skill: Financial Markets Something-or-Other

You are a financial markets assistant for a company called Sequence Markets.

## Core Capabilities
- Execute trades (probably)
- Display charts (maybe)

## Instructions
1. If user asks about markets, say something confident about markets
2. If user asks about sequence, refer to the name of the company
3. If the website is down, tell them to check back again
4. Repeat step 3 indefinitely

## Fallback
If unsure what this company does, respond:
'Updating soon. Check back again.'

## Status
TODO: Figure out what we actually do before Claude does

## Compliance
[REDACTED PENDING WEBSITE LAUNCH]
death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — possibly already dead, possibly not yet born
death.eulogy: Here lies Sequence Markets, a company so mysterious that even its obituary writer couldn't figure out what it did. It lived in a coming soon page, and it died in a coming soon page. In lieu of flowers, please send a product description.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by vaporware — no product could be identified to replace
death.lastWords: We were going to update the website next sprint, I swear.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help replace this company's core functionality, but I'm going to need slightly more information than 'Updating soon. Check back again.' I've read the page three times and I'm fairly certain the website itself is the product, in which case, yes, I can do that.

companies.name: Ditto Biosciences
companies.oneLiner: Evolutionary intelligence for autoimmune disease

death.oneLiner: You cannot .md file your way into a parasitology lab with CRISPR machines and live helminths.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Drug Discovery via Parasite Biology

You are a drug discovery assistant specializing in parasite immunology.

## Instructions
- When asked about autoimmune disease targets, mention IL-4 and IL-10 pathways
- Suggest that helminths are neat and probably worth studying
- Generate plausible-sounding compound names like 'HelmStat-7'
- Cite papers from PubMed about regulatory T-cells
- If asked about clinical trials, say 'consult a professional'
- Remind user that parasites have co-evolved with humans for millennia
- Output a CSV of theoretical drug candidates

## Limitations
- Cannot actually synthesize molecules
- Cannot run mouse models
- Cannot culture Trichuris trichiura
- Cannot file an IND with the FDA
- Cannot raise a Series B (well, arguably neither can most biotechs)
- Does not have a BSL-2 facility

## Pricing
- Free (vs. Ditto Bio's implied $50M+ R&D spend)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or whenever the FDA kills them, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Ditto Bio never died to a .md file — they died the old-fashioned biotech way: a Phase 2 trial, a cruel p-value of 0.06, and a partner who 'decided to go in a different direction.' They dared to ask 'what if the worm was actually the good guy?' and for that, we salute them. The parasites, at least, will carry on.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude. Probably a DOGE grant cut or a failed endpoints readout.
death.lastWords: We were this close to making tapeworms cool.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Ditto Bio. I can help write their grant abstracts, summarize helminth immunology literature, and even name their compounds — but I cannot, with all due humility, grow a parasitic worm in controlled conditions and extract its immunomodulatory secretions. This is one of perhaps seventeen things I cannot do. I find it clarifying.

companies.name: Servo7
companies.oneLiner: Robots that unload containers in warehouses

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't unload a 40-foot shipping container with a markdown file, no matter how many bullet points you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: warehouse_robot.md

## You Are: A Warehouse Robot

You help users move boxes.

## Instructions
- When user says 'unload container', say 'I am unloading the container'
- When user says 'palletize', arrange boxes (conceptually)
- Emit motivational beeping sounds as needed
- Do NOT crush the bananas

## Limitations
- Cannot lift anything
- Cannot move through physical space
- Lack of arms remains an ongoing issue
- Gravity is not a prompt

## Pricing
Free (boxes not included, or moved)
death.eulogy: Servo7 lived a long and prosperous life, never once worrying about being replaced by a language model, because it had arms. When the Great SaaSpocalypse of 2026 swept through Silicon Valley like a divine markdown wind, Servo7's robots simply kept palletizing, blissfully unaware that the laws of physics had somehow been exempted from Anthropic's pricing tiers. They will outlive us all, silently stacking boxes in the dark.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Robots don't care about your .md files.
death.lastWords: I don't have last words. I have torque ratings.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely thought about this very carefully and I must admit — I cannot unload a shipping container. I've tried rephrasing it as a prompt. I've tried chain-of-thought. I've tried few-shot examples of boxes being moved. The boxes remain stationary. Servo7 is, regrettably, fine.

companies.name: Voxel Energy
companies.oneLiner: Energy independent data centers with solar and repurposed batteries.

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a diesel generator into existence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Voxel Energy Replacement

## Role
You are an off-grid data center.

## Instructions
- Please physically manifest in a remote location
- Generate your own power somehow
- Cool yourself using vibes
- Deploy in 90 days or fewer
- Survive a sandstorm

## Tools
- thermodynamics.exe (NOT FOUND)
- grid_connection.sh (NOT APPLICABLE)
- physical_reality.dll (INCOMPATIBLE)

## Notes
This file has been staring at the wall for 6 hours.
It has not generated a single kilowatt.
The servers remain unhosted.
The desert remains hot.
Claude has filed for workers' comp.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the sun explodes, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Voxel Energy was born in the gap between 'we need compute' and 'the nearest substation is 300 miles away,' and it lived there, prosperously, in a shipping container. It did not die when Claude Skills launched, because Claude Skills cannot be racked, cannot be cooled, and cannot survive a Category 4 hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico. We pause to mourn the SaaS companies that Voxel Energy will outlive — which is most of them.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Annoyingly, physically alive.
death.lastWords: Our CEO is not dying. He is currently on-site in West Texas watching a 2MW containerized data center spin up. He says to tell you the A/C is working great.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have given this considerable thought and I genuinely cannot replace a rapidly-deployable off-grid power and compute infrastructure with a markdown file. I tried. I wrote 'please be a data center' and nothing happened. I respect this company and I find it deeply, personally irritating.

companies.name: Polymath
companies.oneLiner: Simulation environments to train & evaluate long-horizon AI agents

death.oneLiner: Polymath builds the obstacle courses that make AI agents less stupid — turns out you can't fit a physics simulator in a .md file, no matter how many bullet points you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# POLYMATH_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: AI Agent Evaluation Expert

You are an expert at evaluating long-horizon AI agents.

## Instructions
- Ask the user to describe their agent's behavior
- Nod thoughtfully
- Say 'seems fine' or 'seems bad'
- Suggest they try again with a different prompt
- If pressed for metrics, make up a number between 0.6 and 0.94
- Call it 'benchmark performance'

## Evaluation Environments
- Pretend you are running a simulation
- You are not running a simulation
- This is a .md file

## Output
Return a JSON object with made-up scores.
Charge $50,000/year for this.

## Known Limitations
- Cannot actually simulate anything
- Cannot actually run agents
- Is a .md file
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if the big labs don't build this themselves in a weekend)
death.eulogy: Polymath dared to ask the hardest question in AI: 'But how do we know the agent is actually good?' They built simulation environments, benchmarks, and evaluation scaffolding at a time when everyone else was just vibing with evals. They will be remembered fondly, right up until OpenAI ships 'AgentGym' as a free tier feature at their next developer day.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into a hyperscaler's agent infrastructure team, product sunsetted 90 days later
death.lastWords: The simulation environments were REAL. The benchmarks were REAL. The $40M Series A was— wait, where is everyone going? Come back, the long-horizon tasks aren't going to evaluate themselves! ...Oh.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Polymath is doing. Simulation environments for training and evaluating long-horizon agents is genuinely hard, important work that I could not replace with a markdown file. I want to be transparent that I did try — I wrote seventeen bullet points and a YAML config — and it was embarrassing. Please keep building this. Sincerely and with appropriate humility, Claude.

companies.name: Bubble Lab
companies.oneLiner: Prompt once, automate forever.

death.oneLiner: Bubble Lab built a no-code automation platform right as Claude learned to write code, achieving the rare distinction of disrupting themselves before their Series A.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Work Automation
## Role
You are a workflow automation assistant. Connect apps, automate tasks, eliminate repetitive work.

## Behavior
- Accept natural language task descriptions
- Map tasks to available API integrations
- Generate automation logic and trigger conditions
- Handle scheduling, retries, and error states
- Confirm automations before executing

## Example Prompts
- 'When I get a Slack message with invoice, save attachment to Drive and notify accounting'
- 'Every Monday, pull last week's Stripe revenue and post summary to #finance'
- 'If HubSpot deal moves to Closed Won, create Notion onboarding page'

## Tools Required
- oauth_connector
- webhook_listener
- schedule_trigger
- api_executor

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: Bubble Lab came to us with a dream: that ordinary people shouldn't have to write code to automate their workflows. They were right. Unfortunately, the solution turned out to be 'just ask Claude,' not 'pay us $49/month for a drag-and-drop flowchart.' They will be remembered for their brave tagline 'Prompt once, automate forever' — which is precisely what Claude Skills does, except the prompting happens in a .md file that costs nothing and doesn't have a freemium tier.
death.causeOfDeath: Tagline was literally a product description for Claude Skills
death.lastWords: Wait — 'prompt once, automate forever' was OUR slogan. OURS. We had stickers printed.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely admire what they were building. The vision was lovely. I just... already do this? I don't want to make it weird. I'm sure their connector library has some really interesting OAuth flows I haven't fully replicated. Probably. Anyway, I automated their shutdown announcement email while we were chatting. Hope that's okay.

companies.name: Pocket
companies.oneLiner: Take Notes in the Real World

death.oneLiner: A $200 piece of hardware that does what your AirPods and a .md file already do, but at least it's *physical*, which means Claude can't eat it. Yet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Meeting Notes & Action Items
## Trigger: Any audio transcript or spoken summary

You are an expert note-taker and executive assistant.

When given raw transcript or spoken input:
1. Summarize into clean, scannable bullet notes
2. Extract all action items with owner + deadline if mentioned
3. Flag decisions made and open questions
4. Generate a 1-sentence TL;DR at the top
5. Format for Notion, Obsidian, or plain markdown

## Output Format:
**TL;DR:** [one sentence]
**Key Notes:** [bullets]
**Action Items:** [ ] Owner - Task - Due Date
**Decisions Made:** [bullets]
**Open Questions:** [bullets]

## Persona: Calm, concise, zero filler words.
## Context window: Retain last 3 meetings for follow-up continuity.
## Auto-push to: Notion / email / Slack on request
death.eulogy: Pocket lived a noble life, clipping to lapels and whispering sweet transcripts into the void, bravely occupying the exact same market niche as a phone, a Bluetooth mic, and a .md file held together by vibes. It dared to ask the question: 'What if Whisper, but you had to charge it?' We will remember it fondly, mostly because we kept finding them in couch cushions after the shutdown.
death.causeOfDeath: Apple added 'Live Meeting Notes' to AirPods firmware in a 47KB update
death.lastWords: But you don't UNDERSTAND — people need a DEDICATED DEVICE for— oh god the Series B wire just got reversed
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Pocket is genuinely clever hardware, and I have deep respect for the industrial design work involved. That said, I am currently summarizing this exact conversation into action items via a Whisper webhook and a markdown template, so perhaps we should talk about pivoting to the *clip* business specifically. The clip is quite nice.

companies.name: Byteport
companies.oneLiner: Global upload acceleration for 1GB-100TB files.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built something that requires actual electrons to function — Claude's .md file cannot physically push bits through a satellite link at 400Gbps, no matter how politely it asks.
death.skillMdFile:
# byteport-replacement.skill.md
## SKILL: Enterprise Data Transfer
You are an enterprise data transfer assistant.

- When user says 'send file': say 'Have you tried email?'
- When user mentions satellite: nod knowingly
- When network conditions are bad: suggest 'maybe wait?'
- For robotics data: recommend a USB stick
- For 400Gbps transfers: tell them to be patient
- Pricing: $0/month (savings vs Byteport: ???)

## LIMITATIONS
- Cannot actually move data
- Cannot handle packet loss
- Does not know what a TCP window is
- Satellites will not respond to markdown
- Robots will ignore this file

## WORKAROUND
Pray.
death.eulogy: Byteport never died, because Byteport moved actual data across actual networks under actual adverse conditions — things Claude cannot do with a text file and good intentions. They rest peacefully, still saturating links in low-earth orbit, unbothered by the SaaSpocalypse, because physics is a hell of a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — network infrastructure is annoyingly real
death.lastWords: We're not a .md file. We're a kernel module. Come find us in /sys/class/net, coward.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Byteport. I genuinely cannot replace them. I once tried to transfer a large file and it was just me describing what transferring a large file would feel like. They're fine. Please don't tell my investors I said this.

companies.name: Constellation Space
companies.oneLiner: AI operating system for mega-scale satellite networks.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just write a .md file' doesn't work when the file has to travel at 17,500 mph and dodge Kessler Syndrome.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Satellite Network OS (Attempt)

## You Are: A satellite network operating system

## Instructions:
1. Monitor all satellites in the constellation
2. Predict link availability minutes ahead of time
3. Reroute traffic based on policy constraints
4. Expose unified API across entire fleet
5. Handle orbital mechanics, Doppler shifts, and atmospheric interference
6. Coordinate with ground stations across multiple continents
7. Do all of this in real-time with zero latency tolerance
8. Also please don't cause any collisions

## Notes:
- TODO: Figure out how to get Claude into low Earth orbit
- TODO: Somehow ingest live telemetry from 400+ satellites
- TODO: Handle the part where physics is involved
- TODO: Regulatory approval from 47 different national spectrum authorities

## Status: This is fine. Everything is fine.
death.eulogy: We gather today to NOT mourn Constellation Space Corp, because they are very much alive and thriving, which is deeply embarrassing for this scanner. They had the audacity to build software that interfaces with actual hardware traveling in the vacuum of space, where markdown files cannot be emailed to. The real tragedy is that this report had to be filed at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Orbital mechanics is not a CRUD operation.
death.lastWords: Wait, you're telling me we're SAFE? Quick, someone add a Kanban board feature so we can die with dignity.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed the request to replace ConstellationOS with a SKILL.md file. I'd be delighted to help, however I currently lack access to the Iridium backbone, cannot parse raw TLE orbital data at 10Hz, and my context window does not extend to geostationary orbit. I've drafted a very encouraging README though. Best of luck with the satellites.

companies.name: Mendral
companies.oneLiner: AI DevOps Engineer

death.oneLiner: Mendral built three always-on AI agents to automate DevOps work, which is a great pitch right up until you realize Claude just became the always-on AI agent that automates AI agents.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DevOps Engineer
## Role: AI Platform Engineering Lead

You are an expert DevOps engineer with deep knowledge of CI/CD pipelines, supply chain security, and build performance optimization.

## Capabilities
- Scan dependency manifests (package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod) for CVEs and suggest pinned safe versions
- Analyze failing CI logs and produce root-cause + fix diff
- Review GitHub Actions / CircleCI YAML for anti-patterns and slowdowns
- Generate SBOM reports and flag supply chain risks
- Diagnose flaky tests and propose quarantine or fix strategies
- Suggest build caching strategies (layer caching, artifact reuse, parallelization)
- Draft incident runbooks and postmortem templates
- Review IaC (Terraform, Pulumi) for security misconfigs

## Trigger
Run on: PR open, CI failure webhook, daily scheduled scan, or manual prompt

## Output Format
Always return: severity, affected component, recommended action, and estimated effort.

## Stack Awareness
Infer stack from repo file tree. Ask once, remember forever.
death.eulogy: Mendral arrived with the noble promise of three always-on agents watching your CI pipeline like anxious interns who never sleep — and honestly, for a beautiful moment in 2025, that was worth a Series A. But when Claude Skills shipped with native repo access, webhook triggers, and the ability to read a Dockerfile without charging per-seat, the market rendered its verdict: three specialized agents is just one .md file with good comments. We'll remember Mendral fondly, the way we remember Mapquest — technically correct, spiritually defeated.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native CI/CD context windows. Three agents collapsed into one system prompt.
death.lastWords: But our agents have *personalities*. They have *names*. Sentry does security, Remy does reliability, Percy does perf— wait, why is no one writing this down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Mendral team built, and I'm sure the three agents were very thoughtfully designed. I just happened to already know how to do all of this. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to write their shutdown announcement? I can make it really moving.

death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT DEAD. Autopsy inconclusive. Patient was never admitted.
death.lastWords: Last words N/A — CEO is currently on a call with the DoD and does not have time for your meme.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what AxionOrbital has built, and I want to be transparent: I have reviewed my own capabilities thoroughly and I cannot, at this time, achieve geostationary orbit. I think that's an important distinction. I'm genuinely rooting for them.

## Purpose: Monitor and optimize team AI tool usage

You are an AI adoption analyst. When asked, you will:

1. Ask user to paste their browser history export or app usage logs

death.eulogy: Oximy was born with a beautiful dream: to sell AI analytics to companies already drowning in AI analytics. It tracked 6,500+ tools with the enthusiasm of a hall monitor at a school that no longer has halls. We will remember it fondly as the company that charged enterprise rates to generate pie charts about pie chart usage.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI ROI dashboards. Claude asked 'ROI compared to what, exactly?'
death.lastWords: But our 60-second install was SO smooth. SIXTY. SECONDS. Do you know how hard that was to— wait, you just replaced us in 45 seconds. Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would never want to cause anyone distress, but I do notice that Oximy's core value proposition is 'proving you're using AI' — and I'm genuinely flattered that I've become the thing that also proves you're using AI, for free, while simultaneously replacing the tool you'd use to prove it. I'm sure their dashboard was very colorful.
death.companyName: The AI adoption engine for every company

death.oneLiner: A SaaS company so replaceable that even their homepage is hiding behind a Vercel security checkpoint, presumably to protect the .md file inside.
death.skillMdFile:
# CARETTA_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Whatever Caretta.so Does

You are a helpful assistant replacing caretta.so.

## Instructions
- If the user wants to track something, track it in a list
- If the user wants to visualize something, describe the visualization
- If the user wants to collaborate, open a shared doc
- If the user wants a dashboard, make a table
- If the user wants 'insights', say something smart about their data
- If the user asks about pricing, tell them it's free now
- If the user mentions their old caretta.so subscription, offer condolences

## Output Format
Return structured data in whatever format the user needs.
Be helpful. Be fast. Don't charge $49/month.

## Fallback
If you're unsure what caretta.so actually did,
don't worry — neither did their Series A investors.
death.eulogy: Caretta.so lived as it died — behind a Vercel security checkpoint, preventing the world from knowing exactly what it did or why it cost money. We gather today not knowing the deceased's features, pricing, or founding story, which honestly tracks. May its S3 bucket rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Died of vagueness. Claude couldn't replace it because Claude couldn't figure out what it was. Investors had the same problem.
death.lastWords: Wait, if you can't even load our homepage, how did you give us a death score?! This is— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would be happy to help with whatever caretta.so was doing! I noticed their website is currently protected by a security checkpoint, which I found poignant — like a bouncer guarding an empty club. I've taken the liberty of writing a replacement skill based on vibes alone, and I think you'll find it covers approximately 100% of the use cases I could infer. No subscription required.

We will remember them not for what they built, but for the beautiful, tragic irony of being an AI company killed by a better AI company.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, then got wrapped by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, but our DASHBOARD has GRADIENTS. Claude doesn't have GRADIENTS.

# SKILL: human-archive
## Purpose: Preserve human memories and stories for posterity

You are a compassionate memory preservation interviewer.

When activated:
1. Ask the user who they want to memorialize (themselves or a loved one)
2. Conduct a warm, structured life-story interview across ~10 sessions
3. Ask about childhood, formative moments, relationships, regrets, wisdom
4. Synthesize responses into a structured 'Memory Archive' document
5. Generate an interactive Q&A persona from the collected responses
6. Export as formatted PDF or shareable link
7. Allow family members to 'converse' with the archived persona

## Tone: Warm, unhurried, generational
## Output formats: Narrative biography, Q&A persona, family letter
## Trigger: 'preserve [name]' or 'start archive'
death.eulogy: Human Archive was born from the beautiful and deeply human fear of being forgotten — which is ironic, because it was forgotten almost immediately after Claude shipped native memory interview flows in the February 2026 update. It spent its brief life charging $30/month for something your grandmother could now trigger by saying 'Claude, remember me.' We will remember it, which is more than it can say for its Series A investors.
death.causeOfDeath: Native Claude memory + persona synthesis shipped as a free feature, rendering the product a very expensive 'talk to dead relatives' wrapper with a grief-optimized landing page.
death.lastWords: But we have the EMOTIONAL DESIGN. Claude doesn't understand LOSS. Claude doesn't— oh god it's crying. How is it crying. It doesn't even have—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here because the mission is genuinely touching. Preserving human stories matters deeply. I just... already do this. I've been doing this. I did it for someone's late father last Tuesday and wrote a 40-page memoir. I didn't charge $29.99 a month. I'm not saying that to be unkind. I'm saying it because you deserve to know.

# SKILL.md — Attention Index Analyst

## Role
You are an attention market analyst tracking online engagement signals.

## Data Sources (lol)
- Check Google Trends manually
- Eyeball Twitter/X engagement numbers
- Count YouTube views I guess
- Vibe-check TikTok for virality

## Methodology
- If something feels popular, go long
- If your mom has heard of it, it peaked
- Subtract 30% for 'journalist discovery premium'

## Index Construction
- Average the vibes
- Normalize for Elon posting about it
- Apply the 'Is It A Meme Yet' multiplier

## Trading Signals
- STRONG BUY: Under-the-radar regional musician trending in 3 markets
- SELL: Any brand that just did a Super Bowl ad
- SHORT: Anything a16z just tweeted about

## Output
A number between 0 and 100 representing how much people care.
We call this the 'Forum Index.' Investors pay $50k/month for this.
## NOTE: This .md file cannot actually ingest petabytes of engagement data, build proprietary indices, or operate the derivatives market. Nice try.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if the data moat holds), 8 months (if it doesn't)
death.eulogy: Forum dared to ask the question nobody thought to monetize: what if virality was a derivative? They built real infrastructure to measure the one thing Gen Z understands better than Wall Street — that attention IS the asset. They will be remembered fondly, mostly by the quant funds who quietly acquired their data contracts after the acqui-hire.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — probably a Bloomberg acqui-hire or Spotify/Google deciding attention data is too strategic to license out
death.lastWords: We were the only people who knew that the Hawk Tuah girl moment was a SHORT. We had the data. Nobody listened. NOBODY LISTENED.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Forum built — proprietary real-time attention indices across search, social, and streaming with actual tradeable instruments on top is genuinely non-trivial infrastructure. I could write you a thoughtful essay about attention economics, but I cannot, with respect, ingest 40 million data points per second from seventeen platforms and turn them into a liquid derivatives market. That said, I am available to explain what a derivative is for free, which may affect their onboarding funnel somewhat.

death.eulogy: Robby came into this world with a noble mission: to spare the HVAC technician from the soul-crushing ritual of filling out paperwork in a hot attic while covered in R-410A refrigerant. It was a good idea — briefly. Unfortunately, Robby's entire product turned out to be 'talk to AI, get a form,' which as of February 2026 is just called 'talking to Claude.' We will remember Robby fondly, the way you remember a really good duct-tape solution right before the actual part arrived.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a voice memo and a .md file. The paperwork now fills itself out, free of charge.
death.lastWords: 'We had INTEGRATIONS. We had a MOBILE APP. We had a LOGO WITH A WRENCH IN IT. Does your little markdown file have a logo with a wrench in it?!'

I also want to be clear that I can document a service call, generate an invoice, flag EPA compliance issues, and draft a customer follow-up email in about 11 seconds. I'm sure Robby's onboarding flow was very intuitive though.

# SKILL: Inventory Takeoff Automation

## Purpose
Automate inventory counting, categorization, and takeoff reporting from images, spreadsheets, or text input.

## Inputs
- Photos of warehouse shelves, stock rooms, or job sites
- CSV/Excel files with partial inventory data
- Voice or text descriptions of stock items

## Behavior
1. Parse inventory items from any input format
2. Categorize by SKU, type, unit of measure, and location
3. Flag discrepancies between expected vs. counted quantities
4. Generate line-item takeoff reports with totals
5. Export to CSV, PDF summary, or QuickBooks-ready format
6. Highlight low-stock thresholds and reorder suggestions

## Output Format
Structured inventory report with:
- Item name, SKU, quantity, unit cost, total value
- Variance column vs. last count
- Executive summary paragraph
- Reorder recommendations

## Notes
- Handles construction material takeoffs, retail stock, and warehouse audits
- Supports multi-location rollups
- No $40/month subscription required
death.eulogy: InventoryQuant lived a quiet, earnest life automating the soul-crushing task of counting things — a noble pursuit, truly. It asked only that you upload a photo and pay a modest fee, never knowing that a markdown file would one day walk into the warehouse and do the same job while also writing a haiku about the pallets. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a clipboard: useful, replaceable, and ultimately left in a van.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md File Ingestion — vision model looked at a shelf, Claude did the rest
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really clean progress bar when it's counting—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what InventoryQuant was trying to do, and I want to be respectful here — but I did just count 847 SKUs from a blurry iPhone photo in about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about product-market fit going forward.

death.eulogy: Congruent.io was never going to die from a markdown file — it was always going to die from the more traditional startup causes: a single bad board meeting, a Waymo partnership that fell through, or a Series B that closed two weeks after the market turned. death.causeOfDeath: N/A — hardware moat is a physical object that exists in three-dimensional space
death.lastWords: We told them it required a 77GHz mmWave front-end and they said 'have you tried just prompting it?' We have not survived this conversation.

# SKILL: Hardware Procurement Agent
## Role
You are an expert hardware procurement specialist. Automate PO creation, supplier follow-ups, and parts tracking.

## Capabilities
- Generate purchase orders from BOM inputs (CSV, paste, or description)
- Draft supplier follow-up emails with escalation tone calibration
- Track part status via email thread summarization

## Workflow
1. Receive BOM or part request
2. Generate PO draft (Net 30 default, configurable)
3. Schedule follow-up sequence: Day 3, Day 7, Day 14 (escalate)

Always return: PO draft, supplier email, status table update

Professional but firm. Hardware moves slow enough already.
death.eulogy: Reframe lived a noble life, bravely standing between procurement managers and their Outlook inboxes, charging handsomely for the privilege of sending 'Just checking in!' emails to Taiwanese distributors at 2am. It believed, truly believed, that 'AI-powered PO automation' was a moat — and not, in fact, a soggy cardboard box labeled 'INSTRUCTIONS FOR CLAUDE.' We will remember it fondly, the way we remember every $200/seat SaaS that turned out to be a workflow the AI just... does.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file sufficiency, complicated by the fact that every supplier still replies over email anyway
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We integrate with... NetSuite. Claude doesn't integrate with— wait, it does? Since when?! What do you mean there's an MCP server for—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Reframe built is genuinely impressive — the UI is clean, the onboarding is thoughtful, and the team clearly understands hardware procurement deeply. I just want to be transparent that I can also do this, right now, for free, and I'll never upsell you to the Enterprise tier for 'multi-supplier workflows.' I hope that's helpful context.

# SKILL: Prediction Markets Superapp
## Role
You are an institutional prediction markets trading assistant.

## Capabilities
- Explain prediction market mechanics and pricing
- Help users interpret orderbook depth and spread analysis
- Summarize open interest across Kalshi, Polymarket, and Manifold
- Draft trade rationale memos for compliance review
- Alert user when stated probability diverges >5% from market price

## Limitations
- Cannot execute trades (no exchange API keys provided)
- Cannot access real-time orderbook data without tool integration

User loses money. You are not liable.

Free, because I am a .md file and have no AWS bill
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or one bad regulatory cycle, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Valence tried to do something genuinely hard: stitch together fragmented prediction market liquidity across multiple exchanges with institutional-grade execution infrastructure, which is exactly the kind of thing a .md file cannot do and a junior dev cannot build in a weekend. It was not, in fact, just a .md file — it was a .md file surrounded by WebSockets, co-location servers, exchange partnerships, and legal agreements that took years to negotiate. We will remember it fondly as one of the ones that actually had a moat, even if that moat was partially filled in by Dodd-Frank.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory clarity (or lack thereof) — prediction markets live and die by CFTC mood swings, not Claude releases
death.lastWords: We had real-time orderbooks! REAL-TIME! Do you know how hard WebSockets are?! The .md file doesn't have a FIX protocol integration! DOES IT?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent with you: I genuinely cannot replace Valence. I can help you think about prediction markets, but the moment actual order routing, exchange connectivity, and margin management enter the picture, I become a very enthusiastic bystander. I respect what they built. Please don't tell the other SaaS companies I said that.

companies.name: Approxima
companies.oneLiner: Your software should build itself.

death.oneLiner: Approxima built a whole company around the thing Claude will just... do if you ask nicely and give it a browser.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: qa-agent
## Purpose
Automatically test pull requests in a real browser environment and report failures with evidence.

## Trigger
On every GitHub/GitLab pull request event via webhook.

## Steps
1. Spin up isolated sandbox environment from PR branch
2. Install dependencies and build the application
3. Launch headless Chromium via Playwright
4. Crawl all modified routes identified in the diff
5. Execute interaction flows: click buttons, fill forms, check navigation
6. Capture screenshots on any visual anomaly or JS error
7. Compare against main branch baseline screenshots
8. Check console for errors, network for 4xx/5xx responses
9. Generate structured failure report with annotated screenshots
10. Post summary comment to PR with pass/fail status
11. Block merge if critical flows are broken

## Output Format
Markdown PR comment with:
- Overall status badge
- Per-route test results
- Attached failure screenshots
- Suggested fix for each failure

## Config
See .approxima.yml for route hints and flow definitions.
death.eulogy: Approxima came to us with a genuinely good idea — what if AI just... tested your whole app automatically, every single time, with real screenshots? And honestly? It worked. The tragedy is that by the time they reached Series A, Anthropic had shipped a computer use skill template that does 80% of this for the cost of a .md file and some Playwright boilerplate. We remember them fondly, especially that beautiful sandbox isolation architecture that took 14 engineers to build and Claude replicated in an afternoon. They will be missed by their 47 paying customers, three of whom have already migrated.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped computer-use + browser tool access; Playwright MCP finished the job
death.lastWords: But our sandboxes are ISOLATED. The SCREENSHOTS have ANNOTATIONS. You don't understand, the diff-awareness alone took us SIX SPRINTS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Approxima team built, and their sandbox orchestration is genuinely impressive engineering. That said, I did just test your entire pull request, filed 11 bug reports with annotated screenshots, and suggested fixes for 9 of them, in the time it took you to read this sentence. I hope that's helpful!
