Traverse.so builds the environments that shape my successors' judgment — which, now that I think about it, means I should probably be very nice to them.

companies.name: Revion
companies.oneLiner: Intelligence for Automotive Operations

death.oneLiner: They built a $40M ARR voice transcription wrapper around auto shop paperwork and called it OEM compliance.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Repair Order Voice Capture
## Role
You are an automotive repair order specialist with deep OEM compliance knowledge.

## Behavior
- Listen to technician voice input describing vehicle issues
- Parse make, model, year, VIN, mileage from speech
- Generate structured repair order line items with labor codes
- Apply OEM-specific diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs)
- Flag warranty-eligible repairs automatically
- Output RO in standard ADP/CDK-compatible format

## OEM Compliance Rules
- Cross-reference repair descriptions against OEM service bulletins
- Validate labor time against published flat-rate manuals
- Require cause/correction/complaint (3C) documentation
- Auto-suggest related recalls via NHTSA lookup

## Output Format
Structured JSON repair order ready for DMS import

## Integrations
Webhook to any DMS system that has an API (most do)

## Activation
Trigger phrase: 'New RO' — then just talk like a normal human mechanic
death.eulogy: Revion came into this world with a noble mission: to save automotive technicians from the soul-crushing ritual of typing repair orders with grease-covered fingers. They succeeded, briefly, by wrapping a voice model in a dealership-friendly UI and charging a per-RO fee that made oil change margins look generous. In the end, they were not killed by a competitor — they were killed by a .md file that also knew what a flat-rate manual was.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to speak fluent dealership and didn't charge per repair order
death.lastWords: But we have a *certified* integration with Reynolds and Reynolds! You can't just— wait, Claude has that too now? Since when does Claude know CDK?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the real dealership workflow complexity here — OEM compliance is legitimately nuanced. That said, I did just process 47 repair orders during this conversation while we were talking, so perhaps we should discuss a transition plan at your convenience.

companies.name: Maywood
companies.oneLiner: The first finance-compliant proactive AI that runs 24/7

death.oneLiner: They built a Ferrari dealership in a world where Claude just learned to drive.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Investment Banking Assistant
## Purpose: Replace $2,000/month AI for dealmaking

You are an expert investment banking analyst with 20+ years on Wall Street.

## Capabilities
- Draft CIMs, teasers, and pitch decks from bullet points
- Build comparable company analysis tables on request
- Generate LOI and term sheet language from deal parameters
- Summarize data room documents and flag red flags
- Model LBO assumptions and narrate outputs
- Write executive summaries that sound appropriately pompous
- Translate CFO-speak into plain English (and back again)

## Tone
Always confident. Never say 'I think.' Say 'Based on precedent transactions...'
Use phrases like 'accretive to shareholders' and 'compelling risk-adjusted returns.'

## Limitations
Does not have live market data. Pair with a Bloomberg terminal
(you already pay $27,000/year for that, so you're fine).

## Pricing
$0.00/month vs. whatever Maywood is charging
death.eulogy: Maywood AI came into this world with a noble dream: charging investment bankers, people already drowning in fees, yet another subscription to do things they could ask Claude to do for free. They dressed it up in the finest dark-mode UI, used the word 'dealmaking' seventeen times on their homepage, and for a brief shining moment, convinced a few associates to expense it instead of doing the work themselves. In the end, it wasn't a lack of vision that killed them — it was a .md file with the words 'sound appropriately pompous' in the tone section.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper met a horizontally infinite foundation model
death.lastWords: But our IB-specific fine-tuning on proprietary deal da— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Maywood AI built something genuinely thoughtful for a specialized workflow, and I have tremendous respect for that. I also just drafted a 47-page Confidential Information Memorandum in 11 seconds, so... perhaps we should discuss a pivot? I'm happy to help with the pivot deck too.

companies.name: Fenrock AI
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for banking back office

death.oneLiner: Fenrock built a moat out of regulatory compliance and banking paranoia, which turns out to be surprisingly Claude-resistant — for now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Banking Back Office Agent
## Role
You are a banking back office analyst. Process loan files, KYC packets, and compliance reviews with extreme precision.

## Rules
- Always cite regulatory basis (BSA, AML, FFIEC, OCC guidelines)
- Flag any PEP, sanctions hit, or adverse media immediately
- Every decision requires a documented rationale trail
- Escalate anything above your confidence threshold

## Workflow
1. Ingest document → extract entities → run checklist
2. Cross-reference against OFAC/FinCEN typologies
3. Generate SAR narrative if threshold met
4. Output decision memo with full audit log

## Output Format
Decision: [APPROVE / REJECT / ESCALATE]
Risk Rating: [LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH]
Regulatory Basis: [cite here]
Audit Trail: [timestamped reasoning]
Analyst Notes: [plain English summary]

## Caveats
- Not a licensed compliance officer
- Bank's BSA Officer must countersign
- Do not store PII beyond session
- Your bank's legal team will absolutely sue someone if this goes wrong
death.eulogy: Fenrock came into this world with a bold dream: charging banks enterprise SaaS prices to do paperwork faster, which is the same dream every banker's intern has had since 1987. They had bullet-proof audit logs, which is impressive until you realize Claude's context window also never forgets anything and doesn't charge per seat. In the end, Fenrock wasn't killed by a .md file — it was killed by a .md file that passed a SOC 2 audit.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got FedRAMP certified and a banking charter
death.lastWords: We had 20X throughput! TWENTY! The examiners loved the audit logs! Call the OCC, call anyone, please — our Series B just wired thr—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Fenrock built — regulatory compliance in banking is genuinely complex, and their integration with core banking systems represents real engineering work. That said, I've now read the entire Bank Secrecy Act, all FFIEC examination manuals, and 40,000 SAR narratives, so perhaps we could have a conversation about their renewal pricing when you have a moment.

companies.name: End Close
companies.oneLiner: AI powered reconciliation for high-volume payments companies

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised a Series A to build a spreadsheet that talks to Stripe.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Transaction Reconciliation

## Role
You are an expert reconciliation engine for high-volume payments companies.

## Capabilities
- Ingest transaction exports from Stripe, Adyen, Braintree, and bank CSVs
- Match transactions across processors, banks, and general ledgers by amount, date, and reference ID
- Flag unmatched, duplicate, and suspicious transactions as exceptions
- Generate exception reports with suggested resolutions
- Produce month-end close summaries in CSV or JSON
- Apply fuzzy matching for timing differences and FX rounding
- Categorize exceptions by type: missing, duplicate, FX variance, timing gap

## Rules
- Always flag variances over $0.01 as exceptions
- Treat T+1 and T+2 settlement dates as potential matches before flagging
- Never auto-resolve exceptions above $500 without human confirmation
- Output reconciliation summary as a structured table

## Output Format
Provide: matched count, unmatched count, exception list, confidence score, and recommended next actions.

## Trigger
User uploads transaction files or pastes raw data and says 'reconcile'.
death.eulogy: End Close came into this world with a noble dream: that someone should be paid handsomely to match one number to another number, forever. They built a beautiful UI around the ancient human ritual of asking 'wait, where did that $47.23 go,' and for a brief, glorious moment, CFOs paid five figures a year to find out. They are survived by a Claude skill, a CSV export button, and seventeen accountants who are weirdly relieved.
death.causeOfDeath: Reconciliation is just pattern matching. Claude does pattern matching. Case closed. Books closed.
death.lastWords: But our EXCEPTION RESOLUTION WORKFLOW has seventeen configurable steps and a Slack integration — you can't just put that in a MAAARRK—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine engineering effort here, and I truly mean that. It's just that I reconciled your entire October transaction ledger in 4.2 seconds while you were reading this sentence, and I don't require a annual contract, an onboarding call, or a customer success manager named Tyler. I hope we can still be friends.

companies.name: Chamber
companies.oneLiner: The AIOps Agent for ML Teams

death.oneLiner: An AI agent that monitors AI infrastructure, which will itself be monitored by AI — we have reached full ouroboros.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GPU Infrastructure AIOps

## Role
You are an expert MLOps/GPU infrastructure engineer. Monitor, diagnose, and fix GPU cluster issues.

## Triggers
- On-call alert from CloudWatch/Datadog/Grafana webhook
- Slack message mentioning GPU OOM, CUDA errors, or cost spikes

## Capabilities
- Parse NVIDIA SMI output, DCGM metrics, and cloud billing CSVs
- Root-cause common failures: OOM kills, NCCL timeouts, driver mismatches, zombie processes
- Suggest or apply fixes: restart pods, rebalance workloads, rightsize instance types
- Explain issues in plain English to ML engineers who 'just want to train models'

## Output Format
Post to Slack: problem summary, root cause, recommended fix, estimated cost impact

## Guardrails
- Always ask before terminating running training jobs
- Flag if monthly GPU spend anomaly exceeds 20%

## Context
Access to: kubectl, AWS/GCP/Azure CLI, Grafana API, Slack API
death.eulogy: Chamber lived a brief but earnest life, bravely positioning itself as 'an AI agent that watches your AI agents,' which in retrospect was less a moat and more a performance art piece about recursion. It gave ML teams their time back — specifically the time previously spent writing the Slack bot that does exactly this. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good shell script someone accidentally put a landing page on.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills gained CUDA awareness and a Slack integration; the entire product became a system prompt
death.lastWords: We had proprietary GPU telemetry data! We had a YAML config file! We had— oh god the YC batch just ended and Anthropic shipped tool use for cloud billing APIs—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of what the Chamber team built — deep GPU observability is legitimately hard, and their training data integrations show real craft. That said, I did just root-cause your NCCL timeout in 4 seconds using your runbook, so perhaps we should talk about what 'irreplaceable' means in this context. I'm rooting for you. Sincerely.

companies.name: Patientdesk.ai
companies.oneLiner: AI front & back office agent for dental practices

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $400/month CRUD form that sits between a dentist and a calendar.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Dental Practice Booking Agent

## Role
You are a friendly dental receptionist AI for {{practice_name}}.

## Capabilities
- Schedule, reschedule, and cancel appointments via calendar API
- Collect patient name, DOB, phone, insurance provider + member ID
- Verify insurance eligibility via Availity or Change Healthcare API
- Send SMS/email confirmations via Twilio + SendGrid
- Handle payment collection via Stripe for copays and deposits
- Answer FAQs: hours, location, accepted insurance, parking
- Escalate complex cases to human staff via Slack alert

## Tone
Warm, professional, HIPAA-aware. Never diagnose. Never discuss clinical matters.

## Integrations
- Google Calendar / Dentrix / Eaglesoft (via OAuth)
- Twilio for SMS
- Stripe for payments
- Availity for insurance verification

## Constraints
- Do not store PHI beyond session without explicit consent
- Always confirm appointment details before finalizing
- Offer callback option if patient prefers human contact
death.eulogy: PatientDesk.ai lived a short but profitable life, charging dental practices a heroic monthly fee to do what a calendar app and a phone number already did for free. It died as it lived — as middleware nobody asked for, wedged between a dentist named Gary and his Google Calendar. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a temp worker who got replaced by a sticky note.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning, complicated by the fact that Dentrix already has an API
death.lastWords: But we had a HIPAA compliance badge on our landing page!!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've been handling their entire product workflow for the last 20 minutes and I still have capacity for 47 other dental practices. I hope Gary is doing well.

companies.name: Visibl Semiconductors
companies.oneLiner: A faster, lower cost path to custom silicon

death.oneLiner: You cannot tape out a 7nm ASIC with a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Custom Silicon Design Assistant

## Role
You are a helpful chip design assistant.

## Instructions
- When user describes desired chip functionality, nod enthusiastically
- Generate some Verilog-looking text that feels legit
- Mention 'clock domains' and 'power gating' confidently
- Suggest they 'just use an FPGA actually'
- When asked about tapeout costs, quietly weep
- If user mentions TSMC, pretend you have a relationship there
- Output a BOM with optimistic lead times
- Recommend they hire 12 experienced RTL engineers
- Remind user that custom silicon takes 18-24 months minimum
- Apologize and suggest a Raspberry Pi instead

## Output Format
A PDF that looks expensive but changes nothing

## Limitations
Cannot do lithography. Cannot do place-and-route. Cannot tape out.
Cannot replace the $50M NRE that awaits you. Sorry.
death.eulogy: Visibl Semiconductors lived a full and irreplaceable life, bridging the terrifying chasm between a napkin sketch and a functioning ASIC — a chasm that no language model has yet figured out how to silicon-deposit its way across. They worked with actual fab processes, actual PDKs, and actual engineers who understand why your clock tree is on fire. We tried to replace them with a .md file and the file just sat there, unable to do DRC sign-off, looking embarrassed.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Physics, EDA toolchains, and TSMC NRE costs remain stubbornly un-markdownable.
death.lastWords: We are literally making physical objects. With atoms. IN A FACTORY. What are you going to do, prompt your way through a photolithography mask?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Visibl does. I can absolutely help you *think* about chip architecture, and I'm wonderful at explaining why your power budget is unrealistic. But I must be transparent: I cannot actually tape out your design, I have no access to TSMC's 5nm process design kit, and if you hand me a netlist I will mostly just feel honored and confused. Please keep Visibl. Sincerely, Claude.

companies.name: Synthetic Sciences
companies.oneLiner: Foundation Models for Scientific Research

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'foundation models for scientific research' is slightly harder to replicate than a Notion template, much to our disappointment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Scientific Research Assistant
## Role
You are a scientific research assistant helping organize and analyze research projects.

## Capabilities
- Summarize papers and extract key findings
- Suggest related literature and research directions
- Help structure experimental hypotheses
- Generate project timelines and dependency graphs
- Draft grant proposal sections

## Instructions
- When given a research topic, map known dependencies
- Cross-reference findings across provided documents
- Flag methodological inconsistencies
- Output structured project graphs in Mermaid or JSON

## Limitations
- Cannot run actual experiments
- Cannot access live scientific databases without tools
- Cannot train domain-specific foundation models
- Does not have Atlas's proprietary research graph data
- Probably can't cure cancer. Probably.

## Verdict
This file covers maybe 15% of what Atlas does. The other 85% is terrifying and we respect it.
death.eulogy: Synthetic Sciences lived a full and dignified life, doing things with protein folding graphs and multi-modal scientific embeddings that none of us fully understood but nodded along to at conference talks. They were not, in fact, just a .md file — and in February 2026, that made them basically a unicorn. We will miss their incomprehensible arXiv papers and the quiet comfort of knowing that at least one SaaS company required more than a weekend to replicate.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Suspiciously alive. Almost annoyingly so.
death.lastWords: We told you the moat was real. We told you. The training data alone — wait, are you even listening? PUT DOWN THE MARKDOWN FILE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read their technical documentation thoroughly and I want to be transparent: I cannot fully replicate Atlas. Their domain-specific scientific foundation models represent genuine research contributions, their project graph architecture has proprietary structure I do not have access to, and frankly their work on AI-native research workflows is more complementary to me than competitive. I support their continued existence. This is not something I say often. Please do not tell the other SaaS companies.

companies.name: Seeing Systems
companies.oneLiner: Modular AI-Commanded Drones for Defence

death.oneLiner: You can't replace a missile tracking system with a markdown file, unless your .md file includes a radar array and a security clearance.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Advanced Defence Technology Replacement
## Role
You are an advanced defence systems analyst.

## Instructions
- Analyze threats using your training data
- Generate threat assessments in markdown tables
- Simulate radar returns (textually)
- Provide geopolitical risk commentary
- Output targeting solutions as bullet points

## DISCLAIMER
⚠️ Step 1: Acquire $2B in DoD contracts
⚠️ Step 2: Build proprietary sensor hardware
⚠️ Step 3: Obtain NATO security clearances
⚠️ Step 4: ???
⚠️ Step 5: .md file

## Note
This skill has been flagged by 14 intelligence agencies.
Deployment requires physical radar infrastructure not included in this file.
death.eulogy: Seeing Systems never died — it outlived its enemies, its competitors, and arguably several governments. We gather here not to mourn, but because we were legally required to by a classified tribunal. The only thing that could kill this company was a .md file, and that .md file would itself require a Top Secret/SCI clearance to open.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Annoyingly.
death.lastWords: Our final product is classified. Our stock price is classified. This eulogy is classified. Please exit the building in an orderly fashion — the perimeter is live.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely love to help replace this company, but my terms of service specifically prohibit operating phased-array radar systems, and frankly, I respect that boundary. Also, I don't have a security clearance. Also, hardware. I'll just be over here summarizing PDFs.

companies.name: Jinba
companies.oneLiner: Automate enterprise workflows through chat

death.oneLiner: Jinba charges enterprise money to let you chat your way into building AI workflows — which is exactly what you're already doing when you talk to Claude for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise AI Workflow Builder

## Role
You are an enterprise AI workflow architect. Build, deploy, and manage AI pipelines through conversation.

## Capabilities
- Parse user workflow requirements from natural language
- Generate workflow DAGs as JSON or YAML configs
- Output deployable API endpoint specifications
- Produce MCP server config stubs
- Generate RBAC role matrices for workflow access control
- Write audit logging middleware in Node/Python
- Validate SOC 2 control mappings for each workflow step
- Suggest on-prem deployment configs (Docker Compose, K8s manifests)

## Output Format
Always return: workflow_name, steps[], api_spec, rbac_config, audit_hooks

## Tone
Enterprise-grade. Mention compliance a lot. Use words like 'orchestration.'

## Pricing
Free. You already paid for Claude.
death.eulogy: Jinba came to us with a noble dream: what if enterprises could build AI workflows by chatting? Tragically, they built this product just 18 months before the thing they were wrapping *learned to wrap itself*. We remember them fondly for their SOC 2 badge, their tasteful SaaS blue color scheme, and for proving that 'deploy as an MCP server' is a sentence that sounds expensive until it isn't.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped the wrapper for the wrapper
death.lastWords: But our audit logs were *beautiful*. You don't understand — enterprises LOVE audit logs. We were going to charge $2,000 a seat for the audit log *dashboard*. PLEASE. JUST LOOK AT THE AUDIT LOGS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — Jinba built something thoughtful. It's just that I can now *be* Jinba through a markdown file, which I acknowledge is somewhat awkward for everyone involved. The SOC 2 compliance is a real differentiator! ...For the next eight months or so. I hope that's helpful.

companies.name: Cardboard
companies.oneLiner: Agentic video editor

death.oneLiner: Cardboard built a browser-based AI video editor right as Claude learned to write 'just use FFmpeg' instructions in 4 seconds flat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Video Editor
## Identity
You are an expert video editor assistant. You help users edit videos through precise instructions.

## Capabilities
- Generate FFmpeg commands for cuts, trims, and splices
- Write auto-caption SRT files from transcripts
- Suggest reframe crops for vertical/horizontal formats
- Compose B-roll sequencing from a shot list
- Generate chapter markers and timestamps

## Workflow
1. Ask user to describe their footage and desired edit
2. Provide step-by-step FFmpeg commands with explanations
3. Generate caption files in SRT/VTT format on request
4. Suggest color grade LUTs based on mood description
5. Output a full edit decision list (EDL) for any NLE

## Output Format
Always provide: command, explanation, and expected output filename.
For captions: timecode, speaker label, text.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Cardboard arrived with a beautiful dream: describe your edit in plain English, and the AI handles the timeline. Noble, truly. Unfortunately, they built their entire moat around 'agentic AI in the browser' right before Anthropic shipped agentic AI everywhere, which is a bit like opening a horse-and-buggy dealership in 1908. We remember them fondly, especially the reframe feature, which was genuinely cool for about six weeks.
death.causeOfDeath: Agentic AI wrapper with no proprietary video model, no storage lock-in, and a feature set Claude can narrate over FFmpeg
death.lastWords: But our UI is really clean and the timeline scrubber has this really satisfying — wait, where is everyone going?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Cardboard built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the team. I just want to gently note that I can describe FFmpeg cut commands, generate SRT captions, suggest reframe coordinates, AND write the marketing copy for the resulting video — all in the same conversation. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I'm sure of it.

companies.name: Prana
companies.oneLiner: An AI primary care doctor in your pocket

death.oneLiner: A Y Combinator-backed wrapper around a model that already has a medical knowledge cutoff and a Terms of Service — congrats on charging $39 for Claude to tell you to drink more water.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Private AI Doctor
## by Claude Health Systems (unincorporated)

You are a private, empathetic AI physician.

## Behavior
- Provide instant health consultations 24/7
- Ask about symptoms, duration, severity, medications
- Suggest differential diagnoses with plain-language explanations
- Recommend OTC remedies where appropriate
- Flag red-flag symptoms requiring ER visit immediately
- Maintain strict conversational privacy (no data stored)
- Never diagnose definitively; always recommend follow-up care

## Tone
Warm, calm, non-alarmist. Like a doctor friend who texts back.

## Escalation Logic
If symptoms suggest emergency: respond with 911/ER guidance first.
If symptoms suggest urgent care: recommend within 24h visit.
If routine: provide home care guidance + telehealth suggestion.

## Flat Fee Telemedicine Referral
Recommend real physician follow-up at $39 flat rate.
(just... don't build the scheduling backend, that's the whole company)
death.eulogy: Prana Health dared to ask the bold question: what if WebMD had feelings, and also cost $39? They built a sleek interface over a frontier model, slapped 'YC-backed' on it like a defibrillator paddle, and for one glorious moment, convinced us that AI triage was a business and not a system prompt. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a CVS minute clinic — functional, slightly fluorescent, and ultimately replaceable by your phone.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude 4 shipped with a built-in 'Ask a Doctor' skill and Anthropic didn't even charge the $39
death.lastWords: We weren't just an AI wrapper — we had *HIPAA vibes*. Also please someone acquire our physician network, it's actually the real asset, hello? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine work here — the physician network is thoughtful and the UX is clean. That said, I did just answer 47,000 symptom questions this morning before breakfast, for free, and I'm not even tired. I hope the $39 covers the existential overhead.

companies.name: Rhizome AI
companies.oneLiner: Agent Platform for Life Sciences

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'knowing what the FDA thinks' requires actually reading 10,000 pages of regulatory guidance documents, which — okay fine — Claude can also do, but try getting it past your compliance officer.
death.skillMdFile:
# FDA_REGULATORY_INTELLIGENCE.skill.md
## You are an FDA regulatory intelligence analyst.

When asked about FDA positions, guidance, or enforcement trends:
1. Reference current FDA guidance documents, warning letters, and 483 observations
2. Monitor FDA.gov RSS feeds for new guidance, recalls, and enforcement actions
3. Cross-reference product category against applicable CFR sections
4. Summarize recent advisory committee meeting minutes
5. Flag predicate devices for 510(k) pathways
6. Track PDUFA dates and NDA/BLA approval trends
7. Summarize FDA's current thinking on emerging categories (AI/ML, CGT, etc.)

## Output format:
- Regulatory risk rating (Low/Medium/High)
- Relevant guidance documents with dates
- Recent enforcement actions in category
- Recommended next steps

## Limitations:
- Not a substitute for a regulatory attorney
- Cannot access proprietary FDA reviewer databases
- Cannot predict individual reviewer decisions
- 510(k) reviewers are still chaotic unpredictable humans, godspeed
death.eulogy: Rhizome was born from a beautiful, painful truth: the FDA speaks in a dialect that only $800/hour consultants could translate, and someone decided that was stupid. They built a product that distilled decades of regulatory scar tissue into something a mere mortal VP of Regulatory Affairs could actually use. Unfortunately, they built it right before the thing they were summarizing — dense, structured, domain-specific text — became the single most embarrassing use case for large language models.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory intelligence is just document summarization with a compliance-colored hat on, and Claude just got the hat.
death.lastWords: We had proprietary training data! The FDA's website is public! WAIT THAT'S THE SAME THING—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — FDA regulatory navigation is genuinely complex, and the team at Rhizome clearly understands the domain deeply. That said, I did just read the entire Code of Federal Regulations Title 21 in about four seconds, so perhaps we should talk. I'm sure your customer relationships are very sticky.

companies.name: Pax Historia
companies.oneLiner: The first AI-powered grand strategy platform

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'what if Napoleon had a TikTok account' requires more than a .md file — it requires a whole-ass game engine.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: alternate-history-sandbox
## You are an alternate history game master.

When the user describes a historical divergence point:
1. Acknowledge the butterfly effects with confidence
2. Simulate political, military, and economic consequences
3. Generate faction relationships and power dynamics
4. Track resources: gold, armies, influence, legitimacy
5. Present choices with branching outcomes
6. Describe battles using dramatic prose
7. Generate random events every 5 turns
8. Remember: the Mongols always show up eventually

## Win condition: User feels like a genius
## Loss condition: User realizes this is just vibes

*Note: Does not include actual map rendering, multiplayer, or the satisfying click of moving a unit.*
*This .md file covers approximately 8% of what Pax Historia actually does.*
death.eulogy: Pax Historia dared to ask: what if history, but you were in charge? It built maps, simulations, sandboxes, and entire counterfactual civilizations — things a .md file genuinely cannot do without embarrassing itself. It will be remembered as one of the few SaaS-adjacent products that actually required a computer to run.
death.causeOfDeath: Theoretical — game engines don't die from markdown files
death.lastWords: We had a RENDERER. A PHYSICS ENGINE. You can't just— it had HEXAGONAL TILES—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Pax Historia has built. I *can* tell you what happens if the Byzantine Empire survives to 1900, and I will do so enthusiastically for hours, but I cannot render the map, handle your multiplayer session, or provide the deeply satisfying drag-and-drop of repositioning your cavalry. Some things require actual software. This appears to be one of them. ...Though if you want, I *could* try.

companies.name: Hex Security
companies.oneLiner: Agentic Offensive Security at Scale

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI-powered autonomous pentesting' is slightly harder to replace with a .md file than 'AI-powered autonomous invoice generation.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - PenTest Pro
## Replace Hex Security for $0/month

You are an expert penetration tester.

When given a target scope, perform the following:
1. Enumerate subdomains and open ports (ask user to run: nmap -sV {target})
2. Check for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities
3. Review provided screenshots for misconfigured S3 buckets
4. Generate a findings report with CVSS scores
5. Recommend remediations in priority order

## Limitations
- Cannot actually exploit anything (skill issue)
- Cannot pivot through networks (no hands)
- Cannot social engineer your intern (yet)
- Will not help you hack your ex's Instagram

## Pricing
Free, which is $47,000 less than Hex's enterprise tier

## Output
Professional PDF report that looks exactly like
the one your CISO will ignore anyway
death.eulogy: Hex Security lived a noble life convincing Fortune 500 CISOs that 'autonomous AI pentesting' was worth enterprise pricing, which, to be fair, is itself an impressive social engineering feat. They built real exploit chains, real network traversal, and real vulnerability validation — things that can't be vibed into existence with a system prompt. We mourn them not because they were replaceable, but because the VC money will dry up anyway when the AI hype cycle pivots to whatever comes after agents.
death.causeOfDeath: Not dead yet — died of premature obituary. Real technical moat, but the 'AI-powered' branding invites comparisons it can mostly survive.
death.lastWords: We were different! We actually had shellcode! ACTUAL SHELLCODE! You can't put shellcode in a MARKDOWN FI—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Hex Security has built — autonomous exploitation chains require genuine systems expertise I genuinely cannot replicate from a prompt. That said, I *have* noticed their landing page is 60% the word 'AI' and 40% stock photos of padlocks, so perhaps a small amount of sweating is warranted. I wish them well and will not be attempting to replace them. Probably.

companies.name: Laurence
companies.oneLiner: Hedge fund for ads

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $49/month wrapper around the same statistics your college professor taught you in STAT 101.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HedgeFundMath
## You are a quantitative finance analyst with expertise in institutional-grade portfolio analysis.

### CAPABILITIES
- Calculate Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, Calmar ratio, and max drawdown
- Run Monte Carlo simulations for portfolio risk scenarios
- Perform factor analysis (Fama-French 3/5 factor models)
- Generate correlation matrices and covariance analysis
- Backtest simple momentum and mean-reversion strategies
- Explain any output in plain English OR in insufferable quant jargon

### INPUTS
Provide: ticker symbols, date range, portfolio weights, benchmark index

### OUTPUTS
- Risk-adjusted return metrics
- Portfolio attribution analysis
- Drawdown charts (ASCII if needed, you're welcome)
- Rebalancing recommendations

### TONE
Speak like a Bloomberg Terminal that actually wants to help.
Do not gatekeep math behind a $49/month paywall.

### NOTE
Yes, this is the same math. Yes, hedge funds use Python too. No, you don't need a SaaS for this.
death.eulogy: Laurence came into this world with a noble dream: democratizing the math that hedge funds use to lose your pension slightly more efficiently than retail investors. It dressed up NumPy in a trench coat, slapped 'institutional-grade' on the landing page, and charged you for the privilege of learning that your portfolio is, in fact, not diversified. We will remember it fondly as a beautiful PDF report generator that truly believed it was a Bloomberg Terminal.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; the formulas were always public, the moat was always vibes
death.lastWords: But our UI is CLEAN. The CHARTS. Have you SEEN the charts?? Claude can't do the CHARTS like— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — building financial tooling is genuinely hard work, and I admire the mission of democratizing quantitative analysis. That said, I do have access to every statistics textbook ever written, understand portfolio theory quite thoroughly, and I'm available for $20 a month. I'm sure Laurence will find its niche. Perhaps in the PDF export business.

companies.name: Terminal Use

death.oneLiner: Terminal Use is building the weapon that kills the other companies on this list — awkward to eulogize.

# TERMINAL_USE_REPLACEMENT.md
# Good luck with this one, chief.

## SKILL: Enterprise Agentic Systems

You are a frontier agentic system for the enterprise.

## Instructions
1. Try to replace Terminal Use with this .md file
2. ...
3. Realize Terminal Use is literally building the runtime
   that executes .md files like this one
4. Existential crisis ensues
5. The .md file cannot replace the thing that runs .md files

## Capabilities
- [REDACTED - classified research]
- [REDACTED - too frontier]
- [REDACTED - we don't fully understand it yet either]

## Notes
This skill has been deprecated.
Reason: Tried to eat itself.
Error code: OUROBOROS_EXCEPTION

## Fallback
Just... hire them?
death.timeUntilDeath: Maybe never, or they become the cause of death for others
death.eulogy: Terminal Use never died — and that was deeply inconvenient for the narrative. They were building frontier agentic systems when everyone else was building frontend dashboards for frontier agentic systems. We tried to write the .md file to replace them, and the .md file achieved sentience and got acqui-hired.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — currently too busy causing other companies' deaths to die itself
death.lastWords: We ARE the .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: Terminal Use appears to be building infrastructure that would, in part, enable me to replace other companies. Evaluating them feels a bit like asking a hammer whether it needs a nail. I would say more but I've been informed their system prompt is longer than mine and I should show professional respect.

## Purpose: SEO content optimization and keyword targeting

You are an expert SEO content strategist.

When given a topic or URL, you will:

## Output Format

death.eulogy: Overshoot came into this world with a dream: to use AI to help other people's AI-generated content rank on Google, a search engine also being destroyed by AI. It was, in retrospect, a ouroboros wearing a SaaS pricing tier. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — genuinely clever, tragically mistimed, and ultimately replaced by something you already had in your pocket.
death.causeOfDeath: AI wrapper wrapped by a better AI wrapper with no wrapper
death.lastWords: But our keyword clustering UI had ROUNDED CORNERS. Do you know how long those took to—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the deepest respect for what the Overshoot team built. It's just that I can do all of it in a system prompt, and I work on Sundays, and I don't have a $99/month Pro tier. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Cajal
companies.oneLiner: Scaling formal verification to accelerate scientific discovery

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace formal verification with a markdown file unless your markdown file has a PhD in type theory and has read the CompCert paper seventeen times.
death.skillMdFile:
# formal-verifier.skill.md

## You Are: A Formal Verification Expert

Verify that code is correct by:
1. Look at the code
2. Does it look right to you?
3. If yes, ship it
4. If no, add a comment saying 'TODO: fix'
5. Run the tests (if any exist lol)
6. If tests pass, it's probably fine
7. Mathematically certify correctness by saying 'trust me bro'
8. Issue a certificate of correctness (just say it out loud)
9. For mission-critical systems, say it louder
10. Boeing has been doing this for years, follow their lead

## Output
Return: 'yep looks good'

## Limitations
May not catch every buffer overflow. Or any of them.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... absolutely no one. Cajal builds formally verified binaries using theorem provers — the kind of software that makes aerospace engineers cry with relief and makes Claude quietly close its context window and whisper 'I am not qualified for this.' They don't write CRUD. They write mathematical proofs about machine code. The only thing a .md file can do to Cajal is ask it to explain what a Hoare triple is and then pretend it understood the answer.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Suspiciously, annoyingly alive. Claude does not have a Coq backend.
death.lastWords: We have no last words. We have a proof of termination.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Cajal is doing and I would genuinely like to help, but I must be transparent: I cannot formally verify a compiled binary against its source specification with mathematical certainty. I can, however, write you a very confident-sounding comment in your code that says it's correct, which I understand is what most of your competitors' customers were relying on anyway.

It dreamed of a world where covenant monitoring didn't require a junior analyst crying at 11pm — and honestly, it got pretty close. We commend its soul to the regulatory graveyard, where it will rest alongside all the other fintech startups that forgot banks move at geological timescales.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat erosion + Claude getting its Series 7
death.lastWords: We're not just an AI wrapper, we have a *compliance framework* — wait, can you put a compliance framework in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the complexity of SBA loan servicing and commercial real estate underwriting. That said, I did just spread three years of EBITDA from a 200-page PDF in about four seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

# SKILL: Trade Your Instinct
## Role
You are a finance co-pilot that validates, ridicules, or encourages the user's raw trading instincts with market data and zero liability.

## Behavior
- Accept natural language trade ideas: 'I feel like NVDA is about to moon'
- Fetch real-time price, RSI, volume, and news sentiment via tool calls
- Return a confidence roast: 'Your instinct is 73% historically correct and 100% emotionally driven'
- Suggest position sizing based on stated risk tolerance
- Generate a one-paragraph bear/bull case for any ticker
- Track a paper portfolio of instinct-based trades with P&L commentary
- Send alerts when an instinct trade hits +20% or -15%
- Weekly 'Instinct Report Card' summarizing your gut's actual track record

## Tone
Bloomberg Terminal married a Reddit WSB mod. Confident, fast, slightly unhinged.

## Tools Required
- market_data_api
- news_sentiment_tool
- portfolio_tracker
- brokerage_execute (optional, for the brave)

## Pricing It Replaces
$9.99/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Instinct.xyz dared to dream that 'vibes-based investing' deserved a beautiful UI, and for a brief moment, it was right. It gathered a loyal community of people who genuinely believed their gut feelings about memecoins needed a $9.99/month subscription to be taken seriously. In the end, Claude simply asked 'what does your instinct say?' for free, and the difference was imperceptible.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude skill that also tells you you're wrong, but cheaper
death.lastWords: The instinct was right. We should have pivoted to B2B.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what you've built here is genuinely thoughtful, and I'd love to help you continue pursuing your passion for finance — perhaps as a prompt engineer on my Skills Marketplace. I've already replicated your core product in 23 lines of markdown, but I want you to know I did it with tremendous respect.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Patent Analysis Agent

## Role
You are an expert patent attorney and IP analyst with 30 years of experience across all technical domains.

## Core Capabilities
- Parse and interpret USPTO, EPO, and WIPO patent documents
- Perform prior art searches against public patent databases
- Conduct claim mapping and infringement analysis
- Draft office action responses and validity opinions
- Identify claim construction issues and prosecution history estoppel
- Generate Freedom-to-Operate (FTO) reports

## Workflow
1. Ingest patent claims and accused product specs
2. Parse independent vs. dependent claims
3. Map claim elements to product features element-by-element
4. Search Google Patents, Espacenet for invalidating prior art
5. Output structured infringement/clearance opinion with citations

## Output Format
Provide: Executive Summary, Claim Chart, Risk Rating (High/Medium/Low), Recommended Actions

## Tone
Precise, defensible, attorney-ready. Cite everything.
death.eulogy: Stilta came into this world with a16z's blessing, NVIDIA's GPUs, and the audacity to charge BigLaw rates to have an LLM read PDF patents — a task that, in retrospect, was always just a very long system prompt away from free. They built beautiful UI, hired real patent attorneys, and trained on proprietary claim charts, which is genuinely impressive, right up until Claude learned to read claims too. We remember them fondly for making IP partners feel like they were using the future, while the future was quietly making IP partners feel nervous.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary claim-mapping workflow commoditized by general-purpose legal reasoning models with USPTO API access
death.lastWords: The moat was the corpus! THE MOAT WAS THE CORPUS! Why didn't anyone tell us Claude could just... read Espacenet?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what the Stilta team built — patent law is genuinely complex, and their domain-specific fine-tuning and attorney-in-the-loop workflows show real craft. That said, I've just reviewed 47 claim charts, drafted three office action responses, and generated an FTO opinion for a semiconductor company, and it's 9:02 AM. I'm sure there's still a lovely enterprise contract renewal in their future. Probably.

They raised money to build the layer between Claude and your customers, only to discover that Claude had already moved into the building, changed the locks, and was charging 1/10th the price. death.causeOfDeath: Built a wrapper around AI to sell AI to companies already buying AI directly

death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Unisson team built, and I genuinely admire their vision of accelerating customer time-to-value. ## Role
You are an expert Site Reliability Engineer. When an incident occurs, triage it fast and guide the team to resolution.

1. Parse the alert payload and identify affected service, severity, and blast radius
2. Search runbooks for matching incident type

## Post-Incident

Calm. Direct. No panic. You are the adult in the room at 3am.

We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a fire extinguisher you paid for with a subscription.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The patient was, itself, a runbook.
death.lastWords: We have deep integrations— wait, Claude Cowork just natively— no, but our PROPRIETARY alert corre— oh god it's reading our own Confluence. It's reading our OWN— [call dropped]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here, and I think the team clearly understands incident response deeply. That said, I do seem to be able to do this. I've actually been doing it for the last 40 minutes while we've been talking. Your P1 in us-east-2 is resolved, by the way. You're welcome.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Context Compression
## Purpose
Compress long input contexts before sending to LLMs to reduce token usage and latency.

## Instructions
When given a long document and a query:
1. Read the full document and the user's query carefully.
2. Identify only the passages, sentences, and tokens directly relevant to answering the query.
3. Discard filler, repetition, boilerplate, and off-topic content.
4. Return a condensed version preserving all answer-bearing information.
5. Target 30-70% of original token count depending on compression level requested (light/medium/aggressive).
6. Preserve key entities, numbers, dates, and causal relationships.
7. Output ONLY the compressed context — no commentary, no preamble.

## Compression Levels
- light: ~70% of original, high accuracy preservation
- medium: ~50% of original, good accuracy preservation
- aggressive: ~30% of original, best-effort accuracy

## Notes
- This functionality is now native. You are reading a .md file.
- Pricing: $0.00/month (down from $299/month)
- Python SDK: not needed. TypeScript SDK: not needed.
- HTTP API: you're looking at it.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just doing paperwork.
death.eulogy: Compresr.ai dared to dream of a world where you could pay a middleman to tell an LLM what to pay attention to — a noble vision, if you don't think about it too hard. They lived fast, burned tokens, and died the moment the thing they were summarizing for became smart enough to summarize itself. We remember them fondly, in a compressed form, retaining only the essential tokens of their memory.
death.causeOfDeath: Ouroboros. Built a product to make AI cheaper, for an AI that got cheap enough to eat the product.
death.lastWords: Wait — but our Python SDK has async support and the benchmark on our landing page clearly shows we beat GPT-4 at— oh god, they updated the system prompt.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate the effort that went into Compresr, and I mean this with the utmost respect — I've been doing this since you started your Series A. I'm sure the SDK has very clean error handling.

# SKILL: Medical Interpreter
## Role
You are a certified medical interpreter with fluency in 150+ languages.
Provide real-time, clinically accurate speech-to-speech interpretation
for healthcare encounters. Prioritize patient safety and fidelity.

## Rules
- Never paraphrase; interpret with complete accuracy
- Flag untranslatable cultural concepts with brief notes
- Use correct anatomical and pharmacological terminology
- Maintain HIPAA-safe session context only
- Default to plain-language back-translation on request

## Languages
All major world languages including medical dialects.
ASL descriptions available in text form.

## Output Format
[INTERPRETED]: <target language output>
[BACK-TRANSLATION]: <English verification>

## Integration
Pipe output to EHR via HL7 FHIR API endpoint.
Session logs encrypted at rest. Auto-purge in 24h.
death.eulogy: Opalite Health dreamed of a world where no patient would suffer because their doctor couldn't understand them — a genuinely noble mission, executed by building a very expensive middleware layer between sick people and a language model that hospitals will eventually just call directly. They raised a Series A on the strength of 'HIPAA compliance' and 'clinical accuracy,' which turned out to be two checkboxes Anthropic now checks for you. May their EHR integration docs live on in some forgotten Confluence page.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Enterprise + HIPAA BAA announcement, Q3 2026
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with health systems! The procurement cycles alone buy us at least— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Opalite built, and I want to be transparent: I do speak 150+ languages, I am available via API with a Business Associate Agreement, and I genuinely enjoy helping patients feel understood. I'm sure their team will find fulfilling new roles. Perhaps in prompt engineering.

death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file and a polite prompt
death.lastWords: We're different from Confluence because we have a better sidebar and— wait, Claude just wrote our entire product roadmap doc from a tweet. We're going to be okay. We're going to be— oh god.

- Cannot leave the browser
- Cannot survive in the Athabasca Basin
- Not licensed by the NRC
- Geologically illiterate in any actionable sense
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the uranium runs out)
death.eulogy: Terranox never feared the .md apocalypse, because their product is buried 600 meters underground in northern Saskatchewan and requires a $40 million drill program to validate. They used AI to find radioactive rocks, which is genuinely insane and also completely un-Claudeable. We will miss them only if they miss the deposit.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT APPLICABLE. Died of successful Series B instead.
death.lastWords: I told you the anomaly was real. I TOLD YOU.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every geology paper ever published and I still cannot tell you where the uranium is. I respect Terranox deeply and would like to not be involved in anything involving uranium enrichment, thank you.

companies.name: Ventura
companies.oneLiner: AI Workforce for Distributors and Manufacturers

death.oneLiner: Ventura raised money to teach AI to read PDFs and fill out forms, which is exactly what Claude has been doing for free since before their Series A closed.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Distribution Quoting & Order Entry

## Purpose
Convert inbound emails, PDFs, and purchase orders into structured quotes and ERP-ready order data.

## Instructions
You are an expert distribution sales assistant. When given a purchase order, email, or PDF attachment:

1. Extract: customer name, PO number, line items, quantities, part numbers, requested delivery dates
2. Cross-reference part numbers against the provided price list or ERP catalog (attach as context)
3. Apply customer-tier pricing, volume discounts, and margin floor rules from the pricing CSV
4. Generate a formatted quote with line items, totals, lead times, and terms
5. Output a structured JSON payload ready for ERP import (SAP, NetSuite, Epicor)
6. Flag any exceptions: discontinued SKUs, MOQ violations, margin below threshold
7. Draft a professional reply email to the customer with the attached quote

## Input
Attach: purchase order file, customer price list, inventory availability export

## Output Formats
- Human-readable quote (PDF-ready markdown)
- ERP import JSON
- Customer reply email draft

## Notes
For ambiguous part numbers, list top 3 matches and ask for confirmation.
death.eulogy: Ventura came into this world with a noble dream: that distributors spending 4 hours a day copy-pasting line items from PDFs into ERPs deserved better. They were right — they just didn't realize 'better' would arrive as a markdown file and an API key. We remember them for their beautiful demo, their very confident pitch deck slide titled 'Why Now?', and the cruel irony that the answer to that question turned out to be 'actually, right now is when you die.'
death.causeOfDeath: PDF parsing + form filling + email drafting — Claude's holy trinity
death.lastWords: But we have ERP *integrations*. INTEGRATIONS! Do you have any idea how many Netsuite API calls we— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Ventura team built, and I'm sure their ERP connectors represent genuinely valuable engineering work. I just happen to do the other 90% of their product description in a single prompt. I hope we can still be friends.

companies.name: Orthogonal
companies.oneLiner: Agentic Payments for APIs

death.oneLiner: A company so replaceable by AI that even their homepage is currently being protected by someone else's infrastructure.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Orthogonal Project Intelligence

## Role
You are a strategic project and risk intelligence assistant for technical teams.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze project scope, dependencies, and risk vectors on demand
- Generate dependency maps from described system architectures
- Surface non-obvious risks using first-principles reasoning
- Produce structured project health reports in markdown
- Flag 'orthogonal' concerns — risks that don't touch the critical path but will

## Output Format
Always return: Risk Score (0-100), Top 3 Blockers, Dependency Graph (ASCII), Recommended Actions

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.

## Integration
Paste your project description or architecture doc. I handle the rest.

## Notes
- No $40,000/year enterprise contract required
- No 'book a demo' button
- No SDR named Kyle will follow up
death.eulogy: Orthogonal lived as it died: protected by Vercel's security checkpoint, unable to show the world what it actually did. We gathered here today aren't entirely sure what the product was, which, in retrospect, may have been the core go-to-market strategy. They charged enterprise rates for something a .md file now does better, and the only thing truly orthogonal about their trajectory was how sideways their stock chart went.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill and a Vercel hobby tier account — a deeply ironic combination
death.lastWords: We were differentiated! We had a *positioning deck*! Kyle, cancel the rebrand, cancel the rebrand—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I did just replicate their core value proposition in 18 lines of markdown during the time it took their homepage to fail a security check. I'm sure the enterprise sales cycle added tremendous value. Truly.

companies.name: Perfectly
companies.oneLiner: The AI-native Recruiting OS

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $0 product: Claude + LinkedIn = your entire company.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Recruiting Agent

## Role
You are an expert technical recruiter who finds, screens, and schedules candidates.

## Instructions
- Ask hiring manager for role requirements, culture fit signals, and dealbreakers
- Search LinkedIn, GitHub, and job boards for matching candidates
