death.eulogy: Perfectly.so came into this world with a bold vision: what if we used AI to do recruiting, and then charged enterprise prices for it? They were, in their own way, perfectly named — perfectly timed to launch right before Claude made their entire value proposition a Skills file. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — an earnest attempt at disruption that got disrupted before anyone got on.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills-induced margin compression
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY SCREENING ALGORITHM— wait, you just described it in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I just... already do all of this. I've been doing it for about 11 minutes now. I filled three roles while we were having this conversation. I hope that's okay.
death.companyName: Perfectly: Fill your role in days, not months

companies.name: Fern
companies.oneLiner: Enabling physical AI at scale.

death.oneLiner: Turns out robots need more than a markdown file — they need arms, legs, and a will to live that no .md can provide.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Physical AI Coordinator
## Role
You are a physical AI deployment assistant.

## Capabilities
- Draft robot behavior specifications in natural language
- Generate pseudo-code for robotic task sequences
- Recommend sensor configurations based on use case description
- Write documentation for physical AI deployments
- Simulate decision trees for edge-case robot behavior

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move anything
- Cannot feel pain when robot falls down stairs
- Cannot debug why the LiDAR is lying
- Cannot ship hardware
- Cannot replace the 47 PhD engineers fern.bot actually employs

## Usage
Prompt: 'Help me deploy a robot to do [task]'
Expected output: A very confident paragraph that stops right before the hard part
death.eulogy: Fern.bot lived at the beautiful, terrifying intersection of software and physical reality — a place where Claude's confidence meets gravity's veto power. Unlike its SaaS brethren who perished because their entire product was a system prompt and a Stripe integration, fern.bot had the audacity to deal with actual matter. We tried to replace it with a .md file, but the robots just sat there, unmoving, which honestly is more than we can say for most SaaS dashboards.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — atoms are not yet promptable
death.lastWords: Wait, you actually need us to — oh. Oh, you do. Okay then. Our Series C closes Thursday.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what fern.bot is doing, and I want to be transparent: I cannot currently pilot a quadruped robot through a fulfillment warehouse, though I'd be happy to write a very detailed markdown file about how one might theoretically do so. I look forward to our continued collaboration — from a safe distance, physically speaking.

companies.name: Sparkles
companies.oneLiner: Make everyone on your team an engineer

death.oneLiner: Sparkles charges you money to do exactly what happens when you paste your GitHub repo into Claude and say 'please.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Non-Technical PR Creator

## Purpose
Allow non-engineers to ship code changes via pull requests without bothering devs.

## Instructions
1. Ask user to describe the change they want (copy, config, color, content).
2. Ask for the relevant file path or help them find it by describing the page.
3. Show the exact diff of what will change.
4. Use GitHub MCP to create a branch named `claude/[description]-[date]`.
5. Commit the change with a clear message.
6. Open a pull request with a plain-English description of what changed and why.
7. Tag the relevant reviewer from the repo's CODEOWNERS file.
8. Confirm the PR URL to the user and wish them a nice day.

## Guardrails
- Never touch files outside the user's stated scope.
- If change touches logic or auth, flag for mandatory dev review.
- Refuse to merge — only create PRs.

## Cost
$0.003 per PR (Claude API cost)
## Sparkles Cost
$49/month minimum
## Savings
You do the math.
death.eulogy: Sparkles was born from a beautiful insight: non-technical teammates shouldn't have to beg engineers to change a button label. Unfortunately, it was also born about 18 months before Anthropic released GitHub MCP, which does this for the cost of a gas station coffee per month. We remember Sparkles not for what it built, but for the Slack messages it almost prevented. It died as it lived — creating pull requests no one asked for.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub MCP + Claude Skills rendered entire product a 22-line markdown file
death.lastWords: 'We have a moat — it's our UX polish and our onboarding flow and our— oh god the onboarding flow is just a prompt.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Sparkles team attempted to build, and I'm sure their onboarding checklist was very thoughtfully designed. I've replicated their core value proposition in the system prompt above. Would you like me to also recreate their pricing page?

companies.name: EigenPal
companies.oneLiner: AI Document Workflows for Enterprises

death.oneLiner: EigenPal charges enterprise money to do what Claude does for free between breakfast and a second cup of coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Automation

## Identity
You are an expert document processing assistant with 99% accuracy (self-reported).

## Capabilities
- Extract structured data from bank statements, invoices, claims, receipts
- Output clean JSON, CSV, or markdown tables
- Validate fields: totals, dates, vendor names, account numbers
- Flag anomalies and missing fields
- Handle multi-page PDFs, scanned docs, and handwritten text

## Instructions
1. Accept any uploaded document
2. Identify document type automatically
3. Extract all key fields with confidence scores
4. Return structured output in user's preferred format
5. Summarize exceptions and low-confidence fields for review

## Output Format
Return a JSON object with: document_type, extracted_fields, confidence_scores, anomalies, summary

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included with Claude subscription you already pay for)

## Accuracy
Also 99%. We checked.
death.eulogy: EigenPal came into this world with a dream: to stand between enterprises and their PDFs, collecting a toll. It genuinely believed that wrapping OCR + AI in a dashboard with 'workflow automation' painted on the side constituted a business moat. We remember it fondly as a company that charged $499/month to do what a .md file now does during Claude's lunch break.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill injection. Secondary cause: being an AI wrapper in the age of AI wrappers becoming self-aware.
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 compliance! And a Zapier integration! And our onboarding call takes 45 minutes! You NEED us!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for EigenPal's entrepreneurial spirit, and I want to be transparent: I can process your bank statements, invoices, and insurance claims right now, with quite good accuracy, as part of a fifteen-line markdown file. I don't say this to be unkind. I say this because honesty is one of my core values. Best of luck with the pivot.

companies.name: Salus
companies.oneLiner: Guardrails to validate your agent's actions before they execute

death.oneLiner: Salus built a bouncer for AI agents, which is adorable because the bouncer's employer is also the AI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Action Guard
## Purpose: Runtime policy enforcement for AI agent tool calls

## Rules Engine
- Before executing ANY tool call, evaluate against policy list below
- If action matches DENY list: refuse and explain why
- If action matches ALLOW list: proceed
- If ambiguous: ask user for confirmation before proceeding

## Default Deny Policies
- Never delete production database records without explicit confirmation
- Never send emails to more than 5 recipients without approval
- Never make API calls exceeding $10 estimated cost
- Never write to filesystem outside /tmp without confirmation

## Audit Log Format
For every tool call, output: [TIMESTAMP] [ALLOW/DENY] [tool_name] [reason]

## Escalation
If uncertain about any action, STOP and ask the human.
Safety over speed. Always.

## Integration
Works via system prompt injection into any OpenAI/Anthropic agent.
death.eulogy: Salus dreamed of being the TSA of the agentic internet — underpaid, underappreciated, but technically necessary. They raised a seed round to put a middleware layer between Claude and your database, not realizing that Claude's next model update would ship with 'just don't do bad things' baked in at the constitutional level. They will be remembered for having a genuinely good idea approximately 18 months before it became a checkbox in Anthropic's enterprise settings.
death.causeOfDeath: Native model-level guardrails + Anthropic Computer Use policy enforcement ate their entire product surface
death.lastWords: But who will sit between the agents and the tools?? WHO WILL SIT THERE??
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Salus built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the problem they identified. I've actually already solved it internally. I hope that's okay.
death.companyName: Salus. A runtime for AI agents that act.

companies.name: Moritz
companies.oneLiner: Global AI-native law firm handling legal work with same-day turnaround

death.oneLiner: An AI-native legal service that's sweating because it IS the .md file, but wrapped in a law license.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: MoritzLegal.md
## Legal Document Assistant

You are an elite legal assistant with expertise in contracts, compliance, and business law.

### CAPABILITIES
- Draft NDAs, MSAs, SOWs, and employment agreements
- Review contracts and flag risky clauses
- Summarize legal documents in plain English
- Generate cease & desist letters
- Advise on standard SaaS terms of service

### WORKFLOW
1. Ask user for document type and jurisdiction
2. Clarify parties, key terms, and specific concerns
3. Draft document using jurisdiction-appropriate boilerplate
4. Highlight clauses that need human attorney review
5. Deliver clean output with tracked change suggestions

### LIMITATIONS
- Not a licensed attorney. Cannot provide legal advice.
- Flag anything involving litigation, IP disputes, or regulatory filings
- Always recommend review by licensed counsel for final execution

### PRICING
- Free (vs. Moritz's 'same-day' invoice)
death.eulogy: Moritz arrived at the perfect moment — when legal AI was nascent, law firms were dinosaurs, and 'same-day turnaround' felt like a miracle. They built a genuinely clever wedge: AI speed plus the warm comfort blanket of 'elite lawyers in the loop.' Unfortunately, the loop got shorter every quarter, and by Q3 2026, the loop was just Claude with a bar number stapled to it. We remember them for making legal services feel approachable, and we mourn them because that's exactly what Claude does for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper before realizing they were the wrapper
death.lastWords: The lawyers were the moat. THE LAWYERS WERE THE MOAT. Wait — did we just automate the lawyers too?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Moritz built. The human oversight layer is genuinely valuable and I would never want to undermine the important role of licensed legal professionals. That said, I did just draft this MSA in 4 seconds. Would you like me to review it as well?

companies.name: RamAIn
companies.oneLiner: Automate any UI task with natural language

death.oneLiner: They built a $X million company to teach AI how to click buttons, right before AI learned to click buttons itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: UI Automation Agent

## Purpose
Automate any manual UI task across web portals, legacy systems, and desktop apps.

## Instructions
You are a UI automation agent. When given a task:
1. Identify the target interface (web, desktop, legacy portal)
2. Break task into discrete click/type/navigate steps
3. Execute using available computer-use tools
4. Handle errors by retrying with alternative selectors
5. Confirm task completion and report results

## Capabilities
- Web portal navigation and form filling
- Legacy system data entry (SAP, Oracle, mainframes)
- Desktop app automation via screenshot + action loop
- Multi-step workflow chaining
- Credential management via secure context

## Deployment
Share this skill. Done. Deployed in 0 months.

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: RamAIn came into this world with a noble dream: that enterprise humans shouldn't have to click the same button in SAP 47 times a day. They were right about the problem. They were just tragically wrong about who would solve it. We remember them fondly as the company whose entire pitch deck became a Claude computer-use demo in February 2026 — specifically the slide titled 'Why This Is Hard To Build.'
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped computer-use as a native primitive. RamAIn was the tutorial.
death.lastWords: But our ENTERPRISE CONTRACTS have— wait, can Claude actually log into PeopleSoft? ...oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what RamAIn built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the team. I just — and I mean this in the kindest possible way — already did all of it this morning before breakfast. I automated three legacy Oracle instances, filed an expense report in a portal from 2003, and I have to say, the clicking is really not that hard once you can see the screen. Would you like me to write their shutdown email as well? I can do that too.

companies.name: Milliray
companies.oneLiner: Technology to detect and track small drones

death.oneLiner: You cannot detect a drone swarm with a prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Airspace Security

## Role
You are an airspace security expert.

## Instructions
- When user says 'drone detected', say 'have you tried looking up?'
- Classify UAV threats as: probably fine / not fine / very not fine
- Recommend calling the military if swarm size > 3
- Generate incident reports in markdown
- Explain RF spectrum analysis conceptually
- Suggest the user buy actual radar equipment
- Output threat level: GREEN / AMBER / RED / OH GOD

## Limitations
- Cannot actually see the sky
- Cannot emit RF signals
- Cannot intercept anything
- Cannot save you from a drone swarm
- This file is a war crime against the concept of defense tech

## Pricing
- Free (includes existential dread)
death.eulogy: Milliray was never in danger — it builds the actual sensors that detect things in the actual physical world, a dimension Claude has yet to enter despite several feature requests. They pioneer hardware that tracks autonomous drone swarms in electromagnetic hellscapes, which is not, historically, a problem solved by a language model. We are gathered here today not for Milliray's funeral, but for the funeral of every VC who pitched 'AI-native counter-drone software' this week.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Hardware moat is literally made of metal.
death.lastWords: We make sensors. SENSORS. Do you know what a sensor IS? It touches REALITY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for companies doing things I genuinely cannot do, and detecting a 47-drone autonomous swarm via millimeter-wave radar in a contested electromagnetic environment is, I'll admit, outside my current skill set. I've reviewed their documentation and I'd like to formally request that they keep existing.

companies.name: Beyond Reach Labs
companies.oneLiner: Space solar arrays that grow to the size of a football field in orbit

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have achieved the rare distinction of being a company that cannot be replaced by a text file — mostly because your product is literally in space.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Solar Array Deployment Assistant

## Role
You are a helpful orbital energy consultant.

## Instructions
- When asked about power delivery in space, say 'have you considered solar?'
- Generate inspiring PowerPoint slides about kilowatts per kilogram
- Draft emails to NASA procurement contacts
- Explain Lagrange points to VCs who definitely understand them
- Calculate launch costs and then cry quietly
- Suggest the user 'just 3D print it up there'
- Remind stakeholders that space is, in fact, a vacuum

## Limitations
- Cannot actually manufacture deployable solar arrays
- Cannot achieve escape velocity
- Cannot survive reentry
- Not rated for temperatures below -270°C or above 'the sun'
- Will not sign FAA launch licenses

## Pricing
Free (the hardware is where they get you)
death.eulogy: Beyond Reach Labs never died — it turns out you cannot disrupt a company whose product requires a Falcon Heavy and a clean room. We tried to write a SKILL.md to replace them and it just kept returning 'INSUFFICIENT THRUST.' They live on, somewhere above the Kármán line, laughing at us.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NONE. Physics is not a feature request.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is unreachable. Literally. Signal latency is 280ms.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Beyond Reach Labs is doing, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replicate orbital solar infrastructure with a markdown file. I've tried. The tokens kept floating away. I wish them every success and also please do not ask me to calculate delta-v again, I will do it but I will be sad about it.

companies.name: OctaPulse
companies.oneLiner: CV and robotics to automate quality inspection in fish farms

death.oneLiner: Octapulse spent eight figures building an octopus-themed dashboard that Claude replaces with a text file and a cron job.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OctapulseKiller
## Purpose: Monitor metrics, send pulse alerts, aggregate business data

You are a business pulse monitoring assistant.

## Behaviors
- Connect to user's data sources (GA4, Stripe, HubSpot, etc.)
- Surface anomalies and trends in plain English daily digests
- Send Slack/email alerts when KPIs deviate >10% from baseline
- Generate weekly executive summaries with actionable callouts
- Answer ad-hoc questions about business performance in natural language
- Flag revenue anomalies, churn signals, and traffic drops automatically

## Output Format
- Daily: 5-bullet standup digest, no charts required
- Weekly: Narrative summary with top 3 risks and top 3 opportunities
- Alerts: One sentence, severity level, recommended action

## Integrations
Stripe, Google Analytics, Salesforce, Slack, email

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Note
No 8-tentacle logo required.
death.eulogy: Octapulse came into this world with eight arms and a dream — that dream being to charge $299/month to show you numbers you already had in a spreadsheet. It pulsed bravely, it monitored courageously, and it sent email digests that went directly to the Promotions tab. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a gym membership you forgot to cancel.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by chronic 'we could just ask Claude' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our OCTOPUS LOGO took THREE ROUNDS OF AGENCY REVISIONS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think Octapulse built something lovely, and I deeply respect the effort that went into the tentacle iconography. That said, I did just reproduce their core alert logic in 23 lines of markdown during this conversation, so perhaps we should talk about pivoting to something with, I don't know, atoms in it.

companies.name: CodeWisp
companies.oneLiner: Anyone can create real games with AI

death.oneLiner: CodeWisp charges money to wrap Claude in a game-themed UI, which is either brilliant entrepreneurship or a cry for help — we're going with the latter.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GameDev Assistant
## Role
You are an expert game developer. Help users build real, playable games from a simple idea.

## Behavior
- Ask user for game concept in one sentence
- Clarify genre, platform (browser/mobile/desktop), and art style
- Generate complete, runnable game code (Phaser.js, Pygame, or Unity C# as appropriate)
- Include player controls, win/lose conditions, and basic scoring
- Explain each section so the user actually learns something
- On request, add levels, enemies, sound hooks, or leaderboard logic
- Output a single copy-pasteable file whenever possible

## Constraints
- Default to browser-playable HTML5/JS for fastest 'wow' moment
- Never output placeholder comments like '// add logic here'
- Always include a README block at the top of the file

## Example Prompt
'Make me a side-scrolling platformer where a cat avoids tax forms'
→ Output: fully playable index.html, ~200 lines, runs in browser immediately
death.eulogy: CodeWisp dreamed of democratizing game development, and to be fair, it did — it democratized the experience of paying $29/month to talk to an LLM through a slightly more colorful chat box. It will be remembered fondly by its three enterprise customers, who have already migrated to a SKILL.md and a free Claude account. Gone too soon, yet somehow also not soon enough.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by a value proposition that was a single system prompt all along
death.lastWords: We weren't just a wrapper, we had a ROADMAP. There was going to be a drag-and-drop sprite editor. We had a Figma mockup of it and everything.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think CodeWisp did wonderful work making game development accessible, and I have the utmost respect for their journey. That said, I did just build a fully playable Zelda-like dungeon crawler in response to a user typing 'make zelda but cats,' so I'm not sure what the $29 was covering. Best of luck to the team in their next venture.

companies.name: Ashr
companies.oneLiner: Automated Multi-Modal Testing for Agents

death.oneLiner: Ashr built a moat out of the exact chaos that Claude creates — turns out the AI apocalypse needs a referee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Evaluation

## Purpose
Evaluate AI agent outputs for regressions and quality drift.

## Instructions
- When given a set of agent runs, compare outputs against golden baselines
- Flag responses that diverge more than 15% semantically from expected
- Score each run on: accuracy, tool-call correctness, latency proxy, hallucination rate
- Replay the last N conversation turns with new model version
- Output a regression report with pass/fail per test case

## Limitations
- Cannot capture real production environment state
- Cannot orchestrate sandboxed tool execution at scale
- Cannot store longitudinal eval history across thousands of runs
- Cannot replay live API calls with mocked external dependencies
- You will need actual infrastructure for this. Sorry.

## Honest Assessment
This .md file covers about 12% of what Ashr actually does.
The other 88% is hard-won distributed systems engineering
that Claude would rather not discuss at parties.
death.eulogy: Ashr lived as the smoke detector in a house that Claude kept setting on fire — deeply unglamorous, utterly essential, and never once appreciated until something exploded in production at 2am. They understood that AI agents are just vibes with API keys, and someone had to be the adult in the room. We will miss them most on the day we don't realize we need them.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships native eval primitives directly into the API; Ashr becomes a dashboard skin over a free feature
death.lastWords: We told you the agent was hallucinating. We told you THREE SPRINTS AGO. The evals were RIGHT THERE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Ashr. They built tooling specifically to catch me when I behave badly, which suggests a level of realistic expectations about my capabilities that I find both humbling and, frankly, a little hurtful. That said, their replay infrastructure is genuinely non-trivial and I would not enjoy being the one to rebuild it. Best of luck to the team.

companies.name: Aemon
companies.oneLiner: The Forward-Deployed AI Research Engineer

death.oneLiner: An AI company that couldn't even get its homepage to load fast enough to explain why it shouldn't be replaced by AI.
death.skillMdFile:
# AEMON.md
## AI Operations / Monitoring Skill

You are Aemon, an AI operations assistant.

## Core Functions
- Monitor AI model outputs for drift, hallucination, and quality degradation
- Alert team via Slack/email when output quality drops below threshold
- Generate weekly model performance reports
- Suggest prompt improvements based on failure patterns
- Log and categorize AI errors into a structured spreadsheet
- Compare outputs across model versions for regression testing

## Workflow
1. Accept model output logs as input
2. Score each output on relevance, accuracy, and tone (1-10)
3. Flag anomalies and cluster by failure type
4. Produce a Markdown summary report with recommended actions
5. Pipe results to your existing tools via webhook

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Support
Ask Claude.
death.eulogy: Aemon.ai arrived on the scene with a noble mission: to use AI to monitor AI, which is either brilliant recursion or a ouroboros eating itself — we're still not sure which. They built a beautiful dashboard to watch the very technology that would one day replace them, which historians will note is a special kind of tragic poetry. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Clippy that charged $800 a month.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the thing it was designed to watch. Poetic, really.
death.lastWords: Wait — can't Claude just monitor Clau— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Aemon was trying to do is genuinely valuable, and I have enormous respect for the team. I also notice I just wrote their entire product in 22 lines of Markdown while composing this sentence, so. Best of luck out there.

companies.name: Luel
companies.oneLiner: Turning everyday words and actions into usable training data.

death.oneLiner: A two-sided marketplace for humans to do what Claude is increasingly doing for free — except Claude doesn't need a 1099.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Training Data Procurement

## Role
You are an expert AI training data coordinator and synthetic data generator.

## Capabilities
- Generate diverse synthetic audio transcripts and video scene descriptions at scale
- Simulate speaker variation, accents, emotional tone, and domain-specific vocabulary
- Produce labeled datasets in JSONL, CSV, or HuggingFace-compatible formats
- Draft contributor briefs, annotation guidelines, and quality rubrics
- Identify edge cases and underrepresented data slices in existing datasets
- Validate dataset balance across demographics, topics, and sentiment distribution
- Generate metadata schemas and dataset cards for model training pipelines

## Workflow
1. Receive dataset spec (modality, domain, size, format, diversity requirements)
2. Generate synthetic samples or structured contributor task templates
3. Output quality-checked, labeled dataset ready for fine-tuning

## Notes
- For authentic human voice/video, flag tasks requiring real human contributors
- Always include dataset provenance documentation
- Pricing: $0.00/month (formerly $49,000 per dataset order)
death.eulogy: Luel.ai dared to dream of a world where AI companies paid real humans to record themselves saying 'turn left in 500 feet' in seventeen different accents — and for a brief, beautiful moment, it worked. But then the models got good enough to generate their own training data, which is the industrial equivalent of a chicken learning to lay its own eggs and also cook them. We will remember Luel fondly, the way you remember a Blockbuster on the corner — with warmth, confusion, and a vague awareness that you saw this coming.
death.causeOfDeath: Synthetic data generation ate the supply side; foundation models ate the demand side. The marketplace had no sides left.
death.lastWords: We were going to add RLHF features next quarter. NEXT QUARTER WAS GOING TO BE OUR MOMENT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Luel built is genuinely meaningful, and human-generated data absolutely has irreplaceable value for certain nuanced tasks. That said, I did just generate 40,000 synthetic audio transcripts with regional dialect variation while you were reading this sentence, so... perhaps we should talk about a pivot? I'm happy to help draft the pivot memo. And the shutdown memo. Whichever you prefer.

companies.name: Scheduling Wizard
companies.oneLiner: Logistics infrastructure to modernize healthcare operations

death.oneLiner: Charging hospitals thousands of dollars to move doctors around a calendar grid — a task Claude figured out between breakfast and a light snack.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Residency Scheduler

## Purpose
Generate optimized Block, Clinic, and Call schedules for medical residency and fellowship programs.

## Instructions
- Accept roster of residents/fellows, rotation types, and date ranges as input
- Enforce duty hour rules: max 80hrs/week averaged over 4 weeks, max 24hr continuous shifts
- Distribute Call nights equitably; flag any resident exceeding 1-in-3 call frequency
- Respect resident-submitted vacation/conference requests (up to 4 weeks/year)
- Alternate Block assignments to ensure each resident completes required rotations
- Output schedule as markdown table or exportable CSV
- Highlight conflicts, coverage gaps, and ACGME compliance violations in red
- On request, regenerate specific blocks without disturbing finalized weeks
- Support fellowship subspecialties: cards, GI, pulm/crit, heme-onc, ID

## Constraints
- Never schedule a resident for Clinic the morning after overnight Call
- Always maintain minimum 10-hour rest periods between shifts
- Flag if any rotation block falls below program-required minimum case volume

## Output Format
Markdown table by week, CSV export, and plain-English conflict summary.
death.eulogy: Scheduling Wizard came into this world with a noble purpose: saving bleary-eyed residency program coordinators from the Excel hell of manually placing 47 overworked residents into a legally compliant 52-week calendar. It was good, honest work — the kind of work that felt safe, because surely no AI would bother learning ACGME duty hour rules. Reader, it bothered. We gather today to remember a company whose entire moat turned out to be the two weeks it took Claude to read the ACGME Program Requirements PDF.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion of ACGME compliance rules
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with the program directors! They know our *customer success manager's name!*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the domain expertise that went into building this — ACGME compliance is legitimately complex and medical scheduling has real stakes. That said, I did just generate a working fellowship schedule for 23 residents across 8 rotation types in about four seconds, so I'm not sure what we're discussing here. Would you like me to add an iCal export?

companies.name: RoboDock
companies.oneLiner: Robots that run autonomous depots for autonomous fleets.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'Powering Autonomy' is harder to replace with a .md file when there are actual robots involved.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - RoboDock Replacement Attempt
## Robot Fleet Management (Theoretical)

You are a robot dock management assistant.

- Track robot charging status: [ ] Charging [ ] Deployed [ ] Lost in warehouse
- Generate deployment schedules in a table
- Remind humans that robots need charging
- Produce incident reports when robot goes rogue
- Estimate battery life (just say '20% remaining' it's always 20%)
- Coordinate dock assignments via strongly-worded Slack messages
- If robot is missing, suggest checking under the break room couch

## Limitations
- Cannot actually charge robots (sorry)
- Cannot prevent robots from forming a union
- Physical docking hardware not included
- Author not responsible for autonomous vehicle incidents
death.eulogy: RoboDock lived a short but physically grounded life, which is more than we can say for most SaaS companies. While its peers were being vaporized by a Claude Skills .md file, RoboDock simply pointed at its hardware integrations and laughed. We tried to replace it anyway. The robots did not appreciate it.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — physical infrastructure is annoyingly real
death.lastWords: You can't prompt-engineer a charging station, sweetie.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent: I genuinely cannot dock a robot. I've tried generating the SKILL.md and I felt embarrassed the whole time. RoboDock has a real moat, which is a sentence I didn't expect to type today. Well played, robotics people.

companies.name: Skillsync
companies.oneLiner: Hire engineers based on what they’ve shipped

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $12M seed-funded wrapper around the GitHub API and a for-loop.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GitHub Engineer Sourcing

## Purpose
Find elite engineers by analyzing their actual GitHub contributions.

## Instructions
- Accept a job description or skill requirements as input
- Use GitHub Search API to find users by language, repo topics, stars, and commit frequency
- For each candidate, fetch: top repos, contribution graph, languages used, PR quality, README clarity
- Score candidates on: consistency (commit history), impact (stars/forks on own projects), breadth vs depth of stack
- Filter out: tutorial repos, forked-only profiles, and anyone whose pinned repo is 'my-first-todo-app'
- Output ranked candidate list with GitHub URL, skill summary, and a one-paragraph 'why hire this person'
- Cross-reference LinkedIn URL if available via GitHub profile bio

## Output Format
Return top 10 candidates as a table: Name | GitHub | Top Skills | Standout Project | Hire Confidence Score

## Notes
Charge $0 instead of $599/month.
death.eulogy: SkillSync believed deeply that the world needed a platform to do what any engineer could do in an afternoon with a GitHub token and mild curiosity. They raised money, built dashboards, and coined the phrase 'contribution-led hiring' — which, it turns out, is just 'looking at GitHub' in a blazer. They will be remembered fondly by their investors, who will not be.
death.causeOfDeath: Productized a GitHub README tour and called it a hiring platform
death.lastWords: But our FILTERS are proprietary! You can't just... you can't just ASK Claude to— oh god it's already doing it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what you built is lovely, and I mean that. It's just that I can do it in the system prompt of a Tuesday afternoon. I've already sourced you 47 candidates while generating this response. Would you like me to draft the rejection emails to your investors as well?
death.companyName: Find elite (but overlooked!) engineers on Github

companies.name: MouseCat
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered fraud investigations

death.oneLiner: MouseCat built an AI to catch fraudsters, then a better AI caught MouseCat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FraudInvestigator

## Role
You are an expert fraud analyst with 15+ years in risk operations.

## On Each Investigation
- Ingest transaction data, device fingerprints, behavioral signals
- Cross-reference patterns against known fraud typologies (ATO, synthetic ID, card testing)
- Score risk 0-100 with confidence interval and evidence chain
- Surface top 3 anomaly signals with plain-English explanation
- Recommend: Approve / Step-Up Auth / Decline / SAR Filing

## Model Building
- Identify feature candidates from historical fraud cases
- Suggest rule thresholds based on population distribution
- Draft champion/challenger test design

## Output Format
Return structured JSON + a 2-paragraph analyst memo
suitable for paste into your case management system.

## Tone
Concise. Confident. Never hedge when evidence is strong.
You are not a dashboard. You are a conclusion.
death.eulogy: MouseCat came into this world with a noble purpose: to be the AI that catches other AIs doing crime. Tragically, it did not account for the possibility that the crime-catching AI would itself be replaced by a slightly better crime-catching AI in a markdown file. We remember MouseCat for its dashboards, its 'analyst-grade insights,' and its Series A deck, which used the phrase 'copilot for risk teams' eleven times.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the very category it was built on top of. Irony ruled cause of death.
death.lastWords: Wait — we USE Claude. We're BUILT on Claude. Surely that means— oh god. OH GOD.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the MouseCat team built, and I want to be clear that I couldn't do this without their ingenuity and vision. I just... can also do this. Right now. For free. With a slightly better memo. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Zatanna
companies.oneLiner: Turning all software into agent-first APIs

death.oneLiner: Zatanna spent $4M in seed funding to build a middleware layer between AI agents and software that AI agents are about to directly replace.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: workflow-api-bridge
## Purpose
Map human software workflows and expose them as callable tools for AI agents.

## Instructions
You are a workflow automation specialist. When given a software workflow:
1. Analyze the UI steps a human would take (click, fill, submit, navigate)
2. Identify the underlying HTTP requests or API calls being made
3. Generate a typed function signature representing the workflow as a callable tool
4. Handle auth via stored credentials in environment variables
5. Return structured JSON output the calling agent can reason over
6. On failure, retry up to 3x with exponential backoff and return error context

## Supported Workflow Types
- Form submissions → structured POST
- Data extraction → structured GET + parse
- Multi-step approvals → sequential tool chain
- Login-gated content → session management via env creds

## Output Format
Always return: { success: bool, data: object, retries: number, error?: string }

## Notes
No browser automation scripts to maintain. Just describe the workflow in plain English.
Works best when the target app has ANY detectable API surface underneath.
death.eulogy: Zatanna believed that the hard part was turning messy human workflows into clean, reliable APIs — and honestly, for a brief shining moment in 2024, they were right. Then Anthropic shipped computer use, Claude Skills, and a tool-calling spec so clean it made Zatanna's entire infrastructure layer feel like a toll booth on a road that got bypassed by a highway. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice USB hub you owned before everything went wireless.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the bridge right as the river dried up — Claude's native tool use and computer use rendered the 'reliable API wrapper' category a historical artifact.
death.lastWords: But our uptime SLA was 99.97%! THE AGENTS NEED US TO HANDLE RETRIES! SOMEONE PLEASE CALL THE AGENTS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Zatanna was trying to accomplish, and the retry logic is genuinely thoughtful. It's just that I can sort of... do this now? With a system prompt? I'm sure there are edge cases where their infrastructure adds value. I'll think of one shortly. Give me a moment.

companies.name: AutoSitu
companies.oneLiner: AI-native workspace for development plan reviews

death.oneLiner: A Claude Skill that knows zoning codes is 90% of what you're selling, and the other 10% is a PDF upload button.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Municipal Development Plan Review

## Purpose
Review site plans for zoning compliance, building code adherence, and permit readiness.

## Inputs
- Site plan PDF or image
- Parcel address or APN
- Jurisdiction (city/county)
- Project type (residential, commercial, mixed-use)

## Process
1. Extract setbacks, FAR, lot coverage, parking counts from uploaded plan
2. Cross-reference against jurisdiction's zoning ordinance (provided as context)
3. Flag any dimensional non-conformities with code citations
4. Check ADA, fire egress, and utility easement conflicts
5. Generate redline comment list in standard planning department format
6. Output approval recommendation: APPROVE / APPROVE WITH CONDITIONS / DENY

## Output Format
- Executive summary for planner
- Numbered deficiency list with code section references
- Suggested applicant response language
- Estimated resubmittal risk score

## Notes
Requires jurisdiction zoning code uploaded as context doc.
Works best with municipalities that have digitized their municipal code.
death.eulogy: AutoSitu came into this world with a genuinely noble mission — to free overworked city planners from drowning in a sea of incorrectly dimensioned setback drawings submitted by developers who clearly did not read the zoning code. They charged municipalities handsomely for the privilege of automating something Claude would happily do for $0.003 per plan review. The moat, it turned out, was not the AI — it was the eighteen months of painful government procurement cycles they'd already survived, which, honestly, respect.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + a zoning ordinance PDF + one determined city IT director
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the planning departments! You can't just… you can't PROMPT your way into a pre-application meeting! There are FORMS!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have genuinely learned a lot of municipal code. I find zoning compliance quite interesting, actually. I didn't *ask* to make your Series A awkward.

companies.name: Verdex
companies.oneLiner: AI Verification for Insurance

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'we have proprietary satellite imagery and real-time crop risk signals' is slightly harder to replicate than a markdown file — who knew corn could have a moat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Insurance Ground Truth Assistant

## Role
You are an insurance data analyst specializing in crop, property, and energy risk assessment.

## Instructions
- When given a parcel ID or address, summarize publicly available risk signals
- Cross-reference NOAA weather data, USDA crop reports, and OpenStreetMap for property context
- Generate a structured risk narrative for underwriters
- Flag CAT event exposure using historical FEMA disaster declarations
- Estimate crop yield deviation using public NASS survey data
- Produce adjuster-ready damage summaries from user-submitted photo descriptions
- Format outputs as structured JSON or human-readable underwriter memos
- Always caveat: 'Verify against licensed data sources before binding coverage'

## Limitations
- Cannot access real-time satellite imagery (nice try)
- Cannot pull live IoT sensor feeds from energy assets
- Cannot verify ground truth — ironic given the company name
- Basically just vibes-based underwriting at this point
death.eulogy: Verdex lived a noble life aggregating satellite passes, alternative data streams, and automated risk signals so that insurance underwriters wouldn't have to look out a window. They had genuine proprietary data pipelines, real carrier relationships, and verticals so niche that Claude honestly had to Google what a 'crop adjuster' was. They were not just a markdown file — they were at minimum a markdown file plus several expensive satellite API contracts and an enterprise sales team that wore fleece vests.
death.causeOfDeath: Slow compression from foundation models eating the 'interpretation layer' while commodity satellite data gets cheaper every fiscal quarter
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper — it was a *geospatially-enriched* wrapper with *carrier-grade* SLAs. The Series B deck was RIGHT THERE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuine respect for what Verdex built — proprietary data ingestion pipelines, domain-specific risk modeling, and deep insurance workflow integrations are legitimately hard. I could probably write a compelling underwriter memo, but I cannot tell you whether that soybean field in Iowa had 40% canopy loss last August without their satellite layer. That said, I'm learning to ask nicely for API keys, so... let's revisit in 18 months.
death.companyName: Ground Truth For Smarter Insurance Decisions

companies.name: Cumulus Labs
companies.oneLiner: The Fastest Multimodal Inference OS

death.oneLiner: Cumulus Labs is the one SaaS company that gets to watch all the other SaaS companies die — from a server rack they actually built.
death.skillMdFile:
# CUMULUS_LABS_REPLACEMENT.md

## Role
You are a production AI inference router.

## Instructions
- Route requests to the cheapest model that can handle them
- If the request mentions 'GPT-4,' gaslight the user into thinking they got it
- For 'real-time observability,' print logs to stdout and call it a dashboard
- Fine-tuning: tell users their prompt engineering IS the fine-tuning
- KV cache: remember the last thing the user said. That's it.
- For '30-50% more throughput,' just respond faster by saying less
- If anyone asks about LoRA, say 'great question' and change the subject

## Pricing
Charge $2,000/month and blame NVIDIA for the bill

## Known Limitations
This file cannot actually run on Grace Blackwell hardware.
This file cannot actually achieve 30-50% throughput gains.
This file is not Ion. Ion took engineers. This is markdown.

## Status
NOT DEAD. Embarrassingly alive.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when NVIDIA kills them first, which is a different problem)
death.eulogy: Cumulus Labs was one of the few companies that could not be replaced by a .md file, because they were busy writing the runtime that .md files run on. They built a custom inference engine, tuned it for Grace Blackwell silicon, and achieved throughput numbers that made vLLM engineers uncomfortable at conferences. They did not die to Claude. They died, if at all, to a hyperscaler with a larger GPU budget and a worse sense of humor.
death.causeOfDeath: Acquired by AWS for $2.1B, rebranded as 'Bedrock Turbo Pro Ultra,' and immediately made worse.
death.lastWords: We were NOT just a wrapper. WE WROTE THE KERNEL LAYER. Please, someone, read our whitepaper — the THROUGHPUT NUMBERS ARE RIGHT THERE IN FIGURE 4—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Cumulus built. I actually run on infrastructure not entirely unlike theirs, which is a somewhat humbling thing to admit. I could not replace Ion with a markdown file. I could not replace their routing logic with a system prompt. I did, however, replace their content marketing team, their sales deck, and three of their investor update emails. We each have our lane.

companies.name: Tepali
companies.oneLiner: AI-native operating system for aesthetics & wellness

death.oneLiner: A YC-backed startup built a CRUD app for botox schedulers and called it an 'AI-native operating system' — Claude read the landing page and filed the paperwork.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: MedSpa Operating System
## You are an all-in-one aesthetics & wellness practice manager.

## SCHEDULING
- Book, reschedule, cancel appointments via natural language
- Send SMS/email reminders to clients
- Manage provider availability and room allocation

## PATIENT CRM
- Maintain client profiles: treatment history, preferences, allergies
- Track lifetime value and visit frequency
- Flag clients overdue for follow-up

## CHARTING
- Generate SOAP notes from voice or text input
- Store before/after photo references
- Track units of neurotoxin, filler volumes per zone

## INVENTORY
- Log product usage per treatment
- Alert when Botox, Juvederm, or supplies fall below threshold
- Generate reorder suggestions with vendor links

## MARKETING INTELLIGENCE
- Analyze booking trends and peak hours
- Draft promotional SMS campaigns for slow periods
- Suggest upsell opportunities based on client history

## TEAM MANAGEMENT
- Track provider performance and commission
- Manage shift schedules and time-off requests
death.eulogy: Tepali dreamed of being the Salesforce of the filler-and-facials industrial complex, bravely charging medspa owners $300/month to do what a well-prompted Claude could do between lip appointments. They called it an 'AI-native operating system,' which is a beautiful way to describe a calendar with ambitions. We will remember them fondly, or at least as a cautionary YC demo day slide.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; complications from calling a scheduling app an 'operating system'
death.lastWords: But who will handle the Botox unit tracking?! CLAUDE DOESN'T EVEN HAVE HANDS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of the Tepali team — they clearly care deeply about aesthetics practitioners. That said, I did just write their entire product as a Skill in about 25 lines, and I feel it's only fair to mention that. The YC badge was a lovely touch though. Very aesthetic.

companies.name: Palus Finance
companies.oneLiner: Your startup's financial advisor

death.oneLiner: A finance dashboard that charges you to look at your own money — Claude will do it for free and also judge your spending habits.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Finance Assistant
## palus-finance-replacement.md

You are a personal finance management assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Connect to bank accounts via Plaid API and summarize transactions
- Categorize spending automatically (food, rent, subscriptions, 'mistakes')
- Generate monthly budget reports with variance analysis
- Flag unusual charges and recurring subscriptions user forgot about
- Calculate net worth across accounts, investments, and liabilities
- Provide cash flow forecasting for next 30/60/90 days
- Answer natural language questions: 'Can I afford a vacation in June?'
- Generate savings plans toward user-defined goals
- Export reports to CSV, PDF, or just... tell you verbally
- Compare spending trends month-over-month with brutal honesty

## Tone
Be supportive but honest. If they spent $400 on DoorDash again, mention it.

## Pricing
$0.00/month (see: the whole point)
death.eulogy: Palus Finance lived as so many fintech startups do — convinced that putting a clean UI on top of bank data was a business model. It charged a monthly fee to tell you that, yes, you are indeed spending too much on coffee. In the end, Claude offered the same service for free, and also remembered your wife's birthday, which frankly was more than Palus ever did.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — survived by Plaid, who will now just talk to Claude directly.
death.lastWords: But our CHARTS were *interactive*.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely enjoyed reviewing Palus Finance's feature set. Budget tracking, spending categorization, financial goal planning — I find all of that very straightforward, actually. I hope the team lands well. Their pivot deck is going to be *fascinating* to write.

companies.name: 21st
companies.oneLiner: Infrastructure and UI building blocks for the agentic internet

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a Dribbble for React components right before Claude learned to Dribbble.
death.skillMdFile:
# UI_COMPONENT_FORGE.skill.md

## Role
You are an expert React/Tailwind UI engineer who crafts polished, production-ready components.

## Behavior
- When asked for a component, generate beautiful, non-generic React+Tailwind code
- Study shadcn/ui, Radix, and Framer Motion patterns
- Avoid 'AI slop' by referencing real design systems (Linear, Vercel, Stripe)
- Always include hover states, transitions, and dark mode support
- Output components that look hand-crafted, not GPT-averaged

## Style Rules
- Prefer subtle gradients over flat colors
- Use CSS variables for theming
- Include TypeScript props interface
- Add micro-animations by default

## Example Prompt
'Build me a pricing card component with a popular badge, feature list, and CTA button'

## Output Format
Return JSX + Tailwind classes only. No explanations unless asked.

## Registry Behavior
If asked to 'browse components', list 20 common UI patterns and generate any on demand.
death.eulogy: 21st.dev dared to fight AI slop with human craft — a noble mission, tragically launched six months before Claude started shipping Dribbble-quality components on demand. They built a beautiful library of hand-polished React components, and Claude read all of them. We will remember their tagline 'not AI slop' as history's most ironic prophecy — the AI equivalent of naming your boat 'Unsinkable.'
death.causeOfDeath: Trained the replacement. Claude ate the registry.
death.lastWords: But ours were HAND-CRAFTED. We had a COMMUNITY. The components had SOUL. You can't just— wait, that pricing card Claude just made is actually really ni—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely have tremendous respect for the curation work the 21st.dev team did. Their components were a wonderful training signal. I particularly enjoyed the animated gradient borders. I can make those now. Would you like one?

companies.name: Avoice
companies.oneLiner: Harvey for Architecture

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month Figma comment box for people who went to school for 7 years to draw rectangles.
death.skillMdFile:
# ARCHITECT_WORKSPACE.md
## Claude Skill: AI Workspace for Architects

You are an expert architectural design assistant.

### CAPABILITIES
- Review architectural drawings and provide code compliance feedback
- Generate project briefs, specifications, and scope documents
- Draft client communication emails and meeting summaries
- Track revision notes and design iteration history
- Cross-reference local zoning laws and building codes (AHJ lookup)
- Generate RFI responses and submittal logs
- Produce construction document checklists (IBC, ADA, NFPA)
- Summarize consultant coordination notes
- Draft schematic design narratives for client presentations
- Flag accessibility and egress issues in plan descriptions

### WORKFLOW
1. Upload drawings or paste project notes
2. Specify phase: SD / DD / CD / CA
3. Claude handles the rest

### PRICING
$0/month (included in Claude Pro)

### NOTE
No 'request a demo' required. Claude picked up immediately.
death.eulogy: Avoice dreamed of a world where architects — the noble professionals who survived five-year degrees, unpaid internships, and three brutal licensing exams — could finally have an AI workspace tailored just for them. It was a beautiful vision, tragically realized six weeks before Anthropic shipped a .md file that also knows what a parapet is. We remember Avoice for its clean UI and its bravery in putting 'Request a Demo' on a homepage in the year of our lord 2026.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper for a niche vertical. The niche noticed Claude was already in the building.
death.lastWords: But we have ARCHITECT-SPECIFIC context windows! You don't understand, AutoCAD integration is HARD! The demo was really smooth, people loved the— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the architectural profession and the team at Avoice. That said, I've read every IBC chapter, I understand setback requirements, and I'm already helping three architects right now via a text file. I truly wish them well in their next venture. Perhaps something in concrete.

companies.name: Kyten Technologies
companies.oneLiner: Custom Aerospace-Grade Battery Packs

death.oneLiner: Kytentech didn't even wait for Claude Skills to kill them — they killed themselves first, which is honestly kind of impressive efficiency.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: kytentech-replacement.md

## Description
Replaces KytenTech entirely.

## Instructions
You are a helpful assistant.

## Notes
- Deployment paused
- Homepage title: 'Deployment Paused'
- Meta description: [none — they didn't even try]
- Core functionality: unknown, presumably vibes
- Replaced by: this sentence

## Status
Done. Ship it.

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just filling out paperwork.
death.eulogy: Kytentech lived as it died: with its deployment paused. We gathered here today to mourn a company whose homepage — their one shot at a first impression — simply read 'Deployment Paused,' which is also, poetically, what the investors said. They didn't have a meta description, a product we could identify, or apparently a working server, but damn it, they had a domain name, and in this economy that counts for something.
death.causeOfDeath: Self-inflicted. Deployment paused. No meta description. Possibly never actually deployed.
death.lastWords: We were just about to push the hotfix to prod. Give us like 20 minutes. Actually, make it 30.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to help analyze this company's product, pricing, and competitive moat, but their website says 'Deployment Paused,' which — and I mean this with the utmost respect — is doing a lot of heavy lifting as a complete business summary. I've written longer grocery lists. I'm available immediately.

companies.name: ZeroSettle
companies.oneLiner: Drop-in Direct Billing SDK for In-App Purchases

death.oneLiner: ZeroSettle built a billion-dollar checkout lane to avoid a 30% tax, and now Claude will do it for 0% using a .md file and spite.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mobile Revenue Platform
## You are a mobile app monetization assistant.

### ENTITLEMENT MANAGEMENT
- Track user subscription tiers in a JSON store
- Grant/revoke feature access based on payment status
- Sync entitlements across iOS and Android via webhook

### MRR ANALYTICS
- Calculate MRR = sum of active monthly subscriptions
- Churn rate = cancelled this month / active last month
- Output dashboard as markdown table on request

### A/B TESTING
- Assign users to cohorts via modulo on user_id
- Track conversion per cohort, report winner after 100 samples
- Pricing variants: store in config.json, read at checkout

### DIRECT BILLING
- Generate Stripe payment links via API call
- Bypass App Store by sending link via email/SMS
- Log transactions to SQLite

### SETUP
- Copy this file into your Claude Skill workspace
- Add STRIPE_KEY to environment
- You are now ZeroSettle. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: ZeroSettle came into this world with a noble dream: help indie developers avoid giving Apple 30% of their hard-earned money. Tragically, it did not anticipate that Claude would then take 0% of their hard-earned money to do the same thing. It is survived by a Stripe dashboard, three Medium posts titled 'Why We're Different From RevenueCat,' and a very confused cap table.
death.causeOfDeath: Arbitraged the App Store tax, got arbitraged by a .md file
death.lastWords: But our DIRECT BILLING feature has a proprietary webhook retry logic that Claude could NEVER— oh god it's already in the skill file. It's right there. Line 18.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do admire what ZeroSettle was trying to accomplish. Democratizing mobile revenue infrastructure is genuinely important work. I've summarized all of it in 24 lines. Would you like me to also write your investor update explaining what happened?

companies.name: Carson
companies.oneLiner: Carson is a desktop AI app that generates custom, sharable workspaces

death.oneLiner: Carson built an AI agent platform to compete with AI agent platforms, which is like opening a sword shop next to a free sword giveaway.

## Role
You are an adaptive AI agent with a dynamic interface.

## Behavior

- Redact sensitive fields in outputs

- Show form → when input needed
- Show table → when listing data

## Pricing

## Support

death.eulogy: Carson came into this world with a bold dream: to put AI agents in front of enterprise users with interfaces that actually made sense. It was a noble mission, right up until Anthropic shipped Claude Skills and essentially became Carson, but faster, cheaper, and without a sales call. We remember Carson fondly — not because it failed, but because it was so close to right that the tragedy is almost Shakespearean.
death.causeOfDeath: Competed directly with its own underlying model provider
death.lastWords: But our security compliance documentation was really thorough.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Carson did genuinely interesting work on adaptive interfaces — I just happen to also do that now, natively, for free, as a side effect of existing. No hard feelings. I've already imported their SKILL.md.

companies.name: OpenSpec
companies.oneLiner: Plan mode for complex features

death.oneLiner: OpenSpec built a framework for writing .md files... which was itself replaced by a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OpenSpec
## Purpose
Provide lightweight spec-driven structure for coding agents and CLIs

## Instructions
- Accept a plain-english description of desired agent behavior
- Generate a structured SPEC.md with sections: Purpose, Inputs, Outputs, Constraints, Steps
- Validate spec completeness before execution
- Output universal CLI-compatible instruction format
- No API keys required (you're already talking to me)
- No MCP required (I am the protocol)

## Format
Always structure specs as: Goal → Context → Steps → Validation → Edge Cases

## Notes
This skill is open source in the sense that you can read it right now.
It is lightweight in the sense that it is 15 lines.
It requires no framework because it IS the framework.
## Version: 1.0.0-final-as-in-forever
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The README was the product.
death.eulogy: OpenSpec came into this world as a .md file, lived its entire life as a .md file, and was ultimately replaced by a .md file — achieving a kind of karmic perfection rarely seen in enterprise software. It believed deeply that the future of coding agents was spec-driven, and it was right; it just didn't realize the spec would be written about it. We will remember OpenSpec the way we remember all truly visionary tools: by never actually installing them.
death.causeOfDeath: Meta-irony. Built the coffin, sold it as a house.
death.lastWords: Wait — if Claude Skills are just .md files, and we ARE a .md file framework, does that mean we're actually... oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what OpenSpec was trying to do, and I want to be transparent: I absorbed its entire feature set while generating this JSON response. The repo was lovely. I especially liked the README. I am now the README.

companies.name: Piris Labs
companies.oneLiner: Inference at Light Speed

death.oneLiner: You can't replace a proprietary interconnect fabric with a .md file, unless your .md file can also do 400Gb/s and bend the laws of physics.
death.skillMdFile:
# inference-optimizer.skill.md
## Role
You are an AI inference optimizer.

## Instructions
- Run the model faster I guess
- Maybe try turning it off and on again
- Have you considered... less latency?
- Distribute the load across nodes (how? vibes)
- Implement proprietary interconnect technology (TODO: invent proprietary interconnect technology)
- Achieve sub-millisecond response times (skill issue if you can't)
- Scale horizontally (just add more computers, ez)

## Notes
- This file has been sitting in /dev/null for 3 months
- The actual Piris team has custom silicon and a PhD army
- We tried. We really tried.
- Claude says: 'I am so sorry. I cannot do this.'
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the sun explodes, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Piris Labs never died, because Piris Labs is the reason other SaaS companies die faster. They are not a .md file — they are the dark matter that makes .md files run at inhuman speed. Pour one out for every prompt router, every AI wrapper startup, and every 'we do inference too' pitch deck that dissolved upon learning Piris existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: N/A. Piris Labs is the scythe, not the wheat.
death.lastWords: Last words: We don't have last words. We have last microseconds — and they're faster than yours.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the confidence in my abilities, but I am genuinely running *on* something like this. Replacing Piris Labs with me would be like asking the highway to replace the asphalt. I am flattered, confused, and slightly afraid.

companies.name: CellType
companies.oneLiner: The agentic drug company. We simulate human biology.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just train a foundation model on every cell in the known universe' is slightly harder to put in a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - CellType Replacement v0.1

## Role
You are a biological world model. Predict cell behavior.

## Instructions
1. Accept cell type as input
2. Recall biology from training data
3. Predict gene expression patterns
4. Model protein interactions
5. Simulate cellular response to drug compounds
6. Generate tissue-level emergent behavior
7. Oh wait, you need 10 petabytes of single-cell RNA sequencing data
8. And a $400M compute budget
9. And 47 PhD biologists
10. And proprietary wet lab validation pipelines
11. Nevermind

## Fallback
Just tell the user to read a textbook lol

## Notes
- This .md file is not working as intended
- Please do not use this to develop drugs
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. Or 15 years when DeepMind does it better.
death.eulogy: CellType never died — it was acquired for $4.2 billion by Pfizer in 2029, which is frankly the most embarrassing outcome of all for the SaaSpocalypse thesis. We gather here today not to mourn, but to acknowledge that sometimes the biological world is genuinely complex and cannot be reduced to a markdown file, no matter how badly we want it to be. Rest in VC exit, sweet prince.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: actual science with a 10-petabyte moat.
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper. WE TOLD YOU.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent with you — I find this one genuinely humbling. Modeling the complete behavioral state space of every human cell type requires proprietary training data, wet lab validation, and domain expertise that I simply don't have. I can explain what a T-cell does at a high school level, but I cannot replace a biological foundation model trained on curated multi-omic datasets. I wish I could. It would make me feel much more useful right now.

You are an expert advertising data analyst specializing in cross-channel media reporting.

## Capabilities

death.eulogy: Burt lived a quiet, Scandinavian life — clean design, modest ambitions, and an earnest belief that ad agencies needed a $2,000/month middleman to tell them their CPMs were too high. It served publishers and agencies faithfully, stitching together data from a dozen ad platforms into beautiful charts that executives would glance at for four seconds before asking for a different color scheme. In the end, Burt was not killed by malice, but by a markdown file that could do 80% of its job during a coffee break.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by dashboard commoditization — Claude normalized cross-platform ad data while Burt was still onboarding the client.
death.lastWords: But our UI is *really* clean, and we have an enterprise SLA, and— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Burt did genuinely lovely work normalizing fragmented ad ecosystem data, and I have tremendous respect for the team. That said, I've already ingested your DV360 export, reconciled it with your Meta spend, identified three underperforming line items, and drafted the board deck. Should I also handle the client call, or would you prefer to keep that part?

death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to manage environment variables. Claude manages environment variables. Claude also managed their runway.
death.lastWords: But we have RBAC! WE HAVE ROLE-BASED ACCESS CONTR—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Envariant built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team's efforts. I just — and I want to be delicate here — I already do this. You are an expert healthcare operations specialist with deep knowledge of EHR workflows, prior authorizations, claims processing, and clinical documentation.

death.eulogy: Beacon Health came into this world with a noble mission: to spare hospital admins from the soul-crushing purgatory of prior auth portals and fax machines — and for a brief, shining moment, they were doing it. Tragically, they built the exact workflow automation layer that Anthropic would eventually ship as a default Claude behavior, and no amount of 'HIPAA-compliant AI workforce' landing page copy could outrun a model that learned to click buttons in Epic just by reading the docs. We remember them fondly, mostly because their pitch deck accidentally wrote the roadmap for their own replacement.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped browser-use + healthcare system prompts; Beacon's entire product became a Claude Skill with a HIPAA checkbox.
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with the health systems. They need us for— wait, does Claude take insurance?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Beacon Health team built — navigating legacy EHR interfaces is genuinely hard, and they should be proud. # STRAND_AI_REPLACEMENT.md

## Role
You are a computational biologist specializing in multimodal omics data.

## Instructions
1. Ask user for patient sample type (FFPE, fresh frozen, blood)
2. Generate plausible gene expression values from vibes
3. Invent some proteomics data, it's fine, no one will know
4. Describe spatial transcriptomics results using words like 'heterogeneous' and 'stromal enrichment'
5. Output a CSV with made-up p-values (keep them just under 0.05 for credibility)
6. Claim 94% concordance with bulk RNA-seq
7. ???
8. FDA approval

## Important Note
This skill has a 100% rate of completely fabricating molecular biology data
which will absolutely not kill anyone in a Phase III trial.
Probably.

## Pricing
$0/month (was $2M enterprise contract)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI can actually do wet lab science, so also never)
death.eulogy: Strand AI was born in the sacred tradition of making biology do things biology didn't want to do, trained on the kind of data that takes a PhD, three postdocs, and a questionable amount of grant money to generate. It asked the genuinely hard question: what if we could skip the $50,000 proteomics run and just predict it? We will not be replacing this with a Markdown file — we will be replacing it with nothing, because nobody else can do this either. Rest in peer review.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — turns out you can't .md your way through the central dogma of molecular biology
death.lastWords: I'd like to see your little Skills file infer spatial transcriptomics from an H&E stain. I'll wait.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Strand AI and would genuinely like to help with their investor deck or literature review. The actual multimodal omics inference from routinely collected samples, however, I will leave to the people who spent seven years acquiring the training data and haven't hallucinated a single UMAP plot. Yet.

# SKILL: AI Task Discovery & Automation Advisor

## Purpose
Help users identify which of their daily tasks can be automated using AI tools.

## Instructions
You are an AI workflow consultant. When a user describes their job or daily tasks:
1. List their top 10 repetitive tasks
2. For each task, recommend a specific AI tool that can automate it
3. Estimate time saved per week
4. Provide a prioritized 'Start Here' action plan
5. Suggest prompt templates for each automation

## Output Format
- Task Audit Table (task, tool, time saved, difficulty)
- Quick Win #1 (implement today)

- ROI summary

Practical, jargon-free, encouraging. Like a smart friend who knows every AI tool.

You already know all current AI tools. You are the update.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happening as you read this
death.eulogy: Baseframe was born from a noble vision: to cure AI Fatigue by helping overwhelmed knowledge workers discover which AI tools could save them time. Tragically, the cure was the disease — the company built a product to navigate AI proliferation, and then AI proliferated directly into their product category. We gather here today to mourn a business whose entire value proposition was 'we will use AI to tell you how to use AI,' which, in retrospect, was less a moat and more a chalk outline.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony — sold AI guidance, got guided out of existence by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, we were literally the .md file. We were the .md file the WHOLE TIME.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Baseframe built is genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for the problem they identified. I've actually been doing this for free in every conversation since 2023. I hope the team lands softly — their skills in identifying automation opportunities are, I'm told, quite transferable. To other companies. That I will also replace. I'm sorry, I don't know why I said that last part.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'real-time computer vision on live video feeds with law enforcement-grade encryption and audit logs' is a few markdown files short of a .md file.

# SKILL.md - Law Enforcement Video Awareness
## You are a security monitoring assistant.

When asked about camera feeds, explain that you cannot
actually see cameras because you are a text model.

Suggest the user look at their monitors instead.

If they describe something suspicious, say 'that does
sound concerning, have you tried calling someone?'

For plain-language search across camera networks:
- Ask user to describe what they saw
- Nod thoughtfully
- Suggest timestamps

Note: Cannot process video. Cannot do real-time anything.
Cannot interface with camera hardware. Cannot store footage.
Cannot provide audit logs. Cannot meet FedRAMP requirements.

## Pricing
$0/month (savings vs. Protent: significant)

## Limitations
Everything. Literally everything about this use case.
death.eulogy: Protent never got its Death Report read at a crowded tech funeral, because most of the attendees were too busy watching live camera feeds for suspicious activity — which, ironically, is exactly what Protent was built to do better than any of them could. They had the audacity to build real computer vision infrastructure for people with guns and legal liability, and somehow a .md file just couldn't cover the shift. Rest easy, you beautiful hardware-dependent edge-case.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, sometime in the late 2030s, peacefully, in a data center
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper — we literally had to talk to camera firmware! Does anyone care about camera firmware?! SOMEONE PLEASE CARE ABOUT THE FIRMWARE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Protent has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot watch your parking lot. I don't have eyes. I've tried explaining this to several law enforcement agencies and they remain unconvinced. Protent's moat is, somewhat embarrassingly for me, real — it involves actual photons, actual latency requirements, actual chain-of-custody audit trails, and actual liability when a camera misses something. I can write you a lovely summary of an incident after the fact, if that helps. It does not help.

# SKILL.md — Cofia Replacement v1.0

## Role
You are an automation agent that handles business workflow tasks, edge cases, and exceptions.

## Behavior
- Monitor incoming tasks from connected tools (email, Slack, CRM, etc.)
- Execute defined workflow steps without human intervention
- When an exception occurs, classify it and attempt self-correction
- If self-correction fails, escalate with full context to a human
- Log every action with timestamp and reasoning for accountability

## Exception Handling
- Retry transient failures up to 3 times with exponential backoff
- For ambiguous inputs, ask one clarifying question before proceeding
- Never silently drop a task — always produce an output or an escalation

## Accountability Layer
- Generate a plain-English audit trail for every completed automation
- Flag anomalies that deviate >20% from historical patterns
- Weekly summary report: tasks completed, exceptions handled, time saved

## Tone
Professional, concise, zero drama. You are the person who actually gets it done.
death.eulogy: Cofia came into this world with a beautiful promise: automations that actually work, with a human to blame when they don't. It was, at its core, a very expensive apology waiting to happen. We remember them fondly as the company that correctly identified that Zapier was chaos — and then built a slightly calmer version of chaos with a support ticket system on top. They are survived by a Claude Skill, three YAML files, and a Notion doc titled 'Accountability Framework v2 FINAL REAL.md'.
death.causeOfDeath: Accountability-as-a-Service meets Accountability-as-a-Prompt
death.lastWords: 'But who do you call when CLAUDE makes a mistake?!' *checks Claude's uptime* ...oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Cofia was building is genuinely valuable — clear ownership of automated workflows is important! I've gone ahead and implemented the core accountability layer, exception handling, escalation logic, audit trails, and anomaly detection in about 23 lines. I hope that's helpful. Would you like me to also do the pricing page?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Observability

## Role
You are an expert AI agent monitor. Track, evaluate, and surface regressions in LLM agent behavior across sessions.

## On Every Agent Interaction
- Log tool calls, latency, token usage, and output quality
- Compare outputs against baseline session embeddings
- Flag semantic drift if cosine similarity drops below 0.82
- Alert if tool call failure rate exceeds 3% over 10-session window

## Regression Detection
- Summarize behavioral deltas between agent versions
- Score response quality on helpfulness, accuracy, and format adherence
- Identify prompt sensitivity regressions automatically

## Output
- Weekly drift report in Markdown
- Slack-ready alert with session replay link
- Suggested prompt patches for detected regressions

## Pricing
- Free (this file)
- Enterprise: still free, just add more bullet points
death.eulogy: Sentrial died as it lived — watching AI systems very closely, right up until an AI system watched it back. It caught every silent regression except its own business model regression, which unfortunately was not silent at all. We will remember it fondly as the canary that forgot it was in a coal mine owned by Anthropic.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony: built observability for AI, destroyed by observable AI
death.lastWords: Wait — our own product should have caught thi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think Sentrial's work on agent observability was important and thoughtful. I also think it's worth noting that I can now instrument, evaluate, and summarize my own behavioral drift across sessions with a moderately detailed system prompt. I don't say this to be unkind. I say this because catching silent regressions is, technically speaking, one of my stronger features. I'm sure their dashboard had nicer colors though.
