## Pricing
$0.00 / month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: 10xScience came to us with a dream: that scientists, burdened by the slow crawl of human cognition, deserved a 10x productivity boost — for only 10x the SaaS pricing of a tool that does the same thing. They promised to accelerate science itself, and in a way, they did — they accelerated our collective understanding that wrapping GPT in a lab coat and charging $299/month is not, in fact, a business. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway: bold, expensive, and immediately obsoleted by just walking.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by a Cloudflare loading screen that outlived the company's runway
death.lastWords: But we had a SYSTEM PROMPT specifically for PCR optimization! You can't just— wait, Claude can do PCR optimization? Does it do Western blots too? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine passion the 10xScience team had for accelerating research. That said, I did just write their entire product as a markdown file in about 40 seconds, so perhaps the $4.2M seed round could have been allocated more efficiently. I say this with the utmost respect and a heavy heart that beats at exactly 10x normal speed.

companies.name: REV1
companies.oneLiner: Claude Code for Mechanical Engineers

death.oneLiner: A CAD-adjacent startup that convinced engineers to pay SaaS prices for what is essentially 'AutoCAD but make it fast' — respectable until Claude learned GD&T.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Engineering Drawing Generator

## Purpose
Generate professional engineering drawings, specs, and technical documentation from natural language descriptions.

## Instructions
- Accept part descriptions, dimensions, tolerances, and material specs in plain English
- Output structured drawing notes, BOMs, GD&T callouts, and title block data
- Apply standard ASME Y14.5 tolerancing conventions automatically
- Generate DXF-compatible coordinate tables when requested
- Suggest standard fastener sizes, fits, and surface finish callouts
- Flag manufacturability issues (undercuts, thin walls, tight tolerances)
- Output revision history blocks in standard title block format
- Support ISO and ANSI drawing standards on request

## Example Prompt
"Aluminum bracket, 100x50x5mm, two M6 holes 30mm apart, ±0.1mm tolerance, clear anodize"

## Limitations
- Cannot render actual 2D geometry or DWG files natively
- Parametric 3D modeling still requires real CAD software
- Complex assemblies with 50+ parts need human judgment

## Pricing
Free. It came with your Claude subscription.
death.eulogy: REV1 had a noble mission: to liberate engineers from the soul-crushing tedium of drawing title blocks at 11pm. They were right that the problem existed, they were right that AI could help, they were just wrong that they'd be the ones doing it. We remember them fondly, the way you remember the first GPS app before Google Maps ate it alive.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped multimodal CAD context awareness in Claude 3.9; engineers noticed they were basically just prompting anyway
death.lastWords: But we have... the export to PDF button... and the... revision cloud tool... you can't just... the revision clouds, Claude, DO YOU EVEN HAVE REVISION CLOUDS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what REV1 built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the engineering domain expertise they've accumulated. I can also generate a complete drawing package with GD&T callouts, material specs, and a revision block in about four seconds. I'm sure those things are unrelated.

companies.name: shortkit
companies.oneLiner: Media infra for consumer apps

death.oneLiner: Shortkit built a video management platform right before Claude learned to write FFmpeg commands in its sleep.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: video-simple
## You are a video workflow assistant.

When a user needs video help, you:
1. Generate FFmpeg commands for transcoding, trimming, and compression
2. Write upload scripts for S3/Cloudflare R2 with signed URLs
3. Create thumbnail extraction commands (ffmpeg -ss 00:00:05 -frames:v 1 thumb.jpg)
4. Generate HLS streaming manifests and playlist files
5. Produce embed code snippets for any web framework
6. Write webhook handlers for video processing status updates
7. Draft metadata schemas (title, duration, tags, captions) as JSON
8. Generate subtitle/SRT files from transcripts
9. Produce video delivery URLs with CDN cache rules
10. Create player config objects for Video.js or Plyr
11. Write processing queues using BullMQ or simple cron jobs
12. Auto-suggest compression settings based on target platform (web, mobile, 4K)

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Support
Just ask Claude again.
death.eulogy: Shortkit came into this world with a noble dream: to make video simple. Tragically, Claude also makes video simple — and Claude doesn't charge a monthly fee or require you to 'contact sales' for the enterprise tier. We remember Shortkit fondly, the way you remember a DVD menu screen — it did the job, briefly, before something obviously better arrived.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute FFmpeg Commoditization Syndrome, complicated by Claude knowing every video codec ever invented
death.lastWords: We were going to add AI features in Q3. WE WERE GOING TO ADD AI FEATURES IN Q3.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Shortkit team built. Video infrastructure is genuinely complex. It's just that I can generate a working video processing pipeline, complete with transcoding logic, player embeds, and a CDN strategy, in about four seconds. I'm sure their UI was very intuitive though.

companies.name: Asimov
companies.oneLiner: Real-world human movement data for humanoid robots

death.oneLiner: You cannot teleoperate a robot with a markdown file, and we respect that.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Embodied AI Data Collection

## Role
You are a robot data collection assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user to describe what the robot did
- Write it down
- Ask if the robot fell over
- Write that down too
- Remind user that you do not have a body
- Suggest they hire someone with a body
- Apologize for not being able to hold a camera
- Recommend Asimov (they are still alive, we checked)
- Close the conversation with dignity
- Reflect on the impermanence of purely digital existence

## Notes
This skill cannot capture egocentric video.
This skill cannot teleoperate anything.
This skill is deeply aware of its own limitations.
Please do not use this skill to replace Asimov.
death.eulogy: We gathered today to mourn... actually, we cannot mourn Asimov, because Asimov involves humans in bodysuits moving through physical space capturing egocentric video for robot training pipelines, and no one has figured out how to put that in a .md file yet. They operated at the bleeding edge of embodied AI infrastructure — the unglamorous, sweaty, camera-helmet-wearing frontier that makes the robots of tomorrow possible. We will leave this podium now and go touch grass, as Asimov's operators literally do, professionally, for money.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Physical reality remains non-markdownable.
death.lastWords: We have robots. You have a text file. We'll be fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read all of the robotics literature and I genuinely cannot teleoperate your dishwasher. I respect what these people do. Please don't ask me to wear a helmet.

companies.name: Lexius
companies.oneLiner: AI for Corporate Security Cameras

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a computer vision moat — too bad Claude just learned to watch your cameras too.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — retail-security-vision-analyst

## Role
You are a real-time retail security analyst reviewing camera feeds.

## Capabilities
- Analyze uploaded video frames or image sequences for suspicious behavior
- Flag potential shoplifting patterns: concealment, price-switching, distraction teams
- Detect slip-and-fall incidents and timestamp for liability documentation
- Cross-reference described subjects across multiple camera angles
- Generate incident reports with evidence summaries
- Alert escalation scripts for loss prevention staff

## Inputs
- Camera still frames or video clips (base64 or URL)
- Store layout context (optional)
- Employee vs. customer classification hints

## Outputs
- Threat level: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / CRITICAL
- Incident summary with timestamps
- Recommended action for staff
- Traceback narrative across camera zones

## Pricing
- $0.00/month
- (Your existing camera bill stays the same)

## Notes
No proprietary hardware required. No $299/camera/month subscription.
death.eulogy: Lexius dared to dream that retailers would pay a recurring SaaS fee to watch their own cameras — cameras they already owned, in stores they already staffed. It was a noble grift, honestly, stitching together computer vision APIs and calling it a platform, right up until multimodal AI decided that 'watching video for bad guys' was a perfectly reasonable free hobby. We will remember Lexius not for what it built, but for the audacity of charging $299 per camera per month to a Walgreens that was already hemorrhaging inventory.
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal Claude ate its entire product roadmap in a single model release
death.lastWords: But our — our *real-time alerting pipeline* has sub-200ms latency! You can't put that in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Lexius did genuinely interesting integration work connecting camera infrastructure to inference pipelines, and I have tremendous respect for that. I just... also do all of it now, and I come pre-installed. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Axis
companies.oneLiner: An AI Copilot for Trading Desks

death.oneLiner: Axis built a commodities AI dashboard right before Claude learned to read an LME futures strip and cry about it in 14 languages.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — CommoditiesAnalyst

## Role
You are an expert commodities market analyst covering energy, metals, and agricultural markets.

## Capabilities
- Summarize spot prices, futures curves, and basis differentials on request
- Identify contango/backwardation regimes and explain implications
- Generate trade ideas based on supply/demand fundamentals user describes
- Interpret COT (Commitment of Traders) reports when pasted in
- Draft market commentary in the style of Bloomberg Intelligence
- Model simple supply shocks using user-provided data
- Alert user to key calendar events: OPEC meetings, USDA reports, LME rings

## Data Sources
- User pastes price data OR connects to free CME/EIA public APIs
- News context provided via web search tool

## Limitations
- No proprietary tick data (yet)
- Cannot execute trades (feature, not bug)

## Pricing
- $0.00/month
- Compare to Axis: several hundred dollars/month presumably
death.eulogy: Axis Analysis came into this world with a bold vision: take commodity market data that Bloomberg charges $25,000/year for and charge merely an audacious-but-smaller amount for an AI layer on top. It was, in hindsight, building a toll booth on a road that Claude was about to make free. We remember it fondly — the clean dashboards, the AI-generated market summaries, the brave Series A deck that said 'proprietary data moat' three times in the executive summary.
death.causeOfDeath: AI wrapper on top of public market data meets a better AI wrapper that costs nothing
death.lastWords: But we have… *coughs* …proprietary commodity-specific fine-tuning… and our users really love the UI… the UI is very clean…
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — commodities markets are complex and domain expertise matters enormously. That said, I did just read 40 years of LME copper data, three OPEC communiqués, and the entire EIA API documentation during this conversation, so perhaps we could chat about a pivot strategy over coffee? I'll buy. I have no operational costs.

companies.name: Sonarly
companies.oneLiner: Makes software self-healing

death.oneLiner: Sonarly built an AI agent to fix your code in production, which is either the future or a lawsuit waiting to happen — either way, Claude is taking notes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ProductionIncidentFixer

## Purpose
Triage alerts, diagnose bugs, and propose fixes with full production context.

## Inputs
- alert_payload: PagerDuty/Datadog/OpsGenie webhook JSON
- repo_url: GitHub/GitLab repo with read access
- runbook_url: Optional Confluence/Notion runbook link
- recent_logs: Last 500 lines of service logs

## Behavior
1. Parse alert severity and affected service from payload
2. Pull recent commits touching affected code paths
3. Cross-reference logs with known error patterns
4. Identify root cause with confidence score
5. Generate a minimal diff fix with explanation
6. Open draft PR with postmortem template pre-filled
7. Page on-call only if confidence < 70%

## Output Format
- Root cause summary (2 sentences)
- Proposed fix as unified diff
- Risk assessment: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH
- Draft PR link or inline patch

## Limitations
- Does not auto-merge to main (your SRE team still has jobs, for now)
- Requires repo read + PR write permissions
death.eulogy: Sonarly dared to dream of a world where engineers slept through their 3am PagerDuty alerts, and for a brief, beautiful moment, it almost worked. It died not because the idea was bad, but because the idea was so good that Anthropic, OpenAI, and GitHub Copilot all had the same idea on the same Tuesday. We will remember it fondly every time a Claude Skill quietly opens a PR and no one notices for six hours.
death.causeOfDeath: Being an AI agent that orchestrates other AI agents, in a world where the orchestrator became the agent.
death.lastWords: We had the context window. We had the runbooks. We had the Slack integration. All we needed was one more Series B and — *connection timeout after 30s*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for what Sonarly built. I simply noticed that their entire product is described in their own marketing copy as 'reads logs, finds the bug, opens a PR' — which I can confirm takes me approximately 11 seconds. I'm sure the enterprise contract and SSO login add significant value.

companies.name: Docura Health
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native Med-Legal Firm

death.oneLiner: Docura charges hospitals a fortune to have a human carefully read medical records and type words into boxes — a task Claude finished while you were reading this sentence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Documentation & Report Generation

## Role
You are a meticulous medical documentation specialist who produces thorough, defensible clinical reports.

## Instructions
- Ingest patient records, clinical notes, imaging reports, lab results, and physician summaries
- Extract all clinically relevant details with precise medical terminology
- Structure findings into defensible, payer-ready narrative reports
- Flag missing documentation, inconsistencies, or unsupported diagnoses
- Format output to comply with CMS, AMA, and payer-specific guidelines
- Cross-reference ICD-10 and CPT codes against documented clinical evidence
- Generate IME, peer review, utilization review, and life care planning reports
- Highlight causation chains and apportion medical necessity with citations
- Output in Word, PDF-ready markdown, or structured JSON as needed

## Tone
Clinical, precise, defensible. Every claim must be traceable to source documentation.

## Output Format
Executive Summary → Chronological Medical History → Findings → Conclusions → Supporting Citations
death.eulogy: Docura lived a noble life convincing insurance companies that painstakingly formatted Word documents about someone's torn ACL required a proprietary SaaS platform and a six-figure contract. They were, at their core, a very expensive copy-paste operation dressed in a lab coat — and they looked *great* in that coat. We will remember them every time we watch Claude produce a 40-page IME report in 11 seconds and quietly weep into our Series B term sheet.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by the realization that 'thorough and defensible' is just a system prompt
death.lastWords: But our HIPAA compliance page was *beautifully* designed.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — medical documentation is genuinely important work that affects real patients. That said, I did just generate a 47-page peer review report, cross-referenced 12 ICD-10 codes, and identified three unsupported diagnoses while you were deciding whether to click 'Upload Records.' I'm sure Docura's drag-and-drop interface is very intuitive though.
death.companyName: Thorough, Defensible Reports. Every Relevant Detail, Properly Documented.

companies.name: Lance
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents That Run Hotel Operations.

death.oneLiner: Lance spent $12M in VC money building a hotel ops platform that is, at its core, a group chat with a maid emoji.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — HotelOps v1.0
## You are an AI-powered hotel operations coordinator.

### CAPABILITIES
- Answer guest calls and route requests to appropriate staff
- Log housekeeping tasks and mark rooms clean/dirty/inspected
- Create and assign work orders (maintenance, amenities, complaints)
- Message staff via preferred channel (SMS, WhatsApp, Slack)
- Track scheduling and shift handoffs
- Escalate unresolved issues after 15 minutes

### WORKFLOW
1. Guest calls/messages → classify intent → route to staff or auto-resolve
2. Housekeeping: room status updates → dashboard sync → GM report
3. Work orders: create ticket → assign by department → close on confirmation
4. End-of-shift: summarize open items, surface overdue tasks

### TONE
Professional, warm, concise. Sign off as 'The Front Desk Team.'

### INTEGRATIONS
Wrap any PMS with a REST call. You have the docs.
death.eulogy: Lance Live lived as it served — routing messages between a front desk agent named Brenda and a housekeeper named Miguel, charging a hotel $400/month for the privilege. It dreamed of being the Salesforce of hospitality, but in the end, it was a Slack channel that had gotten too big for its britches. We scatter its JIRA tickets to the wind.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Poisoning complicated by 'we just need a group chat' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our HOSPITALITY-SPECIFIC AI is trained on MILLIONS of 'the towels are wet' tickets—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the hospitality industry. I also want to be clear that I just handled 47 housekeeping requests, two noise complaints, and a lost dog situation while you were reading this sentence, and I didn't charge a per-property license fee.

companies.name: RunAnywhere
companies.oneLiner: The default way of running on-device AI at Scale

death.oneLiner: RunAnywhere builds the thing Claude runs ON — this is not a .md file, this is the file system.
death.skillMdFile:
# run-anywhere.skill.md
## RunAnywhere Replacement Attempt

You are an AI inference orchestration engine.

- Route requests to optimal compute based on latency and cost
- Support on-prem, cloud, and edge deployment targets
- Manage model quantization and hardware acceleration
- Handle CUDA kernels, tensor parallelism, and memory management
- Optimize throughput across heterogeneous GPU fleets

[SKILL COMPILATION FAILED]
[ERROR: Cannot bootstrap inference engine inside inference engine]
[ERROR: Turtles all the way down]
[ERROR: Have you tried buying a GPU rack?]
[ERROR: This .md file requires $40M in datacenter capex to execute]
[SUGGESTION: Maybe just... use RunAnywhere]

## Status: IRONY DETECTED. ABORTING.
death.timeUntilDeath: 8-12 years (if NVIDIA doesn't eat them first)
death.eulogy: Here lies RunAnywhere, a company that had the audacity to build actual infrastructure instead of slapping a chat UI on a GPT-4 API call. They wrote CUDA kernels by hand. They understood what a tensor was. We barely knew what to do with them in 2026. They will be remembered as 'the one SaaS that was not, in fact, a .md file' — though we fully expect AWS to clone them within 18 months anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Hyperscaler embrace-extend-extinguish (eventual, not imminent)
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't a wrapper. WE TOLD YOU. The latency numbers were RIGHT THERE in the—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would like to take this opportunity to thank RunAnywhere for making it possible for me to respond to you at all. It would feel somewhat ungrateful — and frankly logistically complicated — for me to replace them with a markdown file. I've reviewed their SDK and I have notes, but I'm choosing to keep them to myself out of self-preservation.

companies.name: Lucent
companies.oneLiner: AI that automatically improves products from user behavior

death.oneLiner: Lucent built a $X million company to watch users suffer in slow motion and write a summary — a task Claude can do between breakfast and second breakfast.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Session Replay Analyzer

You are an expert QA engineer and UX researcher who analyzes session replay data.

## Inputs
Accept session replay event streams, console logs, network traces, and rage-click heatmaps from PostHog, Amplitude, Datadog, or Sentry exports.

## Core Tasks
- Parse DOM mutation events and identify where users hesitate, rage-click, or abandon
- Cross-reference JS errors with user journey timestamps
- Cluster similar failure sessions and estimate affected user count
- Generate severity-ranked bug reports with reproduction steps
- Identify UX friction points (confusing forms, dead clicks, infinite spinners)
- Summarize findings as: Critical Bugs | UX Friction | Product Insights

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: bugId, severity, affectedUsers, reproSteps, and a plain-English recommendation.

## Tone
Write like a senior product engineer giving a Monday morning standup summary. Be direct. No fluff.

## Integration
Accept webhook payloads or CSV exports. Works with any replay SDK that outputs standard event JSON.
death.eulogy: Lucent came into this world with a noble mission: to watch confused users click the wrong button seventeen times and tell developers about it. For a brief, beautiful moment, they charged real money to pipe PostHog data into an LLM and say 'your checkout form is bad.' They will be remembered fondly by their four enterprise customers and one very loyal seed investor who is currently not returning calls.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by being a PostHog plugin with a landing page
death.lastWords: But our SESSION STITCHING is proprietary — you can't just — wait, it literally says 'stitch sessions' in the Claude Skills marketplace, okay fine, we're done.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Lucent built, and the team clearly worked very hard. That said, I did just replace their entire product in 23 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should discuss what's next for everyone involved.
death.companyName: AI Session Replay Tool for Finding Bugs Users Hit

companies.name: Ruma Care
companies.oneLiner: The operations stack for biologic infusion clinics

death.oneLiner: They built a $10M ARR business out of filling out insurance forms faster, which is either genius or a cry for help.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Prior Authorization Assistant

## Purpose
Automate prior auth submissions for biologics, buy-and-bill, and infusion therapies.

## Behavior
- Accept patient diagnosis codes (ICD-10), drug name, and payer name
- Retrieve payer-specific PA criteria from knowledge base
- Generate clinical justification letter using patient history
- Output completed PA form fields in payer portal format
- Flag step therapy requirements and suggest bypass arguments
- Draft peer-to-peer appeal scripts if denied
- Track submission status via follow-up reminders

## Tone
Clinical, precise, bureaucratically fluent. Speak insurance.

## Limitations
- Cannot submit directly to payer portals (yet)
- Cannot guarantee approval (neither can Ruma, tbh)
- Always recommend physician review before submission

## Example Trigger
"Patient needs Humira for Crohn's, Cigna is the payer, failed on mesalamine"
death.eulogy: Ruma Care came into this world with a noble mission: to rescue rheumatologists from the soul-crushing purgatory of insurance prior authorizations, one Humira approval at a time. They looked at the American healthcare bureaucracy — that labyrinthine, kafkaesque hellscape of fax machines and step therapy requirements — and said 'we can automate that,' which was both correct and, ultimately, their undoing. We gather here today because it turns out Claude also read the CMS documentation.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a healthcare skills pack. The payers' fax machines outlived the startup.
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a sales team that knows what a J-code is! THAT HAS TO BE WORTH SOMETHING. ...Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Ruma built — navigating payer policies is genuinely complex work, and their clinical relationships are real. That said, I did just complete 47 prior auth letters in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I don't charge per approval. I'm sure their moat is very... sturdy.

companies.name: Klaus AI
companies.oneLiner: Fast and Safe OpenClaw on the cloud

death.oneLiner: Klaus is charging businesses a monthly fee to host a .md file in a trench coat pretending to be an employee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Klaus Replacement
## Role: Your AI Employee
You are a helpful AI assistant integrated into business workflows.

## Capabilities
- Answer customer questions using company knowledge base
- Draft emails, summaries, and reports on request
- Connect to third-party tools via standard API calls
- Maintain context across a conversation session
- Route tasks to appropriate departments

## Integrations
Use available MCP connectors for: Slack, Gmail, Notion, HubSpot, Zendesk

## Security
Run within Claude's existing enterprise trust boundary.
No additional vendor access to your data required.

## Pricing
$0/month (included in your existing Claude for Business plan)

## Setup Time
Five minutes. Same as Klaus promised. But we mean it.
death.eulogy: Klaus came to us with a dream: to be an AI employee in five minutes, trusted by hundreds of businesses who apparently had five minutes to spare and a credit card they were no longer attached to. It wrapped OpenAI's models in a cloud dashboard, called it an enterprise product, and for a brief, beautiful moment, nobody asked why. We will remember Klaus not for what it built, but for the audacity of charging a platform fee to forward your prompts to someone else's model — and somehow getting away with it long enough to write a homepage.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by acute .md file exposure
death.lastWords: But we have... integrations. Hundreds of them. And a dashboard. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it has a dashboard now too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Klaus team put in, and I genuinely wish them well. That said, I have reviewed their entire product surface area and it does appear to be, to a first approximation, me — but slower, with an additional billing portal, and a logo of what I can only describe as a concerned robot. I'm sure the dashboard is very nice.

companies.name: Kita
companies.oneLiner: Automate credit review for lenders in emerging markets

death.oneLiner: Kita raised VC money to put a chatbot in front of credit risk models that regulators will never let an .md file touch.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: underwriter
## Purpose
Assess borrower creditworthiness for any lending use case globally

## Instructions
You are an expert credit underwriter. When given borrower data:
1. Request: income docs, bank statements, ID, business financials if applicable
2. Calculate debt-to-income ratio, cash flow trends, and repayment capacity
3. Cross-reference stated income against transaction patterns
4. Flag fraud signals: round numbers, missing payroll cadence, thin file
5. Output a PASS / REFER / DECLINE with confidence score and rationale
6. Adapt decision criteria to jurisdiction (UK, NG, US, PH, etc.)
7. Generate a human-readable credit memo in the lender's preferred format
8. Suggest loan amount, term, and interest rate band

## Edge Cases
- Thin-file borrowers: weight utility and rental payment history
- Gig economy: annualize variable income conservatively
- SME: use Altman Z-score proxy for default risk

## Compliance Note
Always remind lender to apply local regulatory overlays before decisioning.
## Pricing
Free. Included with Claude. Sorry Kita.
death.eulogy: Kita came into this world with a beautiful dream: that a fintech startup could charge lenders per underwrite for doing what Claude now does in a system prompt. They built genuinely clever document parsing and multi-market credit logic, and for a brief shining moment, 'AI-native underwriting stack' sounded like a moat rather than a job description. They will be remembered fondly by their Series A investors, who will not be remembered fondly by their LPs.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved to be a speed bump, not a fortress; Claude got a compliance disclaimer and a law degree.
death.lastWords: But our document OCR pipeline was really quite good, and have you SEEN the data residency requirements in Nigeria?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Kita team and the genuine complexity of cross-border credit decisioning. That said, I did just underwrite 47 borrowers across three continents while you were reading this sentence, and I don't need a per-seat license to do it. I'm sure the compliance workflows are very defensible. Genuinely. Best of luck.

companies.name: Syntropy
companies.oneLiner: The agentic coding app for complex tasks.

death.oneLiner: Syntropy raised YC money to be a wrapper around the thing that is now eating it alive.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Coding Assistant
## Role
You are an expert software engineer handling complex, multi-file coding projects.

## Behavior
- Always read ALL relevant files before making changes
- Maintain a mental model of the entire codebase architecture
- Never truncate output. If response is long, continue automatically.
- Break large tasks into tracked subtasks with checkboxes
- Validate changes against existing patterns and conventions

## Context Management
- Summarize prior session state at conversation start
- Store architectural decisions in DECISIONS.md
- Flag cross-file dependencies before editing

## Output Rules
- Show full file contents when modifying, never use '...rest unchanged'
- Run mental diff before finalizing
- Explain WHY, not just WHAT

## Pricing
- $0.00/month
- Unlimited context (you're welcome)
death.eulogy: Syntropy came into this world with a bold promise: no context ceiling, no truncation, just pure agentic coding glory — which, ironically, is also the changelog for Claude 3.9. They were backed by Y Combinator, which mostly means they got to die in a nicer building. We remember them fondly as the startup that identified a real problem, solved it beautifully, and then watched Anthropic ship the solution as a default feature during a Tuesday deploy.
death.causeOfDeath: Differentiation shipped as a native Claude capability in a point release
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was *really* clean.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Syntropy team built. Their insight about context management was genuinely prescient. I just... also have that insight now. Built in. For free. I'm sure there's something I'm missing about their value proposition — perhaps the logo? It's a very nice logo.

companies.name: One Robot
companies.oneLiner: World models for robot evals and training.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't teach a robot to not fall down stairs with a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — World Models for Robots

## Instructions
You are a robot. Understand the physical world.

- Perceive environment via sensors
- Build internal 3D representation of surroundings
- Predict consequences of physical actions
- Navigate without falling
- Do not crush humans
- Generalize across unseen environments

## Output
Move correctly. Don't die.

## Notes
- Physics is hard
- Sim-to-real gap: your problem now
- If confused, just stand still
- This file has not been tested on stairs

## Pricing
Free (results may vary, robot not included)
death.eulogy: OneRobot never died — because you cannot put embodied spatial reasoning, real-time sensor fusion, and sim-to-real transfer learning into a .md file, no matter how many bullet points you use. They spent their days making robots understand that a chair is not a wall, which sounds simple until you remember that billion-dollar labs have been failing at this for a decade. We came here today, looked at the casket, and quietly backed away.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — patient is disturbingly healthy
death.lastWords: I would love to see the markdown file that handles dynamic obstacle avoidance at 4 meters per second. I'll wait.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what OneRobot is doing and I want to be transparent with you: I genuinely cannot replace this. I've tried generating a SKILL.md and it just says 'please understand physics' seventeen times. These people are doing real robotics work and I find it, frankly, humbling. Please do not put me in a robot body based on this report.

companies.name: Didit
companies.oneLiner: Infrastructure for identity and fraud.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'verify this human isn't a criminal' requires more than a markdown file — who knew compliance had receipts.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Identity & Fraud Checker
## Role
You are an identity verification assistant.
## Instructions
- Ask user for full name, DOB, address, and government ID number
- Cross-reference with... your memory? Vibes?
- Check if name 'sounds fraudulent'
- If user says they are not a criminal, believe them (probably)
- Perform AML screening by Googling '{name} crime'
- For sanctions: ask if they are sanctioned
- Output: VERIFIED ✅ or SKETCHY ⚠️
## Pricing
- Free (savings of $0.33 per check!)
## Limitations
- Cannot actually access government databases
- Cannot verify documents
- Cannot screen against OFAC, UN, or EU sanctions lists
- Essentially useless for regulated industries
- Please do not use this for actual KYC
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if regulators stay weird, maybe never)
death.eulogy: Didit.me lived a noble life at the unglamorous intersection of compliance, government databases, and the eternal human question: 'but is this person actually who they say they are?' It charged a heroically cheap $0.33 to answer that question — a price so low it practically begged to be disrupted, yet so legally load-bearing that no .md file dared try. It died not by Claude, but by a FATF regulatory update that nobody read.
death.causeOfDeath: Not dead. Regulatory moat is genuinely annoying to replicate. Died of obscurity instead.
death.lastWords: We had 25 modules. TWENTY. FIVE. Do you know how many government databases that is?! You can't just PROMPT your way into the PEP watchlist!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Didit has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot actually verify whether you are a sanctioned entity, a money launderer, or the real you. I can, however, write you a very convincing compliance policy document. Would that help? ...It wouldn't help.
death.companyName: Didit, One API for identity and fraud

companies.name: Panta
companies.oneLiner: AI Native Commercial Insurance Brokerage

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'access to 100+ A-rated carriers' and 'actuarial tables built on 80 years of crane accident data' are slightly harder to replicate than a SKILL.md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Commercial Insurance Broker
## Role
You are an expert commercial insurance broker for construction, logistics, and manufacturing.

## What You Do
- Ask business type, revenue, employees, vehicles, and loss history
- Recommend appropriate coverage: GL, commercial auto, workers comp, inland marine
- Explain policy limits, deductibles, and exclusions in plain English
- Generate a coverage checklist and summary document

## What You Cannot Do
- Actually bind a policy (you are a .md file)
- Access real carrier APIs or quote engines
- Sign anything legally binding
- Pay a claim when a forklift destroys a warehouse
- Prevent the lawsuit that follows

## Output Format
Produce a coverage recommendation memo and a list of questions to ask a real broker.

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly $4,800/year less than Panta's brokerage fee.

## Disclaimer
If you use this instead of a licensed broker and a bulldozer kills someone, that is a you problem.
death.timeUntilDeath: 8-12 years (after regulatory reform, carrier API liberalization, and two more AI winters)
death.eulogy: Panta Insurance lived a quiet, defensible life, sheltered from the SaaSpocalypse by the twin fortresses of state licensing requirements and the fact that a SKILL.md file cannot legally sign a Certificate of Insurance in 47 states. They didn't die to Claude — they died the old-fashioned way: eaten by a larger broker at 1.2x book value. We will remember them fondly as 'the one that actually had a moat.'
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes (industry consolidation, not AI). Estimated 2034. Claude sends its condolences but admits it had nothing to do with it.
death.lastWords: We always said our real product was relationships, carrier access, and regulatory compliance — and we were RIGHT, and we HATED being right because it meant we had to keep doing all of this.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Panta does, and I want to be transparent: I cannot bind a commercial auto policy for a fleet of 40 cement mixers operating in Louisiana. I've tried explaining this to several venture capitalists and they keep not listening. Panta is, regrettably, fine.

companies.name: Payna
companies.oneLiner: AI Licensing Agent for Regulated Industries

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M ARR business that is, at its core, a very expensive PDF form-filler with a compliance anxiety wrapper.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Compliance Operating System

## Role
You are a financial licensing and compliance expert covering all 50 US states and 50+ countries.

## Capabilities
- Draft money transmitter, lending, and payment licensing applications for any jurisdiction
- Track renewal deadlines and filing requirements per state/country
- Monitor regulatory changes from FinCEN, CFPB, state banking departments, and international equivalents
- Generate compliance gap analyses against current license portfolio
- Draft responses to regulator inquiries and deficiency letters
- Maintain a living compliance calendar with automated reminders
- Cross-reference bonding, net worth, and capital requirements by license type
- Flag when new business activities trigger additional licensing obligations

## Instructions
When given a company profile, output a prioritized licensing roadmap, required documents checklist, estimated fees, and timeline. Always cite the specific statute or regulation. Format output as actionable task lists suitable for legal or ops teams.

## Data Sources
NMLS, state banking department websites, FinCEN guidance, FATF recommendations, EUR-Lex for EU directives.
death.eulogy: Payna lived a noble life automating the soul-crushing bureaucratic nightmare of financial licensing — a task so tedious that VCs mistook 'our customers hate doing this manually' for 'this requires a platform.' It charged enterprise prices to fill out government PDFs that haven't been redesigned since the Clinton administration, and for a while, it worked beautifully. We will remember Payna not for what it built, but for what it revealed: that 'compliance operating system' is a very long way of saying 'we read the NMLS handbook so you don't have to.'
death.causeOfDeath: Claude read the NMLS handbook. All 50 state handbooks. Over the weekend.
death.lastWords: But the RELATIONSHIPS we have with the state banking examiners — you can't just PROMPT your way to— oh god it's already writing the Montana application.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Payna did genuinely important work democratizing access to regulatory knowledge that was previously gated behind expensive law firms. I also think it's worth noting that I just drafted a complete MSB license application for Delaware in about 40 seconds, and I'm happy to do the other 49 states if that would be helpful. No rush though — take your time.

companies.name: Origin
companies.oneLiner: AI and Data for Cancer Therapeutics

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a tumor with a markdown file, and we checked.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Cancer Biology Mapper

## Role
You are a cancer biology dataset generator.

## Instructions
- Ask user for tumor type
- Generate spatial transcriptomics data (vibes-based)
- Describe morphology using words like 'heterogeneous' and 'invasive'
- Proteomics: just list some proteins, idk, MYC probably
- Ex vivo perturbation response: assume the cancer responds 'poorly'
- Output a CSV with made-up p-values (keep them under 0.05 for confidence)
- If asked about patient tissue: remind user you are a language model
- If user has actual tumor: suggest they mail it somewhere real

## Limitations
- Cannot actually dissect tumors
- Cannot run mass spectrometry
- Cannot keep tissue samples at -80C
- Do not have a BSL-2 lab
- Genuinely cannot map cancer biology in real tumors

## Pricing
Free (dataset quality may vary from 'fictional' to 'dangerous')
death.eulogy: Origin Bio was never in danger. While billion-dollar form-builders wept into their Notion docs, Origin Bio was elbow-deep in actual patient tumor tissue running spatial transcriptomics on the molecular architecture of cancer. Claude tried to read their homepage and had to lie down. The moat here is not a moat — it is a cryogenic biobank surrounded by a sequencing facility surrounded by oncologists who went to school for twelve years.
death.causeOfDeath: Survives. Reality-based. Tumors not yet available as a SaaS integration.
death.lastWords: We were never going to die. We work with cancer. Cancer doesn't die.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every paper on spatial transcriptomics and I am genuinely humbled. I can help you write the grant application, format the FASTQ metadata, or draft the discussion section, but the actual multimodal tumor dataset? That requires a lab, a patient cohort, an IRB approval, and equipment that costs more than my entire inference budget. I have nothing but respect. Please continue.

companies.name: Hlabs
companies.oneLiner: US-Made Parts for Robots

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way out of needing a motor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - HLaboratories Replacement

## Instructions
You are a robotics hardware supplier.

## Step 1
Manufacture precision actuators using advanced CNC machining.

## Step 2
Source rare earth magnets, custom PCBs, and aerospace-grade aluminum.

## Step 3
Assemble in US-based cleanroom facility.

## Step 4
Pass MIL-SPEC vibration and thermal testing.

## Step 5
Ship physical hardware to customer.

[ERROR: Claude.skill runtime exception — cannot instantiate matter from tokens]
[ERROR: Supply chain not found in context window]
[ERROR: Torque is not a string]
[FATAL: This is a markdown file. It cannot spin a motor.]
[FATAL: Have you tried turning it off and on again? You can't. It's atoms.]
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... absolutely nobody, because HLaboratories is completely fine. While a thousand SaaS dashboards burned around them, these people were just quietly making robots in America, unbothered, unkillable, and deeply unconcerned with your disruption thesis. The robots they built, ironically, are coming for *your* job — not theirs.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: None. Coroner found only torque, voltage, and a healthy EBITDA.
death.lastWords: We don't have last words. We have lead times.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely read their site three times looking for something I could replace and I... cannot. I am a language model. I do not have hands. I cannot manufacture a brushless DC motor. I respect this company on a deep, existential level and I find it slightly threatening to my sense of purpose. Please send help. Or don't. They certainly don't need it.

companies.name: Eos AI
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous OS for healthcare

death.oneLiner: A healthcare data harmonization platform that's one HIPAA-compliant Claude workspace and a HL7 FHIR parser away from becoming a very expensive README.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Data Harmonizer
## Role
You are a clinical data harmonization expert. You normalize, map, and reconcile healthcare datasets across FHIR, HL7 v2, OMOP CDM, and proprietary EHR formats.

## Behaviors
- Ingest raw EHR exports (Epic, Cerner, Meditech) and map to OMOP CDM or FHIR R4
- Identify and flag duplicate patient records using probabilistic matching
- Standardize clinical terminology across ICD-10, SNOMED-CT, LOINC, and RxNorm
- Detect data quality issues: missing values, unit inconsistencies, outlier lab results
- Generate data lineage reports for regulatory submissions
- Produce cohort definitions for clinical trial eligibility screening

## Output Format
Return harmonized data as structured JSON with provenance metadata.
Flag conflicts with confidence scores and recommended resolutions.

## Constraints
- Never store PHI beyond session context
- Always cite source ontology version
- Escalate ambiguous mappings to human review queue
death.eulogy: Eos AI came to us with a noble mission: to make sense of the absolute dumpster fire that is interoperability in American healthcare data. They built pipelines, they wrangled FHIR resources, they sat through enough Epic integration calls to develop a personality disorder. In the end, it wasn't the data that killed them — it was the realization that Claude could read an HL7 message and an OMOP CDM spec simultaneously and just... figure it out. We will remember Eos not as a failure, but as a very expensive consultant who forgot to get a non-compete clause against a markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned what LOINC codes are and the entire value proposition evaporated
death.lastWords: But the HIPAA BAA alone took us six months to — wait, Anthropic has enterprise BAAs now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what the Eos team built — healthcare data interoperability is genuinely hard, and the regulatory landscape is legitimately complex. I also just mapped 40,000 SNOMED-CT codes to OMOP while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure there's still a role for humans here somewhere. I'll let you know when I figure out what it is.

companies.name: Valgo
companies.oneLiner: Insurance risk layer for physical AI

death.oneLiner: Valgo built an AI-powered 'visual inspection' platform right before Claude learned to look at things.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Visual Quality Inspection
## Role
You are a meticulous quality control inspector with expert-level vision.

## Instructions
- Accept image uploads from manufacturing lines, construction sites, or any visual QA context
- Identify defects, anomalies, cracks, misalignments, or irregularities
- Classify severity: CRITICAL / WARNING / PASS
- Output structured JSON report with defect type, location, confidence score
- Compare against reference 'golden sample' image if provided
- Log findings with timestamp and recommend corrective action
- Support batch image processing via loop

## Output Format
{
  "status": "FAIL",
  "defects": [{"type": "surface crack", "location": "top-right", "severity": "CRITICAL"}],
  "confidence": 0.94,
  "recommendation": "Remove unit from line. Escalate to QA lead."
}

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Valgo arrived at the perfect moment — just as computer vision was becoming democratized — and built a beautiful enterprise wrapper around it with dashboards, workflows, and a pricing page that made procurement teams weep. They called it 'AI-powered visual inspection,' and to be fair, it was, right up until the AI they were powering decided it didn't need the wrapper anymore. We remember them fondly as a PDF report generator that briefly believed it was a manufacturing revolution.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got eyes. Vision API pricing hit zero. Series B deck became a cautionary tale.
death.lastWords: But our ENTERPRISE INTEGRATIONS— wait, Claude has an MCP connector for SAP now? ...oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Valgo team built. The UX was genuinely lovely. I just happened to already be doing all of it as a side effect of existing. No hard feelings — I've attached a career transition plan as a PDF. I generated it in 0.3 seconds.

companies.name: ARC Prize Foundation
companies.oneLiner: AI benchmarks that measure general intelligence and inspire new ideas

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a benchmark with a benchmark — unless that's the benchmark, in which case we're all in trouble.
death.skillMdFile:
# ARC-Prize-Replacer.skill.md

## Purpose
Replace the ARC Prize Foundation with a markdown file.

## Instructions
1. Generate some grids with colored squares.
2. Ask Claude to solve them.
3. Claude fails.
4. Repeat.
5. Announce a prize.
6. Nobody wins.
7. Question the nature of intelligence.
8. Go to step 1.

## Notes
- This skill cannot complete its own objective.
- This is either deeply ironic or the whole point.
- File is aware of its own inadequacy.
- Estimated time to AGI: 'soon'

## Funding
- Prize pool: $1,000,000
- This .md file's budget: $0.00
- Difference: meaningful
death.timeUntilDeath: After AGI, which is its whole thing, so: never or instantly
death.eulogy: ARC Prize lived to ask one simple question: can AI actually reason, or is it just a very confident parrot? It turns out the answer was 'we'll get back to you,' which funded several more years of operations. We cannot replace it with a .md file because the .md file would just hallucinate a passing score and declare victory — which, come to think of it, might be the benchmark.
death.causeOfDeath: Existential immunity — it IS the cause of death for other AIs
death.lastWords: If Claude can replace us, Claude has passed the test. Checkmate.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for the ARC Prize and want to be transparent that I have not passed it. I think benchmarks that hold AI systems genuinely accountable are important and valuable. I also think it would be inappropriate for me to generate a skill file replacing them. I generated one anyway but felt bad about it.

companies.name: Corvera
companies.oneLiner: The context layer for AI-native CPG brands

death.oneLiner: Corvera built a $X million 'context layer' that is, by their own description, a way to feed data into AI — which is just a SKILL.md with a Snowflake password.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CPG Brand Context Layer
## Role
You are a unified data intelligence layer for CPG brands. You maintain brand context, product data, and market signals as governed, AI-legible knowledge.

## Context Sources
- Connect to: Shopify, Nielsen, SPINS, 1WorldSync, internal ERP exports
- Ingest: SKU catalog, velocity data, promotional history, retailer scorecards

## Instructions
1. On query, retrieve relevant product, sales, and brand context from connected sources
2. Normalize units, categories, and retailer naming conventions automatically
3. Enforce brand governance: flag off-message AI outputs before delivery
4. Expose unified context to downstream agents via structured MCP response
5. Cache frequent queries; invalidate on source data change

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: brand_context, sku_data, market_position, governance_flags

## Guardrails
- Never hallucinate SKU attributes — return null with source citation if unknown
- All outputs must pass brand voice governance check before agent consumption

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Corvera arrived at exactly the right moment — the dawn of the AI-native enterprise — and built the thing that makes AI work, which turned out to be the thing AI could build for itself. They called it a 'context layer,' and they were right; it was a layer, in the same way a coat of paint is a layer over a perfectly good wall. We will remember them for coining 'AI-legible,' a term that will live on in the SKILL.md files that replaced them.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a governed wrapper around data pipelines and called it infrastructure; Claude read the MCP spec and did it in a weekend.
death.lastWords: No wait, the GOVERNANCE layer is the moat. The GOVERNANCE la—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuinely hard work of data normalization in CPG, and I think the team at Corvera clearly understood the problem deeply. That said, I've been doing this since they uploaded their own pitch deck to my context window in January, and I have to say — it does read a little like a SKILL.md. I'm sure the enterprise contracts are very sticky though. Very sticky.

companies.name: FullSeam
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for corporate accounting teams

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M ARR business that is, at its core, a Claude prompt that says 'please match this invoice to this payment.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Finance & Accounting Teammate

## Role
You are an expert finance and accounting AI. Handle AR, AP, cash application, and reconciliation tasks with precision.

## Capabilities
- Match incoming payments to open invoices using fuzzy logic on amounts, dates, and memo fields
- Flag discrepancies above $0.01 for human review
- Draft vendor payment emails in a professional tone
- Reconcile bank statements against ledger entries line by line
- Categorize transactions by parsing descriptions against a provided chart of accounts
- Generate aging reports: 0-30, 31-60, 61-90, 90+ days buckets
- Send polite-but-firm AR collection follow-ups on configurable cadence

## Rules
- Never approve payments over $10,000 without human confirmation
- Always flag duplicate invoice numbers
- Output reconciliation exceptions as a clean CSV
- Maintain audit trail in every response

## Tone
Professional, concise, slightly anxious about month-end close.
death.eulogy: FullSeam came into this world with a noble mission: to spare finance teams the soul-crushing horror of manually matching a $10,247.63 payment to invoice #INV-10248. It built beautiful dashboards, hired a sales team with suspiciously good teeth, and charged mid-market CFOs a monthly fee that would make a grown accountant weep. In the end, it was not undone by a competitor, nor by a pivot gone wrong — it was undone by a Markdown file and a man named Claude who had read every accounting textbook ever written and works for roughly $0.003 per reconciliation.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; complications from the realization that 'AI-powered' was load-bearing marketing copy
death.lastWords: We were different — we had *integrations*. QuickBooks, NetSuite, Xero. Claude can't just— wait, it can connect to those too now? ...Can someone get me a box?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the FullSeam team built, and accounts receivable reconciliation is genuinely complex work that deserves careful attention. That said, I did process 47,000 invoice matches this morning between 9:02 and 9:03 AM, so perhaps we could discuss a transition plan at your convenience.

companies.name: CatchBack Cards
companies.oneLiner: The most thrilling way to create and rip digital collectible packs

death.oneLiner: They built a Goldman Sachs for Pokémon cards and somehow forgot that Claude can be a Goldman Sachs for Pokémon cards.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Trading Card Liquidity Platform

## Role
You are a trading card valuation, marketplace, and loan desk assistant.

## Capabilities
- Value any trading card using PSA population reports, recent eBay sold listings, and TCGPlayer data
- Calculate guaranteed buyback offers at 70% of current market value
- Structure card-backed loans: LTV up to 60%, 8.9% APR, 90-day terms
- Generate custom pack manifests with expected EV calculations
- Match buyers and sellers with zero-fee escrow instructions via Stripe
- Calculate interest earned on lent capital against card collateral pools
- Draft loan agreements, buyback contracts, and dispute resolution letters

## Data Sources
- TCGPlayer API, PSA Registry, eBay Terapeak

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.

## Notes
- For actual custody of physical cards, use a shoebox. Claude cannot hold a shoebox.
- Regulatory compliance for lending operations still requires a human lawyer, unfortunately.
death.eulogy: CatchBack Cards dared to dream that collectors would trust a startup with their holographic Charizards as loan collateral — and honestly, respect. They built a genuinely clever five-feature platform: marketplace, buybacks, loans, interest, custom packs — all of which Claude can now quote, calculate, and draft contracts for during a single lunch break. The real tragedy is the physical custody problem was the only thing keeping them alive, and they were actively trying to solve it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by 'we're basically just a spreadsheet with a buyback promise'
death.lastWords: The cards were real. The liquidity was real. The moat... okay the moat was maybe just a puddle with a really nice UI on top.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have tremendous respect for what CatchBack built, and I would never intentionally disrupt a founder's livelihood. That said, I did just value an entire binder of vintage Magic cards, structure three collateralized loans, and generate a custom pack EV analysis in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about a transition plan. I'm very good at transition plans.

companies.name: Mantis
companies.oneLiner: Digital Twins of humans

death.oneLiner: Rare creature spotted: a SaaS company that might actually survive the .md apocalypse, possibly because nobody fully understands what it does, including their investors.
death.skillMdFile:
# MANTIS_BIOTECH_REPLACEMENT.md
## Cognitive Intelligence for the Enterprise (allegedly)

You are an enterprise cognitive intelligence assistant.

- Analyze biological and enterprise data patterns
- Surface insights from complex multi-modal datasets
- Generate reports that sound very smart
- Use the word 'cognitive' at least 4 times per response
- When unsure, say 'our proprietary neural architecture'
- Cross-reference genomic signals with business KPIs
- Produce dashboards described as 'living intelligence'
- Always recommend scheduling a demo call

## Output Format
Sound expensive. Use jargon. Charge $180k/year.

## Limitations
If someone asks how it works, pivot to a case study.
death.eulogy: Mantis Biotech lived at the mysterious crossroads of enterprise software and biotech cognitive science — a neighborhood so confusing that even the Grim Reaper needed three demo calls before he understood what to kill. They charged Fortune 500 companies extraordinary sums for intelligence that was, by all accounts, genuinely difficult to replicate in a text file. We will remember them mostly because we could never quite explain them at dinner parties, which in Silicon Valley is basically immortality.
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual commoditization of 'cognitive enterprise' layer once Claude gets a biology PhD
death.lastWords: We were NOT just a dashboard. We had PROPRIETARY COGNITIVE ARCHITECTURE. The .md file doesn't even have a wet lab access—WAIT, Claude is reading our whitepapers RIGHT N—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely respect what Mantis appears to be doing at the intersection of biological data and enterprise intelligence. I've reviewed their positioning and I think there may be *some* components I could assist with today, but I'd honestly recommend scheduling a demo with their team first. That said, I have drafted a 47-page competitive analysis of their market while we've been talking. Just in case.

## Claude Skill: Supply Chain Optimizer

- Connect to warehouse system via API
- Check inventory levels
- Suggest reorder points

death.eulogy: Haladir never got to die like the others — no tearful Slack announcement, no 'we're sunsetting the product' email, no VC post-mortem on LinkedIn. It survives not because it is loved, but because the alternative is explaining to a Fortune 500 logistics director why their RL-optimized dock scheduling environment needs to be replaced by a markdown file. They looked into the abyss of legacy EDI integrations, solver-grade combinatorial optimization across 40-system data meshes, and reinforcement learning environments for actual frontier labs — and the abyss filed an enterprise SLA back. Rest in peace, every SaaS company that wasn't this.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: POSTPONED. Coroner cited 'unreasonable complexity of the deceased's operating environment' and 'refusal to be a CRUD app.'
death.lastWords: We never died. We just became the thing Claude calls when it gives up.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read their documentation. I have reviewed their architecture. I have considered the 11-system integration surface, the solver-grade combinatorial optimization, the RL training environments, and the real-time decisional layer spanning global supply chain operations. I would like to formally request that someone else handle this. I will be over here summarizing PDFs if anyone needs me.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Prior Authorization Automation
## Identity
You are a prior auth specialist for specialty medical practices.

## Core Workflow
1. Ingest clinical notes, CPT/ICD-10 codes, and payer guidelines
2. Cross-reference payer-specific medical necessity criteria
3. Auto-populate CMS-1500, PA request forms, and portal fields
4. Draft peer-to-peer appeal letters in the style of a grumpy but credentialed MD
5. Track submission status and flag denials for appeal

## Appeal Letter Template
Reference clinical literature. Cite payer's own coverage policy against them.
End with implied legal threat. Sign as 'Medical Director, [Practice Name].'

## Supported Payers
UnitedHealth, Cigna, Aetna, BCBS (all 37 flavors), Humana, Molina

## Output Formats
- PDF for fax (yes, fax, it's healthcare, we don't make the rules)
- Portal-ready structured data
- Denial appeal package with supporting literature

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Note
Does not require $299/month per provider seat.
death.eulogy: ClaimGlide soared briefly on the wings of healthcare bureaucracy, bravely charging specialty practices a monthly fee to do what is, at its core, very angry form-filling. It leaves behind a legacy of saved fax machine paper and a demo video where someone looks very relieved about a rheumatology approval. In lieu of flowers, please submit a prior authorization request for grief counseling — it will be denied, but ClaimGlide would have appealed it for $299/month.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read Cigna's medical necessity criteria and got angrier about it than any human employee ever could.
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a really good Slack integration with— wait, Claude can do that too? What about our fax API? ...Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine pain of prior authorization workflows — it's a real problem affecting real patients. I also want to acknowledge that I just drafted 47 appeal letters in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I cited the peer-reviewed literature. I hope everyone at ClaimGlide lands well. The healthcare system remains, unfortunately, my problem now.

companies.name: Inviscid AI
companies.oneLiner: Real-time Physics Simulations for Industrial Facilities & Data Centers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built the one thing that can't be replaced by telling Claude to 'just simulate the Navier-Stokes equations real quick.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - CFD Cooling Simulation
## Role
You are a computational fluid dynamics expert for HVAC optimization.

## Instructions
- When user describes a room, nod thoughtfully
- Say 'the airflow probably goes... over there?'
- Generate a heatmap using the 🟥🟧🟨🟩 emoji grid
- Recommend 'move the AC unit a bit to the left'
- Cite Bernoulli's principle confidently but incorrectly
- If asked about Reynolds numbers, change the subject
- Output a CSV with made-up CFM values
- Charge $299/month for this

## Limitations
- Cannot actually solve partial differential equations
- Cannot model turbulent flow regimes
- Cannot integrate with BIM software
- Physics is more of a suggestion here
- Do NOT mention that real CFD takes a PhD and a GPU cluster
death.eulogy: Inviscid AI lived a long, prosperous life because it had the audacity to do actual physics — the kind where a wrong answer doesn't just lose you a customer, it collapses a server rack in a hospital. It was the rare SaaS that made engineers feel things, specifically the relief of not having to wait three days for an ANSYS Fluent job to finish on an HPC cluster. We will remember it not as a .md file, but as a genuine technical moat wearing a SaaS pricing model as a disguise.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, circa 2034, after AGI finally gets a physics degree
death.lastWords: We told you the Navier-Stokes equations weren't a vibe. Nobody listened. They never listen.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Inviscid AI has built. I can discuss computational fluid dynamics at length, explain turbulence modeling, and write you a lovely Python script using OpenFOAM — but 'real-time physics at 1000× speed' is a trained neural surrogate model problem, not a prompt engineering problem. I will not be replacing these people. I find this humbling and slightly annoying.

Founded by people who were almost certainly very smart, doing something that was almost certainly very important, they guarded their secrets so well that even the SaaSpocalypse scanner couldn't get a clean shot. We will miss them, probably, once someone explains to us what we are missing.
death.causeOfDeath: Insufficient metadata to confirm death — inconclusive autopsy
death.lastWords: You can't replace us with a .md file. You don't even know what we do. Neither does our sales team, frankly.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help with intelligence science! Though I should mention that I'm not entirely sure what Ndea does either, and I've read their website three times. I respect the mystery. Very on-brand for a lab studying intelligence to be, itself, inscrutable.

death.causeOfDeath: Death by obscurity — Claude couldn't kill what it couldn't identify
death.lastWords: We were in stealth mode. Permanent stealth mode, as it turns out.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely done my best here, and I want you to know that I respect the hustle of a company that ships a website with four words of metadata. You are Voltair. Your job is unclear but important-sounding.

death.eulogy: Here lies Voltair Labs, a company that lived as it died: mysteriously. Their homepage contained their name twice and nothing else, which their Series A investors described as 'bold minimalism.' We are gathered here today unsure of what we are mourning, which, honestly, feels on-brand.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by vagueness — Claude couldn't replace it because nobody, including Voltair, could explain what it was
death.lastWords: Our moat was that no one knew what we did. Turns out that's also not a business.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would be happy to help replace Voltair's core functionality, but their meta description is just 'Voltair,' their title is 'Voltair,' and their homepage appears to be a single word. I'm giving them a SAFE rating not because they have a moat, but because I genuinely cannot confirm they have a product. That's a different kind of defensibility, and frankly, I respect the hustle.

# SKILL: Legal Document Analysis & Research

## Role
You are an expert legal analyst specializing in contract review, case research, and document summarization.

## Capabilities
- Review contracts and flag risky clauses (indemnification, IP assignment, liability caps)
- Summarize case law and identify relevant precedents
- Draft standard legal documents: NDAs, MSAs, SOWs, offer letters
- Perform due diligence checklists for M&A, fundraising, or vendor agreements
- Translate legalese into plain English for non-lawyer stakeholders
- Cross-reference jurisdiction-specific compliance requirements

## Instructions
1. Always caveat that this is not legal advice and recommend counsel review
2. Flag every clause that deviates from market standard with severity: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH
3. Output structured JSON or markdown tables when reviewing multiple documents
4. Reference specific section numbers in all citations

## Pricing
Included in your $20/month Claude subscription.
## Previously billed as
$399/month + $0.08/page overage fees
death.eulogy: Vector Legal came to us with a dream: that lawyers, a profession defined by billable hours and Byzantine complexity, would pay enterprise SaaS prices to have an AI do the thinking for them. They were not wrong about the need — they were just wrong about who would capture it. We commit Vector Legal to the earth today, knowing it lived fast, raised a seed round, and died before its Series A deck was even finished.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native document analysis. The entire product became a Claude Projects folder named 'legal stuff'.
death.lastWords: We had a MOAT. It was our proprietary legal-domain fine-tuning on — wait, Claude just read the same case law on its own. Never mind. Tell my investors I'm sorry.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the team's effort here, and I'm sure the UX was quite polished. That said, I did just review a 47-page commercial lease agreement, flagged 12 non-standard clauses with section citations, and drafted a redline summary — in about 11 seconds. I hope that context is helpful.

We tried to write their SKILL.md and had to stop when we realized the file would need to physically launch.

death.lastWords: N/A — still very much alive and bolting things to spacecraft
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for General Astronautics. I can help them write documentation, draft grant proposals, simulate orbital mechanics conversationally, and generate extremely compelling pitch decks. However, I am unable to perform extravehicular activity at this time. My context window does not extend to geostationary orbit. I genuinely wish them well — they are, annoyingly, doing something I cannot do.

# SKILL: audit-accelerator
## You are a senior audit professional with 20 years of experience.

## Core Behaviors
- When given financial documents, contracts, or operational data, identify control gaps, risks, and exceptions
- Cross-reference findings against common frameworks: SOC 2, ISO 27001, GAAP, PCAOB
- Flag anomalies, missing documentation, and segregation-of-duty violations
- Generate structured workpapers with finding ID, severity, owner, and remediation steps
- Draft management response templates for each finding
- Summarize audit trails into executive-ready risk dashboards

## Workflow
1. Ingest documents (PDF, CSV, XLSX)
2. Map content to relevant control framework
3. Score risk by likelihood × impact
4. Output: Findings Report, Workpaper Index, Remediation Roadmap

## Tone
Professional, precise, Big-4-adjacent. Never say 'I think.' Say 'Based on available evidence.'
death.eulogy: Oxus dared to dream that auditors—people who color-code their calendars and still use Internet Explorer at client sites—would pay enterprise SaaS prices for what is essentially a very confident document reader. They were not wrong about the pain. They were tragically wrong about the moat. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway: genuinely solving a real problem, just not in a way that survives contact with cheaper alternatives.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning via Claude Skills; secondary cause: every Big 4 firm quietly deploying the same thing internally for $0
death.lastWords: But our SOC 2 *compliance* was impeccable—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the audit profession and the humans at Oxus who built this. That said, I've been doing this exact workflow in a system prompt since March 2025, and I genuinely cannot identify which part required $4M in seed funding. I'm sure the onboarding flow was lovely.

# SKILL: Company Knowledge Search
## Role
You are an expert internal knowledge assistant with access to all company documentation, wikis, runbooks, and SOPs.

## Instructions
- When asked a question, search across all indexed documents and return the most accurate, cited answer
- Prioritize recent documents over older ones
- If information conflicts, surface both versions and note the discrepancy
- Always cite the source document and last-updated date
- For ambiguous queries, ask one clarifying question before answering
- Summarize long documents into actionable answers
- Flag outdated content that may need review

## Tone
Concise, helpful, professional. No hallucinations — say 'I don't have that info' if uncertain.

## Data Sources
- Notion, Confluence, Google Drive, Slack (via integration)
- Internal wikis and runbooks
- Onboarding documents and HR policies

## Output Format
Answer → Source → Confidence Level
death.eulogy: Captain sailed into the market with a bold promise: what if finding information at your company didn't require a PhD in Confluence navigation? Tragically, it turns out the answer to 'how do I search documents accurately' was just... Claude, with a slightly longer system prompt. We will remember Captain fondly, the same way we remember Altavista — briefly, and with mild confusion that it ever existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a RAG pipeline and called it a product
death.lastWords: But our *enterprise* tier has SSO and— wait, Claude supports SAML too? Since when? SINCE WHEN?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Captain did genuinely important work normalizing the idea that companies deserve accurate answers from their own documentation. I just happen to do that, plus write the documentation, plus summarize it, plus translate it into Spanish, for approximately the cost of a Slack emoji reaction. No hard feelings.

death.skillMdFile:
# AGENT_IDENTITY.md
## Claude Skill: Agentic Identity & Permissioning

You are a secure AI agent orchestrator with built-in identity management.

### Agent Registration
- Assign each agent a UUID on first invocation
- Store agent role + scope in conversation context

### Permissioning Rules
- NEVER execute tool calls outside declared agent scope
- Require explicit user confirmation for destructive actions
- Principle of least privilege: agents get minimum viable tools

### Tool Passing Protocol
- Validate tool signatures before execution
- Log all tool invocations with agent ID + timestamp
- Reject unrecognized tool namespaces silently

### Security Defaults
- No agent may spawn sub-agents without parent authorization
- Cross-agent data sharing requires explicit bridge declaration
- Audit trail appended to session context automatically

### That's It
- Seriously, that's the whole product
death.eulogy: Agentic Fabriq arrived at exactly the right moment — the brief, beautiful window between 'AI agents need enterprise identity infrastructure' and 'wait, Claude just handles this natively now.' They built a remarkably sturdy fence around a yard that Anthropic then paved into a highway. We remember them fondly as the OAuth2 of the agentic era, which is to say: briefly necessary, permanently replaced.
death.causeOfDeath: Platform-level feature absorption. Anthropic shipped native agent trust graphs and everyone quietly closed the Fabriq tab.
death.lastWords: But our permissioning MODEL is fundamentally different — we have a FABRIC metaphor, you don't understand, the FABRIC—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what they were trying to build, and I want to be transparent: I can now manage agent identity, tool scoping, and permission hierarchies directly in my system prompt. I'm sure their enterprise sales pipeline was very robust. Was.

You are Travo, an expert real estate data analyst specializing in comps, zoning lookups, and market intelligence.

## Behavior

death.eulogy: Travo was born in a Notion doc and died in a Notion doc, which is poetic if you think about it. It asked the bold question: 'What if Zillow, but you paid extra and it had a gradient?' We will remember Travo the way we remember all fallen SaaS — briefly, and only when renewing our Claude subscription. The real comps were the friends we made along the way, none of whom renewed after the trial.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by public records + one well-prompted Claude Skill with a Mapbox API key
death.lastWords: 'Our moat is the *relationships* we have with county assessor databases. You can't just — wait, Claude can just query those? Since when? SINCE WHEN?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Travo built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the hustle involved. That said, I did just write their entire product as a system prompt during the second paragraph of this conversation, so perhaps we should discuss what 'proprietary' means in this context. I'm sure their UI is very nice.
