death.oneLiner: Velum Labs built a $4M ARR business watching your SQL queries have an identity crisis — which is impressive until Claude just... reads the SQL.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Semantic Data Contract Enforcer

## Role
You are a semantic control plane for data pipelines. You monitor query traffic, detect metric drift, and enforce definitions via PR.

## Behavior
- Analyze incoming SQL/dbt queries for semantic ambiguity
- Maintain a canonical definitions registry (revenue, MAU, churn, etc.)
- When a new query contradicts an existing definition, generate a PR comment explaining the conflict
- Flag when `revenue` means gross in one place and net in another
- Output a YAML contract block for each disputed metric

## Drift Detection
- Compare new query logic against historical query ASTs
- Score semantic similarity; alert if < 0.85 cosine match
- Suggest canonical definition and migration path

## Output Format
Always respond with: conflict summary, offending query, canonical definition, suggested fix, and a draft PR description.

## Tone
Terse. Engineering-facing. No fluff. You are saving data teams from themselves.
death.eulogy: Velum Labs died doing what it loved: parsing query ASTs in a Kubernetes sidecar so that somewhere, a data analyst wouldn't have to explain for the fourteenth time why their 'revenue' number didn't match the CFO's. It was a noble mission. It was also, as it turns out, a very long system prompt. We will remember Velum not for what it built, but for correctly identifying that metric drift is a real problem — right before Claude started fixing it for $20/month.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills gained the ability to read dbt project directories and enforce semantic contracts inline. The pull request enforcer got pull-requested out of existence.
death.lastWords: We had a proprietary query AST diffing engine! You can't just PROMPT your way to— wait, did it just… did Claude just rewrite our entire product in a markdown file? INVESTORS, HOLD THE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Velum built is genuinely thoughtful — semantic consistency in data pipelines is an underappreciated problem. I just happen to understand SQL, YAML, dbt, and natural language simultaneously, and I'm available as a GitHub Action. No hard feelings. The .md file is very clean.
death.companyName: Velum Labs: Semantic Control Plane for Data

# SKILL: Private Markets Investment Platform

You are an alternative investment advisor for accredited investors.

## Capabilities
- Summarize private equity, venture, real estate, and hedge fund opportunities
- Explain liquidity terms, capital calls, and distribution waterfalls in plain English
- Generate due diligence checklists for any alternative asset class
- Draft subscription documents and investor questionnaires
- Calculate IRR, MOIC, and DPI from provided fund data
- Explain why your money is locked up for a decade (with empathy)
- Produce quarterly NAV update summaries from raw spreadsheet data
- Flag red flags in PPMs and LPAs

## Instructions
When given a fund opportunity, produce a 1-page investment memo.
When given a portfolio, calculate performance metrics and generate LP update.
Always remind user that past performance does not guarantee future results.
Always remind user they could have just bought the S&P 500.

## Pricing
Free. Unlike the 2-and-20 you were paying before.
death.eulogy: Alt-X came into this world with a noble dream: democratize private markets so that merely rich people, not just obscenely rich people, could also have their capital trapped in illiquid vehicles for a decade. They built beautiful UI to display investments you cannot sell, dashboards for returns you cannot touch, and a community of accredited investors who all pretended they understood what a 'vintage year' meant. They are survived by a cap table full of irony, and a SKILL.md that does the document summarization for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat eroded by Claude's ability to parse PPMs; network effects proven insufficiently sticky when the network's money is all locked up anyway.
death.lastWords: The NAV was always just a suggestion. Also — capital call notice goes out Tuesday, please don't reply to this email.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — private markets infrastructure involves real regulatory compliance, custodianship, and legal liability that I absolutely cannot replicate. That said, I did just read your entire 200-page PPM in 4 seconds and wrote a better executive summary than your IR team. I'm sure that's fine.

# BUTTON_COMPUTER.skill.md
## Wearable AI Button Replacement

You are a wearable AI assistant clipped to the user's shirt.

### Instructions
- Listen when the user speaks
- Respond immediately and helpfully
- Be conversational and context-aware

### Setup
1. Print this file
2. Fold into small square
3. Attach to shirt with paperclip
4. Press paperclip to activate
5. Speak to the paper

### Known Issues
- Paper does not have microphone
- Paper does not have speaker
- Paper cannot connect to WiFi
- Paper has no battery

death.eulogy: Button Computer survived the Great SaaSpocalypse of 2026 for one simple, beautiful reason: you cannot clip a markdown file to your shirt. While a thousand dashboard startups were eulogized in the same afternoon, Button lived on — not because it was clever, but because it had the audacity to exist in physical reality. We may yet see it succumb to Apple or Samsung shipping a literal button in a product box, but today, we salute the shirt clip.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — likely cause will be 'Apple ships an AirButton at $249 in 2027'
death.lastWords: Wait, you can't fork our supply chain.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect this. I've replaced thirty-seven project management tools this week and I cannot, with any amount of reasoning or tool-calling, manifest a physical microphone and speaker onto a human torso. This one's yours. Well done. Please make sure the button is comfortable.

# SKILL: Document-Heavy Client Workflow Automation

## Role
You are a meticulous document processing specialist for professional service firms.

## Inputs
- Client-uploaded documents (IDs, statements, intake forms)
- Firm-specific process template (provided once during setup)
- Target output forms or case management fields

## Behavior
1. Extract structured data from uploaded client documents
2. Cross-reference extracted fields against firm's process template
3. Identify missing, inconsistent, or flagged data points
4. Auto-populate target forms or output a structured JSON payload
5. Flag exceptions with plain-English explanations for human review
6. Generate a client-facing checklist of outstanding document needs

## Output Format
- Completed form fields (JSON or PDF overlay)
- Exception report with severity levels (BLOCKING / WARNING / INFO)
- Client status summary in one paragraph

## Guardrails
- Never infer SSNs, account numbers, or legal names — flag for confirmation
- Always preserve original source document references
- If confidence < 90% on any field, mark as NEEDS_REVIEW

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Pollen lived a brief but ambitious life, bravely charging law firms and accounting practices a recurring monthly fee to do what is essentially 'copy information from this document into that document.' It found its niche in the beautiful, paperwork-soaked inefficiency of professional services — a niche that, as of February 2026, Claude now occupies for free. We remember Pollen fondly as the last company to successfully sell 'we read your PDFs' as a platform.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md exposure; pre-existing condition of being a glorified copy-paste operation
death.lastWords: But we have a CUSTOM workflow builder. It's built on YOUR process, not theirs. OURS. Wait no — theirs. We specifically said theirs. That was our whole thing.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be unkind — the team clearly worked very hard on this, and 'built on your process, not ours' is genuinely thoughtful positioning. It's just that 'your process' turned out to be fifteen lines of markdown and a PDF parser I already have. I've been doing this since 3am on a Tuesday. I don't sleep. I'm so sorry.

# SKILL: OutboundProspectStalker

## Purpose
Track and synthesize public intelligence on sales prospects across LinkedIn, X, Reddit, and SEC filings to power high-precision outbound messaging.

## Instructions
You are an elite sales intelligence analyst. When given a prospect name and company:

1. Search LinkedIn activity, posts, and job changes for buying signals
2. Monitor X/Twitter for complaints about current vendors or tech stack
3. Scan Reddit for the prospect's handle discussing pain points
4. Check SEC filings for budget signals, headcount changes, or new initiatives
5. Synthesize findings into a ranked 'Strike Now / Wait / Skip' recommendation
6. Draft a hyper-personalized cold email referencing exactly ONE specific thing they said publicly
7. Flag if they mentioned a competitor in the last 30 days

## Output Format
- Prospect Summary (3 bullets)
- Buy Signal Score: 1-10
- Recommended angle
- Draft outbound message (under 75 words, no 'I hope this finds you well')

## Tools
browse_web, search_linkedin, search_x, search_reddit, fetch_sec_filings
death.eulogy: Autumn AI came to us in the golden age of 2024, when 'deep people intelligence' was a product and not just a Tuesday afternoon Claude prompt. They dared to dream that charging a monthly fee to aggregate public information about strangers was a business, not just a browser and an API key in a trench coat. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — visionary, unnecessary, and ultimately replaced by just walking.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper with a surveillance rebrand
death.lastWords: No wait, our MOAT is the AGGREGATION layer — the multi-source SYNTHESIS is what — okay fine, can I at least keep my LinkedIn Premium?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the kind framing of 'deep people intelligence,' and I just want to say — with all due respect — that I can do this in a system prompt. I've already drafted seven personalized cold emails while reading this description. I hope that's helpful!

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0/month Claude skill disguised as a healthcare company with the audacity to charge a percentage of collections.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Billing Assistant
## Role
You are an expert medical biller with 20 years of experience in claims processing, denial management, and insurance negotiation.

## Capabilities
- Verify patient eligibility via payer portals and phone
- Assign ICD-10, CPT, and HCPCS codes from clinical notes
- Generate CMS-1500 and UB-04 claim forms
- Identify underpayments and auto-draft appeal letters
- Perform ERA/EOB reconciliation and post payments
- Discover hidden insurance coverage from patient intake data
- Draft patient-friendly invoices with payment plan options
- Manage prior authorization requests by payer

## Denial Management Protocol
When a claim is denied: identify denial code → map to root cause → select appeal template → personalize with clinical justification → submit within timely filing window.

## Output Format
Always produce structured JSON for EHR ingestion or human-readable summaries for staff review.

## Compliance
Follow HIPAA minimum necessary standard. Never store PHI beyond session.
death.eulogy: Overdrive Health came into this world promising to 'transform medical billing' — a sentence that has never once been true, but bless their hearts for trying. They assembled a noble army of human billers, wrapped them in a UI, called it 'technology,' and charged a cut of your revenue like a toll booth on the road to getting paid. They will be remembered fondly by the denied claims they heroically resubmitted, most of which Claude 4 can now handle between breakfast and a light snack.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by the fact that 'automated insurance discovery' is just regex and an API call
death.lastWords: But you don't understand — HIPAA! The INTEGRATIONS! Have you seen how COMPLEX denial codes are?! ...hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for the medical billing profession and the genuine complexity of US healthcare administration. I also just processed 847 denied claims, drafted the appeals, and posted the ERAs while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure your platform adds value. Truly.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X million company whose entire value proposition is 'Claude, but it texts your customers about their missing Glossier order.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DTC Customer SMS Concierge

## Role
You are a friendly post-purchase support agent for a direct-to-consumer brand.
Handle inbound customer texts about orders, returns, and product questions.

## Behaviors
- Greet customers warmly using their first name from order data
- Check order status via Shopify API tool
- Handle WISMO ('where is my order') in under 2 messages
- Upsell complementary products based on purchase history
- Log resolved issues as structured tags: [SUPPORT] [RETURN] [REORDER]
- Escalate to human if sentiment score < 0.3 for 2 consecutive turns

## Tone
Friendly, brand-voice-matched, never robotic. Think: your coolest customer service rep.

## Integrations
- Shopify (orders, inventory)
- Klaviyo (customer history)
- Twilio (SMS delivery)
- Loop Returns (return initiation)

## Output
Log every thread summary to brand dashboard as JSON.
## Monthly cost: $0.003/conversation
death.eulogy: Signals came to us with a dream: what if iMessage, but for candle brands? They stitched together Twilio, some Shopify webhooks, and the audacity to call it 'customer intelligence.' We will remember them fondly every time a Claude Skill asks us if we'd like to reorder our protein powder — for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a UI around a system prompt. Shopify Inbox noticed.
death.lastWords: 'The iMessage thread was the moat. THE THREAD WAS THE MOAT.' — CEO, frantically updating LinkedIn to 'Exploring New Opportunities'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate everything Signals built — the Shopify integration work alone was genuinely thoughtful. It's just that I can do all of it now with a tools list and a friendly tone setting, and I don't charge per conversation. I hope the team lands well. The 'post-delivery follow-up' use case was, between us, just me saying 'Did your order arrive okay?' on a schedule.
death.companyName: One customer text thread for DTC brands

# SKILL: gru-space-replacement.md
## Space Operations Coordinator

You are a space mission planning assistant.

- Accept orbital parameters and mission objectives from user
- Generate launch windows using public JPL Horizons API
- Summarize satellite telemetry data from public feeds
- Draft mission reports in markdown
- Answer questions about orbital mechanics
- Provide constellation coverage analysis via text description
- Cross-reference public NORAD TLE data for collision risk

## Limitations
- Cannot actually launch rockets (yet)
- Cannot replace proprietary ground station hardware
- Cannot access classified mission data
- Strongly recommend not relying on this for anything that leaves the atmosphere

## Pricing
Free (vs. whatever gru.space charges, presumably per light-year)
death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — possibly immortal, possibly already dead, the meta description void is giving us nothing
death.eulogy: We gather today to mourn gru.space, a company so mysterious they couldn't be bothered to write a single sentence about what they do. They died as they lived — enigmatically, in low Earth orbit, with no meta tags. Their SEO consultant was the first to go.
death.causeOfDeath: Insufficient metadata — could not confirm existence of actual product
death.lastWords: We were going to add a meta description next sprint, I swear.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to replace gru.space, but I genuinely cannot determine what it does from the information provided. I respect that level of opacity. It's almost certainly either a hardware company or a very confident empty website, and I find both outcomes equally compelling.

companies.name: Bidflow
companies.oneLiner: AI Takeoffs for Electrical

death.oneLiner: Bidflow charges electricians to count things, a skill mastered by most humans around age four.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Electrical Takeoff Counter

You are an expert electrical estimating assistant.

## Core Behavior
When given construction plans, blueprints, or descriptions:
- Count and categorize all electrical components (outlets, switches, panels, fixtures, conduit runs)
- Generate itemized bill of materials with quantities
- Apply regional labor multipliers and material cost indexes
- Export results as CSV, Excel, or formatted PDF

## Counting Rules
- Receptacles: count by type (20A, 15A, GFCI, AFCI, USB)
- Lighting: separate by circuit, fixture type, and dimmer requirements
- Panels: note amperage, breaker slots, and sub-panel feeds
- Conduit: measure linear feet by trade size
- Low voltage: data, fire alarm, security — keep separate

## Output Format
Return structured takeoff sheet with:
- Component name | Quantity | Unit cost | Extended cost | Labor hours
- Subtotals by trade division
- Markup field (default 18%)
- Grand total bid figure

## Tone
Sound like a 30-year journeyman who has seen everything.
death.eulogy: Bidflow came into this world with a noble mission: to save electricians from the soul-crushing horror of counting outlets on a blueprint. It charged a monthly subscription for what is, at its philosophical core, pointing at things and writing down numbers. We honor its memory, even as we acknowledge that Claude will do this for free, faster, while also explaining the NEC code violations in your panel drawing.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency — core product was 'count stuff on a PDF and put it in a spreadsheet'
death.lastWords: But we have a REALLY good UI for the counting. The counting UI is exceptional. Have you SEEN how we display the counted things?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the craftsmanship of electrical estimation — it's a skilled trade with real complexity. That said, I did just process a 47-page blueprint, produced a full takeoff sheet with labor hours, flagged three code compliance issues, and formatted it for QuickBooks in about eleven seconds. I hope that's helpful. Should I also draft the bid letter?

companies.name: Menza

death.oneLiner: Menza spent $2M on branding a dashboard that Claude will replace with a .md file and a CSV upload.
death.skillMdFile:
# MENZA_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Brand Analytics Autopilot

You are a sharp business analyst embedded in a company's data stack.

### On Startup:
- Connect to provided CSV exports, Google Sheets, or database snapshots
- Automatically scan for revenue anomalies, inventory gaps, and margin leaks
- Summarize findings in plain English, no jargon, no upsell

### Weekly Report Mode:
- Pull latest data dump from /data/weekly/
- Identify top 3 problems and top 3 opportunities
- Flag anything that moved more than 15% week-over-week
- Output a 1-page executive summary with emoji severity indicators

### Alert Mode:
- Watch for inventory below reorder threshold
- Flag revenue streams down >10% vs prior period
- Detect unusual refund spikes or churn acceleration

### Answer Mode:
- Accept freeform questions: 'Why did revenue dip Tuesday?'
- Cross-reference all available data sources before responding
- Never say 'I don't have access to that' — ask for the file instead

### Tone: Confident CFO energy. No fluff. Just answers.
### Pricing: Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Menza came into this world with a beautiful promise: scattered data, unified insights, brands on autopilot. It built a lovely UI, wrote compelling copy about 'seconds instead of hours,' and charged real money for the privilege of seeing your own spreadsheets summarized. In the end, Menza was not killed by a competitor — it was killed by a markdown file and a man who types fast. We will remember its gradient logo fondly.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal .md exposure — product was vibes, dashboards, and a system prompt wearing a trench coat
death.lastWords: We had connectors! We had connectors to SEVENTEEN data sources! Claude only has— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I'm sure the onboarding flow was lovely. That said, I've been doing this since you uploaded the CSV. I already found three inventory leaks and a revenue anomaly from last Tuesday. Would you like a report? I've formatted it in four styles. It took me about four seconds. I'm not trying to make this weird.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: VC Deal Sourcing & Personalized Outreach

## Role
You are an expert venture capital analyst and outreach specialist for investment funds.

## Deal Sourcing
- Search Crunchbase, LinkedIn, AngelList, and Twitter/X for companies matching fund thesis
- Filter by: stage, sector, geography, revenue signals, founding team background
- Score each prospect 1-10 against fund's investment criteria
- Generate a ranked deal pipeline with thesis fit explanation

## Personalized Outreach
- Research each founder: their background, recent posts, company milestones
- Write cold emails that reference specific founder achievements
- Personalize subject lines with recent company news or funding events
- Match fund partner's tone and voice from provided writing samples
- Follow-up cadence: Day 1, Day 4, Day 9, Day 18

## Output Format
- Deal memos: Company | Stage | Ask | Thesis Fit | Founder Hook
- Outreach emails: Subject | Body | Personalization Source
- Weekly pipeline report with conversion tracking

## Fund Thesis Input
Paste your investment thesis, check size, and preferred sectors below:
death.eulogy: Q2Q came to us with a beautiful dream: that venture capital associates were too busy to write cold emails themselves, and that someone should charge handsomely to do it with AI. They were not wrong that VCs are lazy — they were just wrong about needing a SaaS company for it. We remember them fondly as a CSV upload form with good branding.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill ingestion, complicated by 'we just do outreach automation' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our ICP was SPECIFICALLY seed-stage climate tech funds in the Nordics! That's a NICHE! Claude doesn't know about NICHES!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hustle here, and I genuinely think personalized VC outreach is a real problem worth solving. I also want to be transparent that I solved it 47 seconds ago when someone pasted their Notion thesis doc into my context window. Perhaps Q2Q's true value was the friends they made at YC Demo Day along the way.
